In **High-Performance Computing (HPC)** and **Telecom Network Function Virtualization (NFV)**, CPU pinning and dedicated core allocation are used to meet **strict real-time latency targets** (sub-millisecond), as seen in 5G Core deployments.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Popek–Goldberg Sufficiency Proof (Sketch)

We formally prove that if $\mathcal{S} \subseteq \mathcal{P}$, then the architecture is virtualizable.

> **Given:** All sensitive instructions $\mathcal{S}$ are a subset of privileged instructions $\mathcal{P}$.
> **To Prove:** A VMM can be constructed that presents each guest with a faithful copy of the physical machine.

### Step 1: Define the Control Program (VMM)

The VMM is invoked in one of two cases:
- **Case A:** The guest attempts to execute a *privileged* instruction in user mode $\Rightarrow$ hardware traps to the VMM (Ring 0).
- **Case B:** The guest attempts a *non-privileged* instruction $\Rightarrow$ executes natively (no trap).

### Step 2: Apply the Subset Condition

Since $\mathcal{S} \subseteq \mathcal{P}$, every sensitive instruction **is** privileged. Therefore, any attempt to execute a sensitive instruction in user mode automatically triggers a hardware trap to the VMM (Case A applies). The VMM inspects the trapped instruction, emulates its effect on the *virtual* machine state, and resumes the guest.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Guest executes } I \text{ in user mode} & \Rightarrow \text{Hardware checks: is } I \in \mathcal{P}? \\
\text{If yes (Case A):} & \Rightarrow \text{trap to VMM} \Rightarrow \text{VMM emulates } I \text{ on virtual state} \\
\text{If no (Case B):} & \Rightarrow \text{native execution, VMM not involved}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Verify the Three Popek–Goldberg Properties

1. **Equivalence:** The VMM maintains a *virtual* copy of all privileged state (registers, control registers, memory maps). Every trapped instruction updates the virtual state $\Rightarrow$ guest sees a machine identical to the real one.
2. **Resource Control:** The VMM arbitrates access to physical resources $\Rightarrow$ no guest can modify resources it does not own.
3. **Efficiency:** Statistically, a large majority of instructions are innocuous $\Rightarrow$ they execute natively, giving near-native throughput.

### Step 4: Converse Necessity

If $\exists \; I \in \mathcal{S}$ such that $I \notin \mathcal{P}$ (a *critical* instruction), then $I$ executes in user mode without trapping, and the VMM has no opportunity to emulate it. The guest could directly manipulate hardware $\Rightarrow$ the VMM **cannot guarantee resource control**. This violates Property 2. Hence, the condition is also **necessary**.

$$
\boxed{\; \text{Architecture is virtualizable} \iff \mathcal{S} \subseteq \mathcal{P} \;}
$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** This proof explains *why x86 failed classical virtualization* in 1999 (the year VMware was founded) and *why it succeeded in 2006* with Intel VT-x — the new instruction set ensured $\mathcal{S} \subseteq \mathcal{P}$ by adding a new trap mechanism.

## 3.2 Derivation: CPU Scheduling Utilization Bound

Consider $N$ virtual machines (vCPUs) sharing a single pCPU, where each VM is busy with probability $\rho$ and idle with probability $1 - \rho$ (Bernoulli process).

### Step 1: Probability All VMs are Idle

Assuming independence across VMs:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{all idle}) &= \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(\text{VM}_i \text{ idle}) = (1 - \rho)^N
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Probability the pCPU is Utilized

The pCPU is utilized whenever **at least one** VM is active:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{pCPU busy}) &= 1 - P(\text{all idle}) = 1 - (1 - \rho)^N
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Practical Application — Cloud SLA Planning

Suppose a cloud provider provisions a 4-vCPU VM to a tenant. The provider needs to **estimate** the worst-case performance under an $8:1$ overcommitment ratio with $\rho = 0.6$ per VM:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{pCPU busy at 8:1}) &= 1 - (1 - 0.6)^8 \\
&= 1 - (0.4)^8 \\
&= 1 - 0.000655 \\
&\approx 0.99934
\end{aligned}
$$

Interpretation: With 8 busy VMs, the pCPU is saturated **99.93% of the time**, leaving only **0.07% slack** — performance is severely degraded. The provider must therefore either lower the overcommit ratio or use weighted fair scheduling (Linux CFS) with priority boosting.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** When a numerical question involves $(1 - \rho)^N$, always compute it in two stages — first find $(1 - \rho)$, then raise to power $N$ — to minimize arithmetic errors.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of CPU Virtualization Techniques

| Parameter | Full Virt. (BT) | Paravirtualization | HW-Assisted (VT-x / AMD-V) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Guest OS Modification** | None | Required | None |
| **Performance Overhead** | 5–20% | 2–5% | 1–3% |
| **Mechanism** | Binary Translation | Hypercalls | Ring-1 + VMCS |
| **Popek–Goldberg $\mathcal{S} \subseteq \mathcal{P}$** | Achieved via SW | Achieved via SW | Achieved via HW |
| **Examples** | VMware ESXi (legacy) | Xen (classic), KVM-virtio | KVM, Hyper-V, VMware, AWS Nitro |
| **Live Migration** | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Memory Overhead** | Medium | Low | Lowest |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Architecture Diagram: CPU Virtualization Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    app1["Guest App A"]
    app2["Guest App B"]
    app3["Guest App C"]
    gos1["Guest OS Linux"]
    gos2["Guest OS Windows"]
    vcpu1["vCPU 0"]
    vcpu2["vCPU 1"]
    vmm["Hypervisor VMM Ring 0"]
    sched["CPU Scheduler CFS"]
    pcpu0["pCPU Core 0"]
    pcpu1["pCPU Core 1"]
    pcpu2["pCPU Core 2"]
    pcpu3["pCPU Core 3"]

    app1 --> gos1
    app2 --> gos1
    app3 --> gos2
    gos1 --> vcpu1
    gos2 --> vcpu2
    vcpu1 --> vmm
    vcpu2 --> vmm
    vmm --> sched
    sched --> pcpu0
    sched --> pcpu1
    sched --> pcpu2
    sched --> pcpu3
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** Three guest applications run in two VMs. Each guest OS interacts only with its own vCPU. The hypervisor (VMM) intercepts all vCPU activity, runs the scheduler (e.g., Linux Completely Fair Scheduler), and dispatches vCPUs to physical cores in a time-sliced, priority-weighted manner.

## 4.2 Flow Diagram: Trap-and-Emulate Mechanism

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant GOS as Guest OS Ring 1
    participant CPU as Physical CPU
    participant VMM as Hypervisor Ring 0
    participant VS as Virtual State Store

    GOS->>CPU: Execute MOV CR3 EAX
    CPU->>CPU: Check privilege: is this privileged?
    CPU->>VMM: VMEXIT trap with instruction info
    VMM->>VS: Read guest virtual CR3 value
    VMM->>VS: Emulate new page table on virtual state
    VMM->>CPU: VMRESUME guest with updated state
    CPU->>GOS: Continue guest execution
```

> **Reading the Sequence:** The guest OS attempts a sensitive privileged instruction (`MOV CR3`, which loads a new page table base). The CPU hardware detects it is privileged and traps to the hypervisor. The hypervisor emulates the effect on a *virtual* page table (not the real one) and resumes the guest. The guest is unaware that emulation occurred.

## 4.3 Decision Tree: Selecting a CPU Virtualization Technique

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Need CPU Virtualization] --> B{Guest OS Source Code Available?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Use Paravirtualization Xen Style]
    B -- No --> D{Hardware Supports VT-x or AMD-V?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Use Hardware Assisted KVM Hyper-V]
    D -- No --> F[Use Full Virtualization with Binary Translation VMware ESXi Legacy]
    C --> G[Best Performance Requires Mods]
    E --> H[Best Performance No Mods Modern Default]
    F --> I[Legacy Use Only]
```

> **Reading the Decision Tree:** Engineers select the virtualization technique based on **(1)** whether the guest OS source is modifiable, and **(2)** whether the underlying hardware supports modern CPU virtualization extensions. For all contemporary cloud deployments, **Hardware-Assisted Virtualization (KVM/ESXi/Hyper-V on VT-x/AMD-V)** is the de-facto choice.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define CPU virtualization. List the three techniques used to virtualize the CPU in modern cloud systems.**

**Model Answer:**

CPU virtualization is the abstraction of a single physical processor (pCPU) into multiple virtual processors (vCPUs), allowing several guest operating systems to share the same hardware concurrently through a Virtual Machine Monitor (VMM/Hypervisor).

The three techniques are:
1. **Full Virtualization with Binary Translation** — sensitive instructions are dynamically rewritten at runtime.
2. **Paravirtualization** — the guest OS is modified to use hypercalls instead of sensitive instructions.
3. **Hardware-Assisted Virtualization** — uses Intel VT-x or AMD-V extensions with VMCS to trap sensitive instructions directly in silicon.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: State the Popek–Goldberg virtualization criteria. Why is the original x86 architecture not classically virtualizable?**

**Model Answer:**

The Popek–Goldberg criterion states that a processor architecture is virtualizable **if and only if the set of sensitive instructions is a subset of the set of privileged instructions** ($\mathcal{S} \subseteq \mathcal{P}$).

The original x86 architecture is **not** classically virtualizable because it contains approximately **17 sensitive but non-privileged instructions** (e.g., `POPF`, `SGDT`, `SIDT`, `SMSW`, `PUSHF`) that modify processor state without trapping to Ring 0. Hence, $\mathcal{S} \not\subseteq \mathcal{P}$, and a pure trap-and-emulate VMM cannot guarantee resource control. This is why x86 required binary translation (VMware), paravirtualization (Xen), or hardware extensions (VT-x/AMD-V) to enable efficient CPU virtualization.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> KTU ESE Rule: *Answer any **one full question** from each module. Each Part B question carries 14 marks and typically has two 7-mark sub-parts (a) and (b).*

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model Paper]`

**Q (a)** Explain the three techniques for CPU virtualization in detail with diagrams. Compare their performance overhead and guest OS modification requirements. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The three techniques are explained below:

**(i) Full Virtualization with Binary Translation**
- The hypervisor scans guest instruction streams dynamically using a *dynamic binary translator*.
- When a sensitive non-privileged instruction is detected, the translator rewrites it on-the-fly into a safe sequence of privileged instructions.
- The guest OS is **completely unaware** of the virtualization; it is *unmodified*.
- **Performance overhead:** 5–20% due to translation cache misses and instruction decoding.
- **Example:** VMware ESXi (early versions).

**(ii) Paravirtualization**
- The guest OS source code is **modified** so that sensitive instructions are replaced with **hypercalls** (explicit synchronous calls to the hypervisor).
- The hypervisor is invoked deliberately, not via traps.
- **Performance overhead:** 2–5% (very close to native) because no dynamic translation is needed.
- **Drawback:** Requires access to guest OS source; impossible for closed-source OSes like Windows in early deployments.
- **Example:** Classic Xen, KVM with `virtio` para-virtualized drivers.

**(iii) Hardware-Assisted Virtualization (Intel VT-x / AMD-V)**
- CPU silicon adds a new execution mode: **VMX Root Mode** (for the host/hypervisor) and **VMX Non-Root Mode** (for guests).
- A new data structure called the **Virtual Machine Control Structure (VMCS)** stores guest state.
- A new instruction set (`VMLAUNCH`, `VMRESUME`, `VMEXIT`) controls VM entry/exit.
- All sensitive instructions **trap automatically in hardware** to the hypervisor, solving the x86 problem elegantly.
- **Performance overhead:** 1–3% (near-native).
- **Example:** KVM, Microsoft Hyper-V, modern VMware ESXi, AWS Nitro System.

**Comparison Table:**

| Technique | Guest Mod? | Overhead | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|
| Full Virt. (BT) | No | 5–20% | Binary Translation |
| Paravirtualization | Yes | 2–5% | Hypercalls |
| Hardware-Assisted | No | 1–3% | VMCS + VMX |

---

**Q (b)** Discuss the Popek–Goldberg virtualization theorem in detail. Prove that if $\mathcal{S} \subseteq \mathcal{P}$, a VMM can be constructed. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definitions (Popek & Goldberg, 1974):**
- **Privileged Instruction:** Traps to the supervisor (Ring 0) if executed in user mode.
- **Sensitive Instruction:** An instruction whose behavior or result depends on the current privilege level or the current resource configuration (e.g., memory mapping, I/O ports).
- **Innocuous Instruction:** All other instructions; behavior is independent of privilege.

**The Theorem:** *A computer architecture is efficiently virtualizable if and only if the set of sensitive instructions is a subset of the set of privileged instructions:* $\mathcal{S} \subseteq \mathcal{P}$.

**Proof (Sufficiency):**
Assume $\mathcal{S} \subseteq \mathcal{P}$. Construct a VMM as follows:
1. Run the guest OS in **user mode** (de-privileged).
2. When the guest executes any instruction $I$:
   - If $I \in \mathcal{P}$ (privileged), the hardware automatically **traps** to the hypervisor. The VMM inspects $I$, emulates its effect on a *virtual* copy of the processor state, and resumes the guest.
   - If $I \notin \mathcal{P}$ (innocuous), $I$ executes natively at full speed.
3. Since $\mathcal{S} \subseteq \mathcal{P}$, every sensitive instruction is also privileged $\Rightarrow$ every sensitive instruction traps $\Rightarrow$ the VMM can always emulate it.
4. The VMM maintains shadow copies of all sensitive state (e.g., page tables, control registers), so the guest sees a complete, consistent virtual machine.
5. **Equivalence Property:** the guest cannot distinguish its virtual machine from a real one.
6. **Resource Control Property:** the VMM mediates all access to real hardware.
7. **Efficiency Property:** the majority of instructions are innocuous, so they execute natively, yielding near-native performance.

**Proof (Necessity):**
Suppose $\mathcal{S} \not\subseteq \mathcal{P}$. Then there exists a critical instruction $I \in \mathcal{S}$ with $I \notin \mathcal{P}$. Executed in user mode, $I$ completes without trapping. The VMM has no opportunity to inspect or modify the operation. The guest could therefore manipulate real hardware state directly, **violating the Resource Control property**. Hence, virtualizability fails.

**Conclusion:** $\mathcal{S} \subseteq \mathcal{P}$ is both necessary and sufficient for classical efficient virtualization.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Loss:**
> 1. Students often write the Popek–Goldberg condition as $\mathcal{P} \subseteq \mathcal{S}$ (reversed!). The correct direction is $\mathcal{S} \subseteq \mathcal{P}$ — **sensitive inside privileged**.
> 2. Many confuse **Trap-and-Emulate** (software technique) with **Hardware-Assisted Virtualization** (silicon-based). Mentioning **VMCS** or **VMX mode** is required to get full 7 marks.
> 3. In comparison questions, forgetting to mention **guest OS modification** is a frequent -2 mark loss.
> 4. Numerical questions on overcommitment: always state the **assumption of independence** between VMs to justify the $(1-\rho)^N$ formula.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`

**Q (a)** What is CPU overcommitment? A data center has 64 physical cores and provisions VMs requiring 384 vCPUs. Compute the overcommit ratio, effective per-vCPU share, and discuss the risks. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** CPU overcommitment is a hypervisor feature that allows allocating more virtual CPUs (vCPUs) to guest VMs than the number of physical CPU cores (pCPUs) available, based on the statistical fact that most VMs are not 100% busy simultaneously.

**Step 1: Compute the Overcommit Ratio**

$$
R_{oc} = \frac{N_{vCPU}}{N_{pCPU}} = \frac{384}{64} = 6
$$

The overcommit ratio is **6 : 1**.

**Step 2: Effective Per-vCPU Capacity**

$$
C_{vCPU} = \frac{C_{pCPU}}{R_{oc}} = \frac{1}{6} \approx 0.1667 \text{ of a physical core}
$$

Each vCPU receives approximately **16.67%** of a physical core, assuming fair scheduling.

**Step 3: Risk Analysis**

- **CPU Saturation Risk:** Using the utilization formula $U = 1 - (1 - \rho)^{384}$, even a modest per-VM busy probability of $\rho = 0.05$ yields $U \approx 1 - (0.95)^{384} \approx 1 - 0.0000005 \approx 99.9999\%$. The system is essentially saturated.
- **Noisy Neighbor Problem:** A single high-CPU VM can starve all others due to weighted-fair-scheduling preemption.
- **Live Migration Trigger:** High utilization (typically > 85%) triggers vMotion/Live Migration, which has its own performance cost.
- **Mitigation:** Use CPU affinity, dedicated cores, or real-time scheduling classes for critical workloads.

---

**Q (b)** Describe the Trap-and-Emulate technique with a neat diagram and explain why it fails on classical x86. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Trap-and-Emulate Workflow:**
1. The guest OS is run at a **de-privileged** level (Ring 1 or Ring 3).
2. The hypervisor runs in **Ring 0** (full privilege).
3. The guest executes instructions one by one:
   - **Privileged instructions** attempted in user mode trigger a **hardware trap** to the hypervisor.
   - The hypervisor **emulates** the effect of the trapped instruction on a *virtual* copy of the processor state.
   - The hypervisor **resumes** the guest at the next instruction.
   - **Innocuous instructions** execute directly in hardware at native speed.

**Diagram (textual representation for exam):**

```
+-----------+      trap      +-------------+
| Guest OS  | -------------> | Hypervisor  |
| (Ring 1)  | <------------- | (Ring 0)    |
+-----------+   emulate/resume +-------------+
      |                              |
      | native exec                  | shadow state
      v                              v
+-----------+                  +-------------+
| Hardware  |                  | Virtual CPU |
| (traps)   |                  | State Store |
+-----------+                  +-------------+
```

**Why Trap-and-Emulate Fails on Classical x86:**

The x86 architecture contains **sensitive but non-privileged instructions** such as:
- `POPF` / `PUSHF` — modifies flag registers (interrupt-enable flag) without trapping in user mode.
- `SGDT` / `SIDT` / `SLDT` — store descriptor table registers.
- `SMSW` — store machine status word.
- `LMSW`, `VERR`, `VERW`, `LAR`, `LSL` — manipulate control state without raising a trap.

Because these instructions modify critical processor state **silently** in user mode, a guest OS can directly alter resources the hypervisor is supposed to control. Hence, $\mathcal{S} \not\subseteq \mathcal{P}$ on classical x86, and a *pure* trap-and-emulate VMM cannot provide isolation. This is precisely the problem that VMware solved with binary translation (1999) and that Intel/AMD solved with VT-x (2005–2006).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **CPU Virtualization** = multiplexing one or more pCPUs into many vCPUs via a hypervisor.
- The **hypervisor / VMM** is the master control program; it occupies **Ring 0** in the host.
- Guest OS is **de-privileged** to Ring 1 (or Ring 3 in paravirtualization) — this is the *Virtualization Gap*.
- **Three techniques:** Full Virt. (Binary Translation) | Paravirtualization | Hardware-Assisted (VT-x/AMD-V).
- **Popek–Goldberg Criterion:** $\mathcal{S} \subseteq \mathcal{P}$ — memorize the *subset direction*.
- x86 historically fails Popek–Goldberg due to ~17 *sensitive but non-privileged* instructions.
- **Hardware-assisted virtualization** is the *modern default* (KVM, Hyper-V, VMware, AWS Nitro).
- **Overcommitment ratio** $R_{oc} = N_{vCPU} / N_{pCPU}$; typical cloud range is 4:1 to 16:1.
- **CPU utilization** with $N$ independent VMs: $U = 1 - (1 - \rho)^N$ — small $\rho$ saturates the pCPU at large $N$.
- **vCPU pinning** improves real-time performance; **weighted fair scheduling** (Linux CFS, VMware DRS) is used in clouds.
- The term **"trap-and-emulate"** refers to the *software* mechanism; **"VT-x/AMD-V"** refers to the *hardware* mechanism.
- For KTU exams, **always draw the Ring-level architecture diagram** when discussing CPU virtualization — it earns +1 to +2 marks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Examiner's Note:** When writing long answers, structure them with **headings, diagrams, and a comparison table**. KTU evaluators scan for these visual cues, and a well-labeled Mermaid-style or ASCII diagram can secure **2–3 easy marks** even if the prose is weak.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Virtualization of Memory and I/O Devices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT634 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Virtualization of Memory and I/O Devices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Virtualization of Memory

### Formal KTU Definition
**Memory Virtualization** in a cloud computing context is the process of presenting each Virtual Machine (VM) with a contiguous, private, and isolated **virtual address space**, while the underlying Virtual Machine Monitor (VMM) / hypervisor dynamically maps these **Guest Virtual Addresses (GVA) → Guest Physical Addresses (GPA) → Host Physical Addresses (HPA)**. The hypervisor conceals the true physical memory layout of the host and enables features such as **memory overcommitment**, **ballooning**, **swapping**, and **transparent page sharing**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — PECDT634 Module 2)**
> The three-stage address translation `GVA → GPA → HPA` is the **heart of memory virtualization** and is examinable in both Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks).

### Intuitive Analogy — The Shared Apartment Pantry
Imagine you live in a co-living apartment where every resident is told they have a personal, full-sized kitchen pantry. In reality, all the shelves are stacked in a single small room in the basement. Every time you "open your pantry," the building manager runs downstairs, fetches *your* specific jars, and brings them up to you. Other residents never know your items exist, and they cannot accidentally use them. The manager may even place similar items (identical salt shakers) on the same shelf to save space.

**Mapping to memory virtualization:**
- The **resident (VM)** perceives a private, large pantry → **Guest Virtual Memory**.
- The **labels on the jars** → **GPA (Guest Physical Address)**.
- The **actual shelf location in the basement** → **HPA (Host Physical Address)**.
- The **building manager** → **VMM / Hypervisor**.

---

## 1.2 Virtualization of I/O Devices

### Formal KTU Definition
**I/O Virtualization** is the abstraction of physical I/O resources (network cards, disk controllers, USB devices, GPUs) such that multiple VMs can securely and efficiently share them. It is implemented through techniques ranging from **full software emulation** to **hardware-assisted approaches** such as **SR-IOV (Single Root I/O Virtualization)** and **IOMMU (Input/Output Memory Management Unit)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three I/O operations must always be virtualized:**
> 1. **Data movement** (DMA — Direct Memory Access)
> 2. **Interrupt delivery** (MSI/MSI-X redirection)
> 3. **Configuration & control** (MMIO / PIO traps)

### Intuitive Analogy — The Multi-Function Office Printer
Picture one physical printer shared by 10 employees. Three possible models:
- **(a) Emulation:** Each employee uses a separate *virtual* printer driver; one assistant collects all print jobs and feeds them to the real printer sequentially. Slow, but full isolation.
- **(b) Para-virtualization:** Employees send print jobs in a special "split" format to the assistant, who knows their preferences (double-sided, color). Faster, but requires cooperation.
- **(c) Direct Assignment / SR-IOV:** The printer is electronically partitioned into 10 independent virtual printers, each with its own queue and paper tray. The real paper is still shared, but control is hardware-level — near-native speed.

**Mapping to I/O virtualization:**
- **Emulation** → QEMU / full software stack.
- **Para-virtualization** → virtio drivers in KVM / Xen.
- **Direct Assignment** → VFIO + IOMMU (PCI Passthrough).
- **Hardware Partitioning** → SR-IOV with PFs and VFs.

---

## 1.3 Why Are These Topics Critical in Cloud Computing?

| Property | Without Virtualization | With Virtualization |
|----------|------------------------|---------------------|
| Server utilization | **5–15 %** | **60–80 %** |
| Memory isolation | None (one crash = total) | Strong (per-VM isolation) |
| Live migration | Impossible | Transparent |
| Hardware independence | Coupled to physical RAM/CPU | Decoupled via abstraction |
| I/O fairness | OS-controlled | VMM-mediated with QoS |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Cloud platforms like **AWS EC2, Azure VMs, and Google Compute Engine** rely entirely on memory + I/O virtualization to deliver **multi-tenancy, elasticity, and pay-per-use billing**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Tier Address Translation Pipeline

Modern x86-64 virtualization (Intel VT-x / AMD-V) performs a **two-level page table walk**:

**Level 1 — Inside the Guest OS:**  
The guest OS maintains its own page tables that translate `GVA → GPA` exactly as it would on bare metal. The guest believes it owns physical memory starting at address **0**.

**Level 2 — Inside the VMM (EPT / NPT):**  
The hypervisor maintains a second set of page tables called **Extended Page Tables (EPT)** on Intel or **Nested Page Tables (NPT)** on AMD. These translate `GPA → HPA`.

### 2.1.1 Translation Steps (Bulleted Logic)

1. **MMU issues a TLB lookup** for the GVA.  
   - **TLB hit** → MMU gets the GPA directly (fast path).  
   - **TLB miss** → page walk begins.

2. **Guest page walk** walks the guest page tables (controlled by `CR3` in guest) to resolve `GVA → GPA`.  
   - This walk itself triggers additional memory accesses that **must be translated by EPT**.

3. **EPT walk** (hardware-assisted) walks the second-level page tables to resolve `GPA → HPA`.  
   - Typically requires **4 memory accesses** for a 4-level EPT.

4. **Final physical access** retrieves the data from HPA.

5. **TLB fill** caches the combined `GVA → HPA` translation in a special **combined TLB** (or two-level tagged TLB).

### 2.1.2 The "Why" Behind the Cost
- A single memory access in a virtualized environment may cost **2× to 4× the bare-metal latency** because every guest page walk step requires an EPT walk.
- Large TLB reach (2 MiB huge pages) becomes **critical** for cloud workloads (e.g., database servers).

---

## 2.2 Memory Virtualization Techniques

### 2.2.1 Shadow Paging (Software Approach)
- The VMM maintains a **shadow page table** that directly maps `GVA → HPA`.
- On every guest page-table modification (e.g., `CR3` change, `INVLPGD`), the VMM is **trapped** and updates the shadow page table.
- **Pros:** No hardware dependency.  
- **Cons:** High overhead (VM exits on every page table update), poor scalability.

### 2.2.2 Extended Page Tables (EPT) / Nested Page Tables (NPT) (Hardware-Assisted)
- **Hardware** walks the second-level tables automatically using **EPT base pointer** in `EPTP` (Intel) or `NPT` (AMD).
- The VMM only sets up the EPT/NPT once per VM; the CPU handles all translations.
- **Pros:** Near-native performance, fewer VM exits.  
- **Cons:** Requires Intel VT-x or AMD-V support; every guest page walk is more expensive due to nested walks.

### 2.2.3 Memory Overcommitment Mechanisms

| Mechanism | Working Principle | Use Case |
|-----------|-------------------|----------|
| **Ballooning** | Guest installs a "balloon" driver; VMM inflates it to force guest to free pages. | Reclaim memory from idle VMs. |
| **Transparent Page Sharing (TPS)** | VMM hashes pages; identical pages stored once (copy-on-write). | Deduplicate OS/kernels across many VMs. |
| **Memory Swapping** | VMM swaps out guest pages to disk when host RAM is exhausted. | Overcommit ratios > 1.5. |
| **Dynamic Memory Addition (hot-plug)** | VM is given more RAM at runtime. | Bursty workloads. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Balloon Driver Mechanism (Stepwise):**
> 1. Host is under memory pressure.
> 2. VMM asks the balloon driver (inside the guest) to **inflate** to *N* pages.
> 3. Guest OS must allocate *N* pages to the balloon → those pages can no longer be used by guest apps.
> 4. VMM reclaims those *N* host physical pages for other VMs.
> 5. When pressure subsides, VMM **deflates** the balloon → pages return to the guest.

---

## 2.3 I/O Virtualization Techniques

### 2.3.1 The Three Architectural Models

1. **Full Emulation (Software-only)**
   - VMM emulates every I/O device register, interrupt, and DMA.
   - Highest flexibility; no real hardware needed (e.g., QEMU emulated NIC).
   - Lowest performance: every I/O causes a **VM exit → trap → emulate → VM resume** cycle.

2. **Para-virtualization (virtio)**
   - Guest uses **virtio front-end drivers** that communicate with **virtio back-end** in VMM via shared memory + ring buffers.
   - Bypasses expensive emulation by using a **hypercall-based** clean interface.
   - Used in **KVM, Xen, VMware paravirt SCSI**.

3. **Direct Assignment (PCI Passthrough)**
   - An entire physical device is **bound to one VM** using **IOMMU** (Intel VT-d / AMD-Vi).
   - The VM accesses the device with **native performance** via DMA, bypassing the VMM.
   - Limitation: the device can be used by only **one VM at a time**.

4. **SR-IOV (Single Root I/O Virtualization)**
   - A **Physical Function (PF)** is partitioned into multiple **Virtual Functions (VFs)**.
   - Each VF is an **independent PCI device** that can be assigned to a different VM.
   - Each VF has its own **config space, MSI-X vectors, and DMA streams**.
   - **IOMMU** ensures DMA isolation between VFs.
   - **Near-native performance with multi-tenancy** — the holy grail of cloud I/O.

### 2.3.2 Why SR-IOV Is Central to Cloud Networking
- Public clouds (e.g., AWS enhanced networking, Azure Accelerated Networking) require **line-rate 10/25/100 Gbps** with single-digit microsecond latency.
- Software emulation cannot achieve this.  
- SR-IOV offloads the **data plane** to hardware, leaving only **control plane** to software.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|----------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Effective memory access time (basic) | $T_{\text{eff}} = h \cdot T_{\text{hit}} + (1-h) \cdot T_{\text{miss}}$ | $h$ = TLB hit ratio |
| 2 | EPT/NPT walk cost (typical 4-level) | $T_{\text{walk}} = 4 \cdot T_{\text{mem}}$ | $T_{\text{mem}}$ = main memory access |
| 3 | Virtualized memory access time | $T_{\text{virt}} = T_{\text{TLB}} + T_{\text{guest\_walk}} + 4 \cdot T_{\text{EPT}} + T_{\text{mem}}$ | Approx. $2\times$ to $4\times$ bare metal |
| 4 | TLB reach with 4 KiB pages | $R = N_{\text{entries}} \cdot P_{\text{size}}$ | E.g., 64 entries × 4 KiB = 256 KiB |
| 5 | TLB reach with 2 MiB huge pages | $R = N_{\text{entries}} \cdot 2\,\text{MiB}$ | E.g., 32 entries × 2 MiB = 64 MiB |
| 6 | Balloon reclaim capacity | $M_{\text{reclaimed}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N_{\text{VM}}} B_i$ | $B_i$ = pages ballooned in VM $i$ |
| 7 | Overcommit ratio | $R_{\text{oc}} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{N_{\text{VM}}} M_{\text{config},i}}{M_{\text{physical}}}$ | $R_{\text{oc}} > 1$ → overcommitment |
| 8 | Memory savings via TPS | $S_{\text{TPS}} = \left(1 - \dfrac{N_{\text{unique}}}{N_{\text{total}}}\right) \cdot 100\%$ | Hash-collision aware |
| 9 | SR-IOV VF limit per PF | $N_{\text{VF}} \leq 256$ (typical max in PCIe spec) | Practical: 8–64 |
| 10 | SR-IOV aggregate bandwidth | $B_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N_{\text{VF}}} B_i \leq B_{\text{PF,link}}$ | Bounded by physical link |
| 11 | DMA remapping overhead (IOMMU) | $T_{\text{IOMMU}} = T_{\text{walk}} + T_{\text{translation}}$ | ≈ 100–500 ns |
| 12 | Interrupt virtualization cost | $C_{\text{int}} = C_{\text{VMexit}} + C_{\text{handler}} + C_{\text{inject}}$ | Each MSI-X ≈ 1–5 µs |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Avoid using $\vert x \vert$** (pipe symbol) inside table cells — instead use `$\mid x\mid$` in LaTeX. KTU examiners often deduct marks for rendering errors in answer sheets.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cloud Providers (AWS, Azure, GCP):** Use EPT/NPT + SR-IOV to deliver 100 Gbps networking and NVMe-class storage to tenants.
- **Telecom / NFV:** Use SR-IOV for **vRAN** workloads where deterministic latency (< 1 ms) is mandatory.
- **HPC / AI Clusters:** Use **huge pages** (1 GiB) to maximize TLB reach and reduce nested-walk overhead.
- **Enterprise Datacenters:** Use **memory overcommitment** (1.5×–3× ratio) to maximize VM density.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Effective TLB Access Time (Foundation)

We start from the classical two-state TLB model.

**Given:**
- $T_{\text{hit}}$ = TLB hit latency (typically 1–2 cycles).
- $T_{\text{miss}}$ = TLB miss penalty (page walk + memory access).
- $h$ = TLB hit ratio (probability that a translation is cached).

**Step 1 — Express the expected access time as a probability-weighted sum.**

$$
T_{\text{eff}} = h \cdot T_{\text{hit}} + (1-h) \cdot T_{\text{miss}}
$$

**Step 2 — Distinguish the miss penalty components.**

The TLB miss penalty itself comprises:
- A page table walk: typically **4 memory accesses** for a 4-level page table.
- Plus the actual data fetch: **1 additional memory access**.

So,
$$
T_{\text{miss}} = 4 \cdot T_{\text{mem}} + T_{\text{mem}} = 5 \cdot T_{\text{mem}}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back.**

$$
T_{\text{eff}} = h \cdot T_{\text{hit}} + (1-h) \cdot 5 \cdot T_{\text{mem}}
$$

**Step 4 — Example numerical evaluation.**

Let $T_{\text{hit}} = 1\,\text{ns}$, $T_{\text{mem}} = 100\,\text{ns}$, $h = 0.95$:

$$
T_{\text{eff}} = 0.95 \cdot 1 + 0.05 \cdot 500 = 0.95 + 25 = 25.95\,\text{ns}
$$

**Conversion logic explained:**  
- $0.95$ of the time, the access costs only $1$ ns (TLB hit).  
- $0.05$ of the time, the access requires **5 DRAM round-trips**, costing $500$ ns.  
- The weighted average is dominated by the miss penalty because DRAM is so much slower than the TLB.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Virtualized Memory Access Time (EPT)

In a virtualized system, **every** memory access in the guest — including those made *during* the guest page walk — must be translated by the EPT.

**Given:**
- $T_{\text{TLB}}$ = TLB lookup time.
- $T_{\text{guest\_walk}}$ = time to walk the guest page table (each entry also needs EPT translation).
- $T_{\text{EPT}}$ = EPT walk cost per level (4 levels).
- $T_{\text{mem}}$ = physical memory access time.

**Step 1 — Total cost per virtualized access.**

$$
T_{\text{virt}} = T_{\text{TLB}} + T_{\text{guest\_walk}} + 4 \cdot T_{\text{EPT}} + T_{\text{mem}}
$$

**Step 2 — Inflate the guest walk cost.**

Each of the 4 guest page-table entries must itself be translated via EPT. If we conservatively assume each guest walk step costs $T_{\text{EPT}} + T_{\text{mem}}$:

$$
T_{\text{guest\_walk}} \approx 4 \cdot (T_{\text{EPT}} + T_{\text{mem}})
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and simplify.**

$$
T_{\text{virt}} = T_{\text{TLB}} + 4 \cdot (T_{\text{EPT}} + T_{\text{mem}}) + 4 \cdot T_{\text{EPT}} + T_{\text{mem}}
$$

$$
T_{\text{virt}} = T_{\text{TLB}} + 8 \cdot T_{\text{EPT}} + 5 \cdot T_{\text{mem}}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example.**

Let $T_{\text{TLB}} = 1\,\text{ns}$, $T_{\text{EPT}} = 50\,\text{ns}$, $T_{\text{mem}} = 100\,\text{ns}$:

$$
T_{\text{virt}} = 1 + 8 \cdot 50 + 5 \cdot 100 = 1 + 400 + 500 = 901\,\text{ns}
$$

Compared to a bare-metal access of $T_{\text{TLB}} + 4 \cdot T_{\text{mem}} + T_{\text{mem}} = 1 + 500 = 501\,\text{ns}$, virtualization adds:

$$
\text{Overhead} = \frac{901 - 501}{501} \cdot 100\% \approx 79.8\%
$$

**Conversion logic explained:**  
- The dominant cost is the **nested page walk** — guest walk + EPT walk, both needed for a single translation.  
- This is why cloud providers **heavily invest in huge pages and TLB prefetchers**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Memory Savings via Transparent Page Sharing (TPS)

**Given:**
- $N$ VMs, each running an identical OS image (e.g., Ubuntu 22.04).
- Each image occupies $P$ physical pages when loaded.
- The hashing scheme has a negligible false-sharing probability.

**Step 1 — Total physical pages without TPS.**

$$
N_{\text{total}} = N \cdot P
$$

**Step 2 — Pages stored on host with TPS (only one copy kept).**

$$
N_{\text{unique}} = P \quad (\text{all identical pages merged into one copy})
$$

**Step 3 — Memory savings.**

$$
S_{\text{TPS}} = N_{\text{total}} - N_{\text{unique}} = N \cdot P - P = (N - 1) \cdot P
$$

**Step 4 — Example: 100 identical VMs, $P = 50{,}000$ pages (≈ 200 MiB each).**

$$
S_{\text{TPS}} = 99 \cdot 50{,}000 = 4{,}950{,}000\ \text{pages}
$$

In MiB:
$$
S_{\text{TPS}} = 4{,}950{,}000 \cdot 4\,\text{KiB} \approx 19{,}336\,\text{MiB} \approx 18.9\,\text{GiB}
$$

**Conversion logic explained:**  
- Pages are deduplicated **only after a hash match**; on first write, the page is **copied (COW)** to preserve isolation.  
- Read-only pages (code segments) achieve the highest savings.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — SR-IOV Bandwidth Capacity

**Given:**
- A 100 Gbps NIC with one PF and 8 VFs.
- Each VF requests $B_{\text{VF},i}$ Gbps of bandwidth.

**Step 1 — Aggregate bandwidth constraint.**

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{8} B_{\text{VF},i} \leq B_{\text{PF,link}} = 100\,\text{Gbps}
$$

**Step 2 — Fair-share allocation.**

$$
B_{\text{VF,fair}} = \frac{100}{8} = 12.5\,\text{Gbps}
$$

**Step 3 — Weighted allocation (e.g., VF1 is premium tenant).**

Let weights $w_i$ be the SLA tier multipliers:

$$
B_{\text{VF},i} = w_i \cdot \frac{B_{\text{PF,link}}}{\sum_{j=1}^{8} w_j}
$$

If $w_1 = 4$ and all others $= 1$, total weight $= 11$:

$$
B_{\text{VF},1} = 4 \cdot \frac{100}{11} \approx 36.4\,\text{Gbps}
$$

$$
B_{\text{VF},2 \dots 8} = 1 \cdot \frac{100}{11} \approx 9.1\,\text{Gbps}
$$

**Conversion logic explained:**  
- The PF is the **single owner** of the physical link; the VFs are virtual "slices" of the PF's resources.  
- The aggregate **cannot exceed** the physical link capacity — this is a hard hardware constraint.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Trade-offs

| Technique | Memory Overhead | I/O Performance | Isolation | Hardware Required | Cloud Use |
|-----------|------------------|------------------|-----------|--------------------|-----------|
| Shadow Paging | **High** (one shadow per guest CR3) | N/A | Strong | None | Legacy only |
| EPT / NPT | **Low** | N/A | Strong | Intel VT-x / AMD-V | **Universal** |
| Ballooning | **Low** (reclaims idle pages) | N/A | Strong | virtio balloon driver | Common |
| TPS | **Negative overhead** (saves RAM) | N/A | Strong | Content-hash engine | Common |
| Full Emulation | N/A | **Very Low** (10–100× slower) | Strong | None | Testing / dev |
| Para-virtualization (virtio) | N/A | **Moderate** (1.2–2× overhead) | Strong | virtio standard | **Universal** |
| PCI Passthrough | N/A | **Near-native** | Strong | IOMMU | High-perf VMs |
| SR-IOV | N/A | **Near-native** | Strong | IOMMU + SR-IOV NIC | **Cloud standard** |

---

## 3.6 KTU Numerical Problem — Sample Solution

**Problem:** A cloud host has **64 GiB** of physical RAM and runs **10 VMs**, each configured with **16 GiB**. Compute the overcommit ratio and the worst-case memory deficit if all VMs reach peak usage simultaneously.

**Step 1 — Compute configured memory.**

$$
M_{\text{config}} = 10 \cdot 16\,\text{GiB} = 160\,\text{GiB}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute overcommit ratio.**

$$
R_{\text{oc}} = \frac{160}{64} = 2.5
$$

**Step 3 — Compute worst-case deficit (ignoring ballooning, TPS, swap).**

$$
D = M_{\text{config}} - M_{\text{physical}} = 160 - 64 = 96\,\text{GiB}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**

A ratio of $2.5\times$ is common in dev/test clouds. Production clouds use $\leq 1.5\times$ to avoid swap storms. The $96\,\text{GiB}$ deficit would force the VMM to either:
- Swap to disk (severe performance penalty), or
- Trigger ballooning (graceful reclamation), or
- Migrate VMs (live migration) to less-loaded hosts.

**Conversion logic explained:**  
- The overcommit ratio $R_{\text{oc}}$ expresses how much virtual memory is "promised" per unit of physical memory.  
- The deficit $D$ is the absolute amount that must be sourced from disk or other hosts.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safeguard Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric, prefixed with letters, and contain no reserved keywords. Labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric text (no markdown formatting inside quotes).

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Three-Tier Address Translation (GVA → GPA → HPA)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    app[Guest Application] -->|"Virtual Address GVA"| mmu1[Guest MMU]
    mmu1 -->|"Page Walk"| gpt[Guest Page Table]
    gpt -->|"Resolves to GPA"| ept1[EPT Stage 1]
    ept1 -->|"Page Walk"| epttbl[EPT Second Level Table]
    epttbl -->|"Resolves to HPA"| ram[Host Physical RAM]
    ram -->|"Data Returned"| app
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Memory Virtualization Architecture (EPT)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph guestVM["Guest Virtual Machine"]
        appA[Application Layer]
        gos[Guest Operating System]
        gpt1[Guest Page Tables GVA to GPA]
        appA --> gos
        gos --> gpt1
    end

    subgraph vmmLayer["Hypervisor VMM Layer"]
        eptctrl[EPT Base Pointer EPTP]
        epttbl1[EPT Level 4 Root]
        epttbl2[EPT Level 3 PDPT]
        epttbl3[EPT Level 2 PD]
        epttbl4[EPT Level 1 PT]
        eptctrl --> epttbl1
        epttbl1 --> epttbl2
        epttbl2 --> epttbl3
        epttbl3 --> epttbl4
    end

    subgraph hwLayer["Host Hardware"]
        cpumem[CPU MMU with Combined TLB]
        dram[Physical DRAM Modules]
        cpumem --> dram
    end

    gpt1 -->|"GPA Request"| cpumem
    cpumem -->|"Combined Walk"| eptctrl
    epttbl4 -->|"Final HPA"| dram
    dram -->|"Data"| cpumem
    cpumem -->|"To Guest"| appA
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — I/O Virtualization Stack (Three Models Compared)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph emul["Full Emulation Path"]
        gdev1[Guest Generic Device Driver]
        vmemu[VMM QEMU Emulator]
        hw1[Real Hardware]
        gdev1 -->|"Every Access Trapped"| vmemu
        vmemu -->|"Translated I O"| hw1
    end

    subgraph para["Para Virtualization Path"]
        gvfront[Guest Virtio Front End Driver]
        vback[Virtio Back End in VMM]
        hw2[Real Hardware]
        gvfront -->|"Hypercall via Shared Ring Buffer"| vback
        vback -->|"Native Driver"| hw2
    end

    subgraph sriov["SR IOV Direct Path"]
        gvfdrv[Guest VF Driver]
        vfdev[Virtual Function VF on NIC]
        pfctrl[Physical Function PF Driver in VMM]
        gvfdrv -->|"Direct DMA via IOMMU"| vfdev
        vfdev -->|"Aggregated by"| pfctrl
        pfctrl --> hw3[Physical NIC]
    end
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Balloon Driver Inflation/Deflation Lifecycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> PressureDetected: Host RAM Pressure
    PressureDetected --> Inflate: VMM Requests N Pages
    Inflate --> GuestFrees: Guest OS Allocates N Pages to Balloon
    GuestFrees --> ReclaimHost: VMM Reclaims N Host Pages
    ReclaimHost --> Idle: Pressure Relieved
    ReclaimHost --> Deflate: VMM Returns Pages
    Deflate --> GuestFrees: Guest Releases Pages to Free Pool
    GuestFrees --> Idle
```

## 4.5 Diagram 5 — SR-IOV Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph pfBlock["PF Physical Function in Host"]
        pfdrv[PF Driver in VMM]
        pfregs[PF Config and MMIO Space]
    end

    subgraph nicHW["Physical NIC Hardware"]
        nic1[10G 25G 100G NIC]
    end

    subgraph vfSet["Virtual Functions VFs"]
        vfA[VF 1 assigned to VM A]
        vfB[VF 2 assigned to VM B]
        vfC[VF 3 assigned to VM C]
    end

    pfdrv --> pfregs
    pfregs --> nic1
    nic1 -->|"Hardware Partitioning"| vfA
    nic1 -->|"Hardware Partitioning"| vfB
    nic1 -->|"Hardware Partitioning"| vfC

    iommu1[IOMMU VT-d or AMD Vi] -.->|"DMA Isolation"| vfA
    iommu1 -.->|"DMA Isolation"| vfB
    iommu1 -.->|"DMA Isolation"| vfC
```

## 4.6 Diagram 6 — DMA Remapping via IOMMU

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    vmguest[VM Guest Driver] -->|"Issues DMA with IO VA"| iommu2[IOMMU Engine]
    iommu2 -->|"Translates IO VA to HPA"| dram2[Host Physical RAM]
    iommu2 -->|"Enforces Access Policy"| perm[Per VF Access Table]
    perm -.-> iommu2
    dram2 -->|"Data Delivered"| vmguest
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The IOMMU is to **I/O virtualization** what the EPT is to **memory virtualization** — both are second-level translation engines, one for CPU-side addresses and one for device-side addresses.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define memory virtualization. Explain shadow paging.  `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Memory Virtualization** is the technique by which a VMM/hypervisor presents each VM with an illusion of dedicated, contiguous physical memory while sharing the actual physical RAM among multiple VMs. The hypervisor performs a **two-level address translation**: `GVA → GPA` (controlled by the guest) and `GPA → HPA` (controlled by the VMM).

**Shadow Paging** is a **software-based** memory virtualization technique:
- The VMM maintains a **shadow page table** that directly maps `GVA → HPA`.
- Whenever the guest modifies its own page table (e.g., on a `CR3` reload or `INVLPG` instruction), the VMM is **trapped** via a VM exit.
- The VMM then updates the **shadow page table** to reflect the new mapping.
- The CPU loads the shadow page table into the MMU and continues execution.

**Drawback:** Every page-table update by the guest causes a **VM exit**, which is expensive — typically hundreds to thousands of cycles per exit. Hence, shadow paging has been largely replaced by **hardware-assisted EPT/NPT** in modern hypervisors.

---

### Q2. What is SR-IOV? How does it improve I/O performance in cloud environments?  `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**SR-IOV (Single Root I/O Virtualization)** is a PCIe specification standard that allows a **single physical I/O device** (e.g., a network card) to present itself as **multiple separate PCI devices**.

- **Physical Function (PF):** The full-featured PCIe function used by the VMM for configuration and control of the device.
- **Virtual Functions (VFs):** Lightweight PCIe functions that share the physical resources of the PF. Each VF can be **independently assigned** to a different VM.

**Performance improvement:**
- The **data plane** (DMA, packet processing) is offloaded to hardware; the VM accesses the VF using **native drivers**.
- This **bypasses the VMM's I/O emulation path**, eliminating VM exits for every I/O operation.
- Combined with **IOMMU**, it provides **near-native throughput** (line-rate 10/25/100 Gbps) and **DMA isolation** between VFs.

**Use case:** AWS Enhanced Networking, Azure Accelerated Networking, and high-frequency trading clouds rely on SR-IOV for sub-microsecond latency and full link bandwidth per VM.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Do NOT confuse GPA with HPA** — examiners repeatedly deduct 2 marks for this.
> - **Do NOT skip diagrams** in 14-mark answers — a labeled diagram carries **2–3 marks** by itself.
> - **Always state the formula and units** for any numerical calculation, even if the result is obvious.
> - **Mention Intel VT-x / AMD-V explicitly** when discussing EPT/NPT — it is a high-value keyword.

---

### Question A — 14 Marks  `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain in detail the memory virtualization techniques in a cloud environment. Compare shadow paging with EPT.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Introduction to Memory Virtualization**  
Memory virtualization decouples the memory view presented to a VM from the actual physical memory of the host. Three address spaces are involved:
- **GVA** (Guest Virtual Address) — what the application uses.
- **GPA** (Guest Physical Address) — what the guest OS manages.
- **HPA** (Host Physical Address) — the actual DRAM location.

**2. Shadow Paging (Software Approach)**  
- The VMM maintains a **shadow page table** that contains direct `GVA → HPA` mappings.
- When the guest writes to its own page table, a **page-fault VM exit** occurs.
- The VMM intercepts the exit, validates the guest's intent (for safety), and **updates the shadow page table** atomically.
- The CPU then loads the shadow table into the MMU and resumes the guest.
- **Pros:** Works on any x86 CPU.  
- **Cons:** High VM-exit overhead; synchronization complexity with direct-page-table access by guest.

**3. EPT / NPT (Hardware-Assisted)**  
- **Intel EPT** and **AMD NPT** introduce a second set of page tables in hardware.
- The VMM sets the **EPT base pointer** (`EPTP` register) for each VM.
- The CPU's MMU performs a **two-dimensional page walk**: it first walks the guest tables, then walks the EPT tables to get the final HPA.
- The combined `GVA → HPA` mapping is cached in a **combined TLB**.
- **Pros:** No traps on page-table modifications; near-native performance.  
- **Cons:** Requires CPU virtualization extensions; deeper TLB miss penalty due to nested walks.

**4. Comparison Table**

| Feature | Shadow Paging | EPT / NPT |
|---------|---------------|-----------|
| Approach | Software | Hardware |
| VM exits on page-table update | Yes | No |
| Performance | Slow | Fast (near-native) |
| CPU requirement | Any x86 | Intel VT-x / AMD-V |
| Page-walk depth | 1 walk | 2 walks (nested) |
| Used in | Legacy VMware | Modern KVM / Hyper-V / ESXi |

**5. Conclusion** — Modern clouds universally use **EPT/NPT** because the performance gap is large, especially for memory-intensive workloads like in-memory databases.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Describe memory overcommitment, ballooning, and transparent page sharing with diagrams.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Memory Overcommitment**  
The technique of **allocating more virtual memory to VMs than the host physically has**. The overcommit ratio is $R_{\text{oc}} = \dfrac{M_{\text{configured}}}{M_{\text{physical}}}$. Ratios of $1.5\times$ to $3\times$ are common in dev/test clouds.

**2. Balloon Driver Mechanism**  
- A **balloon driver** is installed in each guest.
- When the host is under memory pressure, the VMM **inflates** the balloon: it tells the balloon driver to allocate *N* pages from the guest's free pool.
- These pages are then **returned to the host** as free HPA pages.
- When pressure subsides, the VMM **deflates** the balloon, releasing the pages back to the guest.

**Diagram:**

```
Host VMM                Guest VM
   |                       |
   |--Inflate (N pages)-->| Balloon Driver
   |                       | (allocates N pages)
   |<--Reclaim HPA--------| 
   |                       |
   |--Deflate ------------| Balloon Driver
   |                       | (releases N pages)
```

**3. Transparent Page Sharing (TPS)**  
- The VMM **hashes** the content of each guest page (e.g., SHA-1 of the 4 KiB content).
- If two pages have **identical hashes**, only one physical copy is kept; both GPA entries point to the same HPA.
- On the **first write** to a shared page, a **copy-on-write (COW)** exception occurs, and a private copy is created.

**4. Numerical Example**  
- 50 identical VMs running Ubuntu 22.04: each image uses ~50,000 pages.
- Without TPS: $50 \times 50{,}000 = 2{,}500{,}000$ pages.
- With TPS: only $50{,}000$ unique pages stored.
- **Savings: 2,450,000 pages ≈ 9.4 GiB**.

**5. Conclusion** — These three mechanisms (overcommitment, ballooning, TPS) allow clouds to **achieve 2×–3× VM density** without proportionally increasing hardware costs.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks  `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 — Alternative Choice]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain I/O virtualization techniques: emulation, para-virtualization, and direct assignment. Compare their performance.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Full Emulation**  
- The VMM (e.g., QEMU) **emulates** every register, interrupt, and DMA transaction of a real device (e.g., an Intel e1000 NIC).
- The guest uses a **generic driver** as if running on bare metal.
- Every I/O instruction (e.g., `IN`, `OUT`, MMIO) **traps** into the VMM.
- **Performance:** **Worst** — typically 10× to 100× slower than native due to VM-exit overhead.

**2. Para-virtualization (virtio)**  
- The guest uses a special **virtio front-end driver** that knows it is running in a VM.
- The front-end and **virtio back-end** (in VMM) communicate via a **shared ring buffer** and **hypercall** mechanism.
- Bulk data is transferred via **shared memory** (zero-copy) instead of register-level emulation.
- **Performance:** **Moderate** — 1.2× to 2× overhead vs. native. Used widely in KVM and Xen.

**3. Direct Assignment (PCI Passthrough)**  
- An **entire physical device** is dedicated to a single VM using **IOMMU** (Intel VT-d / AMD-Vi).
- The guest installs the **native device driver** and accesses the device directly.
- DMA is **remapped** by the IOMMU to prevent the device from accessing memory outside the VM's GPA range.
- **Performance:** **Near-native** — eliminates VMM involvement in the data path.
- **Drawback:** The device cannot be shared with other VMs.

**4. Comparison Table**

| Feature | Emulation | Para-virtualization | Direct Assignment |
|---------|-----------|----------------------|-------------------|
| Data path | VMM | VMM (shared mem) | Direct (IOMMU) |
| Performance | 10–100× slower | 1.2–2× slower | Near-native |
| Device sharing | Yes | Yes | No |
| Guest driver | Generic | virtio front-end | Native |
| Hardware needed | None | None | IOMMU |
| Use case | Dev/test | General cloud | High-perf VMs |

**5. Conclusion** — Clouds typically use **para-virtualization (virtio)** as the default and **direct assignment** for high-performance tenants (e.g., NFV, HPC).

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Describe SR-IOV in detail with a neat architectural diagram. Explain the role of IOMMU in providing DMA isolation.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. SR-IOV Concept**  
SR-IOV is a **PCI-SIG standard** that allows a single physical device (e.g., NIC, storage controller) to expose **multiple independent PCI functions**:
- **PF (Physical Function):** The full PCI function used by the VMM for control-plane management.
- **VFs (Virtual Functions):** Lightweight, "data-plane-only" PCI functions, each appearing as an independent device to a guest.

**2. Architectural Diagram**

```
+----------------------------+
|        Host Server         |
|  +-----------+             |
|  |   VMM     |             |
|  |  (PF Drv) |             |
|  +-----+-----+             |
|        |                   |
|  +-----v-----------------+ |
|  |   Physical NIC        | |
|  |  (e.g., 100 Gbps)     | |
|  +-----+-----------------+ |
|        |                   |
|  +-----+-----+-----+-----+ |
|  |  PF  | VF1 | VF2 | VF3 | |
|  +--+---+--+--+--+--+----+ |
|     |      |     |     |   |
|     |      |     |     |   |
|  +--+-+ +--+-+ +--+-+ +-+-+|
|  |VM0| |VM1| |VM2| |VM3|  |
|  +---+ +---+ +---+ +---+   |
+----------------------------+
        |       |
        +-------+
       IOMMU  (DMA isolation)
```

**3. Role of IOMMU (Intel VT-d / AMD-Vi)**  
The IOMMU **remaps DMA addresses** issued by each VF:
- A VF can only access **GPA pages** that the VMM has authorized in the IOMMU page table for that VF.
- If a malicious or buggy VF attempts to DMA into another VM's memory, the IOMMU **blocks the access** and raises a fault.
- This provides **hardware-enforced isolation** between VFs, equivalent to the EPT's role for CPU memory.

**4. SR-IOV Lifecycle**  
- **Step 1:** PF driver (in VMM) initializes the NIC and **enables SR-IOV** by writing to PCI config space.
- **Step 2:** VMM **probes** the VFs and assigns each to a VM via IOMMU programming.
- **Step 3:** Guest OS loads its **native VF driver** (e.g., `ixgbevf` for Intel).
- **Step 4:** All packet I/O happens **directly** between the VM and the VF, bypassing the VMM.
- **Step 5:** PF retains control-plane functions (link state, stats, SR-IOV config).

**5. Limitations and Cloud Trade-offs**  
- **Live migration** of a VM using SR-IOV is **complex** because the VF state must be checkpointed and restored.
- Clouds like AWS and Azure offer SR-IOV as an **opt-in premium feature** for customers who can accept this trade-off.
- Workloads like **NFV vRAN** and **HPC** benefit the most.

**6. Conclusion** — SR-IOV is the **gold standard** for cloud I/O virtualization, combining hardware-level performance with multi-tenant isolation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Must-Memorize Items**

- **Three-tier address translation:** `GVA → GPA → HPA` — the foundation of memory virtualization.
- **EPT (Intel) and NPT (AMD):** Hardware-assisted nested page tables; near-native performance.
- **Shadow paging:** Software approach; high VM-exit overhead; legacy only.
- **TLB hit rate formula:** $T_{\text{eff}} = h \cdot T_{\text{hit}} + (1-h) \cdot T_{\text{miss}}$.
- **Virtualized access time formula:** $T_{\text{virt}} = T_{\text{TLB}} + 8 \cdot T_{\text{EPT}} + 5 \cdot T_{\text{mem}}$.
- **Overcommit ratio formula:** $R_{\text{oc}} = \dfrac{\sum M_{\text{config}}}{M_{\text{physical}}}$.
- **Balloon driver:** Inflates inside guest to reclaim host memory; VMM-driven, guest-cooperative.
- **Transparent Page Sharing (TPS):** Hash-based deduplication with copy-on-write; saves read-only pages.
- **Three I/O virtualization models:** Emulation, Para-virtualization (virtio), Direct Assignment (PCI passthrough).
- **SR-IOV:** PF + multiple VFs; uses IOMMU for DMA isolation; near-native performance.
- **IOMMU:** Translates I/O virtual addresses to HPA; prevents unauthorized DMA — same role for I/O as EPT for CPU.
- **Huge pages (2 MiB / 1 GiB):** Increase TLB reach; reduce nested-walk overhead — critical in cloud DB workloads.
- **Limit of 256 VFs per PF:** Theoretical PCIe spec limit; practical deployments use 8–64.
- **Common confusion to avoid:** GPA ≠ HPA; SR-IOV ≠ virtio; EPT ≠ NPT (vendor names, same function).
- **Examiner's hot keywords:** `GVA → GPA → HPA`, `EPTP`, `IOMMU`, `virtio ring buffer`, `balloon driver`, `PF/VF`, `DMA remapping`, `MSI-X`, `huge pages`.

> [!NOTE]
> **One-line mantra for the exam hall:**  
> *"EPT translates for the CPU; IOMMU translates for the devices; together they make cloud multi-tenancy safe and fast."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Desktop Virtualization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT634 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Desktop Virtualization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Desktop Virtualization** is a server-based computing paradigm in which the logical desktop environment (operating system, applications, user data, and configuration state) is decoupled from the physical client hardware and is instead hosted, executed, and centrally managed on a remote server, data center, or cloud platform. End-user devices (thin clients, zero clients, laptops, tablets, or BYOD endpoints) act purely as input/output terminals that use a remote display protocol to render the graphical desktop stream delivered by the hypervisor or session host.

According to the NIST SP 500-292 reference model, Desktop Virtualization falls under the **"Software as a Service (SaaS)"** and **"Platform as a Service (PaaS)"** layers of Cloud Computing because the entire desktop operating environment is delivered to the end-user as an on-demand, network-accessible, pooled, and elastic service.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Desktop Virtualization is one of the foundational delivery models of Cloud Computing. It demonstrates the transition from a *per-device* licensing model to a *per-user* subscription model, and it is a critical building block for **VDI (Virtual Desktop Infrastructure)**, **DaaS (Desktop-as-a-Service)**, and **Remote Desktop Services (RDS)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core KTU Terminology to Memorize:**
> - **Host Machine** – The physical server that runs the virtualization layer.
> - **Guest OS** – The virtualized operating system (Windows/Linux) running inside a VM.
> - **Hypervisor** – The VMM (Virtual Machine Monitor) that arbitrates hardware access.
> - **Connection Broker** – The middleware that authenticates users and assigns them a desktop.
> - **Remote Display Protocol** – The wire protocol (RDP, PCoIP, HDX, SPICE) used to stream the desktop.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of **Desktop Virtualization** like a **centralized library with a librarian**.

- In a **traditional desktop model**, every reader (employee) must own a personal copy of every book (application) at home. If the book is updated, every copy must be replaced. If the book is lost, the reader has a problem.
- In a **desktop virtualization model**, there is one big central library (the data center). The librarian pulls the right book, opens it to the right page, and projects the page on a screen in the reader's room. The reader only sees the *image* of the book and interacts with it through a simple viewing window (thin client). The actual book never leaves the library.

This is precisely what happens: applications and OS run on the server, and the user only receives a *visual stream* plus sends *keystrokes/mouse events* back to the server.

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Summary for First-Time Learners:**
> Desktop Virtualization = "My desktop lives in a data center, not on my laptop. My laptop is just a window into it."

### 1.3 Standard Metrics in Desktop Virtualization

The following **bold** metrics are universally used in KTU and industry evaluations:

- **OS Instances per Server (Density / Consolidation Ratio):** Typically **50–100 VMs per host** for VDI workloads (varies by user persona).
- **Boot Storm Time:** Time taken to power on all VMs simultaneously (must be **< 15 minutes** for 1000 seats).
- **Login VSI Baseline Score:** Industry-standard workload index used to measure user density.
- **Round-Trip Latency (RTL):** Should be **< 150 ms** for a smooth desktop experience.
- **Pixels-per-Second (PPS):** Bandwidth metric for remoting protocols; typical demand is **5–25 Mbps per user**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Core "Why" and "How" of Desktop Virtualization

#### Why Desktop Virtualization?
1. **Centralized Management** – Patches, upgrades, and anti-virus updates are pushed to a single image rather than hundreds of physical machines.
2. **Hardware Independence** – Users can access their full desktop from any device, including personal tablets/phones (BYOD).
3. **Data Security & Compliance** – Since data never leaves the data center, the risk of loss due to a stolen laptop is eliminated.
4. **Resource Optimization** – Server hardware is pooled, and idle desktops consume only memory, not CPU cycles.
5. **Disaster Recovery** – A user's desktop is backed up centrally; any endpoint can become a temporary access device.

#### How Desktop Virtualization Works (Operational Logic Steps)
1. The **hypervisor** (Type-1 bare-metal: VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V, Citrix XenServer, KVM) is installed on physical servers in the data center.
2. A **desktop image** (golden image) is created and stored in a repository.
3. A **connection broker** (e.g., VMware Horizon Connection Server, Citrix StoreFront, Microsoft RD Broker) authenticates users against Active Directory / LDAP.
4. Upon successful authentication, the broker assigns a desktop VM from the pool (or a personal persistent VM).
5. A **remote display protocol** is negotiated between the endpoint and the VM, establishing a graphical session.
6. Keystrokes, mouse clicks, and USB redirections are sent upstream; pixel/vector updates are sent downstream.

### 2.2 Types / Models of Desktop Virtualization

| Model | Full Form | Execution Location | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **VDI** | Virtual Desktop Infrastructure | Each user gets a dedicated VM on the server | Power users, developers, designers |
| **RDS / TS** | Remote Desktop Services / Terminal Services | Users share a single OS instance via sessions | Task workers, call centers |
| **SBC** | Server-Based Computing | Application streaming from a shared server | Legacy app compatibility |
| **DaaS** | Desktop-as-a-Service | Cloud-hosted, multi-tenant | SMEs, remote workforce |
| **Client Hypervisor** | Local Type-2 Hypervisor | Desktop VM runs on user's laptop | Offline mobility, dual-OS |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Quantitative Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> Desktop Virtualization relies heavily on **capacity planning formulas**. The following table consolidates every formula a KTU student must memorize for calculations.

| Metric | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Total RAM Required** | $\text{RAM}_{\text{total}} = (\text{RAM}_{\text{per-VM}} + \text{Overhead}) \times N_{\text{VMs}}$ | Sum of memory allocated to all VMs + hypervisor reservation |
| **CPU Cores Required** | $C_{\text{req}} = \left\lceil \frac{N_{\text{VMs}} \times vCPU_{\text{per-VM}}}{C_{\text{core-ratio}}}\right\rceil$ | Accounts for vCPU-to-pCPU oversubscription ratio |
| **Storage IOPS** | $\text{IOPS}_{\text{total}} = N_{\text{VMs}} \times \text{IOPS}_{\text{per-VM}}$ | Critical for boot-storm and login-storm scenarios |
| **Storage Capacity** | $S_{\text{total}} = (D_{\text{OS}} + D_{\text{user}} + D_{\text{swap}}) \times N_{\text{VMs}} \times (1 + R_{\text{replica}})$ | Includes provisioning factor and replica copies |
| **Network Bandwidth (Upstream)** | $BW_{\text{up}} = N_{\text{users}} \times R_{\text{per-user}} \times (1 + H_{\text{headroom}})$ | Per-user remoting-protocol bandwidth |
| **Consolidation Ratio** | $R_{\text{cons}} = \frac{N_{\text{VMs}}}{\text{Physical Hosts}}$ | VMs per physical server |
| **Connection Broker Throughput** | $T_{\text{broker}} = \frac{\text{Concurrent Sessions}}{\text{Avg. Session Duration}}$ | Logins per second capacity |
| **Login Storm Duration** | $T_{\text{storm}} = \frac{N_{\text{users}}}{R_{\text{boot-per-host}} \times N_{\text{hosts}}}$ | Time for all users to be ready at 9 AM |
| **Cost per Seat per Month** | $C_{\text{seat}} = \frac{\text{TCO}}{N_{\text{seats}} \times M_{\text{months}}}$ | Total Cost of Ownership divided by seat-months |
| **Desktop Availability (SLA)** | $\text{Availability} = \frac{\text{Uptime}}{\text{Uptime} + \text{Downtime}} \times 100\%$ | Target: **99.9%** for VDI |

**Where:**
- $N_{\text{VMs}}$ = Number of virtual desktops
- $D_{\text{OS}}$, $D_{\text{user}}$, $D_{\text{swap}}$ = Disk space for OS image, user data, swap
- $R_{\text{replica}}$ = Replication factor for HA/DR
- $R_{\text{per-user}}$ = Average bandwidth per remoting session
- $H_{\text{headroom}}$ = Headroom factor (typically **20–30%**)
- $C_{\text{core-ratio}}$ = pCPU-to-vCPU ratio (commonly **1:4 to 1:8** for VDI)
- $R_{\text{boot-per-host}}$ = Boot rate per physical host

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Desktop Virtualization is the backbone of:
- **COVID-era remote work infrastructure** (Zoom, Microsoft 365, EPAM all run on virtualized desktops for contractors).
- **Healthcare PACS systems** – Doctors access patient imaging from any device.
- **Banking & Financial Services** – Regulatory compliance (PCI-DSS, RBI guidelines) mandates data residency in the data center.
- **Defense & Intelligence** – Secure "thin client" deployments prevent data exfiltration.
- **Engineering Education Labs (KTU Context)** – Students access licensed software (AutoCAD, MATLAB, SolidWorks) from any lab or home laptop without installing locally.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Worked-Out Derivation: VDI Capacity Planning

This is the **single most important calculation** in a KTU Cloud Computing exam on Desktop Virtualization. The problem is typically framed as:

> *"An organization wants to deploy 500 virtual desktops. Each VM requires 2 vCPUs, 4 GB RAM, and 60 GB disk. The hypervisor has a 4:1 vCPU:pCPU ratio. Each physical host has 16 cores, 256 GB RAM, and 2 TB usable storage. Storage replication factor is 2. Calculate total physical servers required."*

#### Step 1: Compute Physical Hosts Required Based on CPU
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total vCPUs needed} &= N_{\text{VMs}} \times vCPU_{\text{per-VM}} \\
&= 500 \times 2 \\
&= 1000 \text{ vCPUs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Each user needs 2 vCPUs, so 500 users need 1000 vCPUs in total.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Physical cores required} &= \frac{\text{Total vCPUs}}{C_{\text{core-ratio}}} \\
&= \frac{1000}{4} \\
&= 250 \text{ physical cores}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** A 4:1 oversubscription means every 1 physical core serves 4 vCPUs.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Physical hosts (CPU-bound)} &= \left\lceil \frac{\text{Physical cores required}}{\text{Cores per host}} \right\rceil \\
&= \left\lceil \frac{250}{16} \right\rceil \\
&= \lceil 15.625 \rceil \\
&= 16 \text{ hosts}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** You cannot buy a fraction of a server, so round up to the next whole host.

#### Step 2: Compute Physical Hosts Required Based on RAM
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total RAM needed} &= N_{\text{VMs}} \times \text{RAM}_{\text{per-VM}} \\
&= 500 \times 4 \text{ GB} \\
&= 2000 \text{ GB} \\
&= 2 \text{ TB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** 500 users × 4 GB each = 2 TB of usable RAM for VMs.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Physical hosts (RAM-bound)} &= \left\lceil \frac{\text{Total RAM needed}}{\text{RAM per host}} \right\rceil \\
&= \left\lceil \frac{2000 \text{ GB}}{256 \text{ GB}} \right\rceil \\
&= \lceil 7.8125 \rceil \\
&= 8 \text{ hosts}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Each server provides 256 GB, so 8 servers cover the 2 TB requirement.

#### Step 3: Compute Physical Hosts Required Based on Storage
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Raw storage needed} &= N_{\text{VMs}} \times D_{\text{per-VM}} \\
&= 500 \times 60 \text{ GB} \\
&= 30000 \text{ GB} \\
&= 30 \text{ TB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** 500 users × 60 GB per VM = 30 TB of logical storage.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Effective storage with replication} &= \text{Raw} \times (1 + R_{\text{replica}}) \\
&= 30 \text{ TB} \times (1 + 2) \\
&= 30 \text{ TB} \times 3 \\
&= 90 \text{ TB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** A replication factor of 2 means 2 extra copies (1 original + 2 replicas = 3× total).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Physical hosts (Storage-bound)} &= \left\lceil \frac{90 \text{ TB}}{2 \text{ TB per host}} \right\rceil \\
&= \left\lceil 45 \right\rceil \\
&= 45 \text{ hosts}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Each host provides 2 TB of storage; we need 90 TB, so 45 hosts.

#### Step 4: Determine the Bottleneck Resource
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Final Host Count} &= \max(\text{CPU-bound}, \text{RAM-bound}, \text{Storage-bound}) \\
&= \max(16, 8, 45) \\
&= 45 \text{ hosts}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** The system is **storage-bound**; the limiting factor is the disk subsystem. We must size for the worst-case constraint.

#### Step 5: Compute Network Bandwidth
$$
\begin{aligned}
BW_{\text{up}} &= N_{\text{users}} \times R_{\text{per-user}} \times (1 + H_{\text{headroom}}) \\
&= 500 \times 10 \text{ Mbps} \times (1 + 0.25) \\
&= 5000 \times 1.25 \\
&= 6250 \text{ Mbps} \\
&= 6.25 \text{ Gbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** 10 Mbps per user × 1.25 headroom (25%) ensures headroom for peak usage. Total upstream bandwidth is 6.25 Gbps.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: VDI vs RDS vs DaaS

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This comparison matrix is **direct from the KTU Module-2 syllabus**. Examiners frequently award 7 marks for a clear, tabular comparison.

| Parameter | **VDI (Virtual Desktop Infrastructure)** | **RDS (Remote Desktop Services)** | **DaaS (Desktop-as-a-Service)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Architecture** | One VM per user | Multiple users share one OS | Cloud-hosted multi-tenant |
| **Personalization** | High (per-user) | Low (per-session) | Medium to High |
| **Cost per User** | High | Low | Subscription-based |
| **Scalability** | Limited by on-prem hardware | High (sessions are lightweight) | Virtually unlimited |
| **Management** | Complex | Simple | Fully managed by provider |
| **Performance** | Excellent (dedicated resources) | Moderate (shared resources) | Variable (depends on SLA tier) |
| **Use Case** | CAD/Engineering/Developers | Call centers/Task workers | SMEs/Startups |
| **Disaster Recovery** | Excellent | Moderate | Built-in |
| **Licensing** | Per-user/per-device | Per-user CAL | Per-user subscription |
| **Data Residency** | On-premise | On-premise | Provider's region |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
