# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**Define Cloud Storage. List any FOUR characteristics of cloud storage.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Cloud Storage is a *Storage as a Service (STaaS)* model in which digital data is stored on logical pools of physical storage hosted and managed by a Cloud Service Provider (CSP), accessed over a network (typically the Internet) on a pay-per-use basis.

**Four Characteristics (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):**

1. **Elastic Scalability** — Capacity grows/shrinks on demand without downtime.
2. **High Durability** — Achieves 11 nines (99.999999999%) via redundancy.
3. **Pay-per-Use Pricing** — Billed per GB stored, transferred, and per request.
4. **Multi-Tenancy** — Multiple customers share physical infrastructure securely.
5. *(Any other valid: Self-service provisioning, Geo-replication, High availability SLA)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
**Differentiate between Object Storage, Block Storage, and File Storage with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per row):**

| Type | Abstraction | Example |
|------|-------------|---------|
| **Object Storage** | Flat namespace; data + metadata + unique ID stored as objects accessed via HTTP REST API | AWS S3, Azure Blob Storage |
| **Block Storage** | Raw storage volumes divided into fixed-size blocks; mounted as a disk | AWS EBS, Azure Disk |
| **File Storage** | Hierarchical directory structure (folders & files) accessed via standard file protocols (NFS/SMB) | AWS EFS, Azure Files |

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Module 3 Question]**

**(a) Explain the architecture of Cloud Storage with a neat block diagram. List and briefly describe any THREE storage tiers/classes offered by major cloud providers.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Architecture (4 marks):** Cloud Storage is organized as a **multi-layered architecture** comprising the following components (refer to the block diagram in Section 4.1):

1. **Access Layer (1 mark):** Provides REST APIs (HTTP GET/PUT/DELETE), SDKs, and web consoles for users to interact with storage. Each request is authenticated, authorized, and routed to the backend.
2. **Metadata/Indexing Layer (1 mark):** Maintains a distributed index of all objects using a Consistent Hash Ring or DHT. Stores metadata such as object name, size, location, owner, and version.
3. **Storage Layer (1 mark):** The physical disks (HDD, SSD, NVMe) that store the actual data bytes. Fault tolerance is achieved via Replication or Erasure Coding.
4. **Infrastructure Layer (1 mark):** The data center facilities housing servers, networking equipment, power backups, and cooling systems.

**Storage Tiers (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Hot Tier (e.g., AWS S3 Standard):** Designed for frequently accessed data. Millisecond-level access. Highest cost per GB (~$0.023/GB/month). Backed by SSDs.
2. **Cool/Cold Tier (e.g., AWS S3 Standard-IA, Glacier):** For data accessed once a month or quarter. Retrieval time of minutes to hours. Lower cost (~$0.004/GB/month).
3. **Archive Tier (e.g., AWS S3 Glacier Deep Archive):** For long-term retention (7–10 years). Retrieval takes 12 hours. Lowest cost (~$0.00099/GB/month). Backed by tape libraries.

---

**(b) A company wants to store 20 TB of data using Reed-Solomon erasure coding with parameters k=10 and m=4. Compute (i) total physical storage required, (ii) storage overhead percentage, and (iii) maximum number of simultaneous node failures tolerated. Compare this with 3-way replication in terms of storage efficiency.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** $U_{data} = 20$ TB, $k = 10$, $m = 4$

**(i) Total Physical Storage (2 marks):**

$$S_{total} = (k + m) \cdot \frac{U_{data}}{k} = (10 + 4) \cdot \frac{20}{10} = 14 \cdot 2 = 28 \text{ TB}$$

**(ii) Storage Overhead Percentage (2 marks):**

$$E_{ec} = \frac{m}{k+m} \cdot 100 = \frac{4}{14} \cdot 100 = 28.57\%$$

**Extra storage used** = $28 - 20 = 8$ TB (parity data only).

**(iii) Maximum Tolerable Failures (1 mark):**

$$F_{tolerance} = m = 4 \text{ nodes}$$

Up to **4 nodes can fail simultaneously** without data loss.

**Comparison with 3-Way Replication (2 marks):**

For 3-way replication of 20 TB:

$$S_{replication} = U_{data} \cdot R_{factor} = 20 \cdot 3 = 60 \text{ TB}$$

$$E_{replication} = \frac{n-1}{n} \cdot 100 = \frac{2}{3} \cdot 100 = 66.67\%$$

| Metric | 3-Way Replication | Erasure Coding RS(10,4) |
|--------|-------------------|--------------------------|
| Physical Storage | **60 TB** | **28 TB** |
| Overhead | **66.67%** | **28.57%** |
| Failures Tolerated | 2 replicas | 4 nodes |
| Storage Savings | — | **~53.3% less storage used** |

**Conclusion:** Erasure Coding RS(10,4) uses **53.3% less storage** than 3-way replication while tolerating **more simultaneous failures** (4 vs 2), making it vastly superior for large-scale cloud storage.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Module 3 Question]**

**(a) Explain the concepts of (i) Replication and (ii) Erasure Coding as fault-tolerance mechanisms in cloud storage. State one advantage and one disadvantage of each.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Replication (3.5 marks):**

**Concept:** Replication creates multiple identical copies (replicas) of the data and stores them on different physical nodes, racks, or Availability Zones (AZs). If one replica fails, the system reads from another replica seamlessly.

- **Example:** In 3-way replication, $R_{factor} = 3$ — three copies of every data chunk exist on three different nodes.
- **Replication Factor Formula:** $S_{replicated} = U_{data} \cdot n$
- **Mechanism:** When a write request arrives, the primary node replicates the data to other nodes before acknowledging success (synchronous) or shortly after (asynchronous).

**Advantage:** Fast read/write performance; simple to implement; low recovery latency since data is already available on another node.
**Disadvantage:** Very high storage overhead (200% for 3-way); expensive at petabyte scale.

**(ii) Erasure Coding (3.5 marks):**

**Concept:** Erasure Coding splits the original data into $k$ equal chunks, then computes $m$ parity chunks using mathematical functions (e.g., Reed-Solomon codes). The $k+m$ chunks are distributed across different nodes. Data can be reconstructed from any $k$ surviving chunks.

- **Example:** RS(10,4) splits data into 10 chunks, computes 4 parity chunks, distributed across 14 nodes. Can survive any 4 simultaneous failures.
- **Storage Formula:** $S_{total} = (k+m) \cdot \frac{U_{data}}{k}$

**Advantage:** Much lower storage overhead (~28.6% for RS(10,4) vs 200% for 3-way replication); provides higher fault tolerance per unit of storage.
**Disadvantage:** Higher write latency (parity computation); complex and CPU-intensive reconstruction on failure.

---

**(b) A startup generates 5 TB of log data monthly. 80% of the data is queried frequently in the first 30 days, after which it is rarely accessed. Design a tiered storage strategy and compute the monthly cost assuming the following rates: Hot = ₹2/GB/month, Cool = ₹0.5/GB/month, Archive = ₹0.1/GB/month. Also compute the savings if all 5 TB were stored in Hot tier.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Strategy Design (3 marks):**

| Data Fraction | Storage Tier | Duration |
|---------------|--------------|----------|
| **4 TB (80%)** — fresh logs | Hot | First 30 days |
| **4 TB (80%)** — aged logs | Archive (after 30 days) | After 30 days |
| **1 TB (20%)** — frequently queried | Hot | Always |

**Tiered Cost Calculation (3 marks):**

Assuming steady-state (data older than 30 days is in Archive):

$$C_{tiered} = (S_{hot} \cdot c_{hot}) + (S_{archive} \cdot c_{archive})$$

$$C_{tiered} = (1 \text{ TB} \cdot 1024 \text{ GB} \cdot ₹2) + (4 \text{ TB} \cdot 1024 \text{ GB} \cdot ₹0.1)$$

$$C_{tiered} = (1024 \cdot 2) + (4096 \cdot 0.1)$$

$$C_{tiered} = ₹2048 + ₹409.6 = ₹2457.6 \text{ per month}$$

**All-Hot Cost Calculation (1 mark):**

$$C_{hot} = 5 \text{ TB} \cdot 1024 \text{ GB} \cdot ₹2 = 5120 \cdot 2 = ₹10240 \text{ per month}$$

**Savings (1 mark):**

$$\Delta C = ₹10240 - ₹2457.6 = ₹7782.4$$

$$\text{Savings \%} = \frac{7782.4}{10240} \cdot 100 = 76.0\%$$

> **Conclusion:** A proper tiered strategy saves **76%** in monthly storage cost. This is a classic **Cost Optimization** pattern in cloud storage — a frequently tested KTU concept.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Unit Confusion in Cost Calculations:** Forgetting to convert **TB → GB** (1 TB = 1024 GB) is the #1 mark-losing mistake. Always state units explicitly.
> 2. **Misapplying Replication Formula:** Students often write $E_{replication} = \frac{n}{n-1}$ instead of $\frac{n-1}{n}$. Memorize: **overhead = extra copies = n − 1, divided by total copies = n**.
> 3. **Confusing Durability with Availability:** Durability = "data will not be lost"; Availability = "data can be read when requested". They are NOT the same metric.
> 4. **Forgetting Tier Retrieval Costs:** Hot tier costs are storage-only. Archive retrieval incurs per-GB retrieval fees. KTU may deduct marks if you ignore this.
> 5. **Mixing up k and m in Erasure Coding:** In RS(k,m), **k is data chunks, m is parity chunks**. Reversing these gives wrong answers.
> 6. **Ignoring Lifecycle Policies:** For tiered cost questions, ALWAYS state the lifecycle policy (e.g., "move to archive after 30 days of inactivity"). Bonus marks for mentioning automation via S3 Lifecycle Rules.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Top 15 High-Yield Points for KTU Module 3 — Cloud Storage:**

- **Definition:** Cloud Storage = *logical pools of physical storage over the network, pay-per-use, managed by CSP*.
- **Core Service Model:** Storage as a Service (**STaaS**).
- **Three Storage Types:** Object (HTTP/REST), Block (SAN), File (NFS/SMB).
- **Object Storage is most cloud-native** (S3, Azure Blob, GCS).
- **Four Architectural Layers:** Access → Metadata → Storage → Infrastructure.
- **Hot vs Cool vs Archive Tiers:** Trade-off between cost and access latency.
- **Replication Overhead Formula:** $E = \frac{n-1}{n}$.
- **Erasure Coding Formula:** $E = \frac{m}{k+m}$, Tolerates `m` failures.
- **RS(10,4):** 28.6% overhead, tolerates 4 failures → preferred for large-scale data.
- **Durability Formula:** $D = 1 - (1-p)^{R \cdot Z}$; target = 11 nines.
- **Cost Formula:** Monthly Cost = $(C_{storage} \cdot S) + (C_{bandwidth} \cdot B)$.
- **Consistency Models:** Strong, Eventual, Read-Your-Writes, Causal.
- **Major Providers:** AWS S3/EBS/Glacier, Azure Blob, GCS, MinIO, Ceph.
- **Cost Optimization:** Use lifecycle policies to move aged data to cheaper tiers (saves 40–80%).
- **Key SLAs:** Durability 99.999999999%, Availability 99.9–99.99%.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Provisioning Cloud Storage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT634 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Provisioning Cloud Storage

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Cloud Storage Provisioning** is the process of dynamically allocating, configuring, and managing logical and physical storage resources (capacity, performance tier, redundancy level, and geographic location) to cloud tenants, applications, or services through automated or semi-automated mechanisms that align with **Quality of Service (QoS)**, **Service Level Agreements (SLAs)**, and **pay-per-use** consumption models.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECDT634 – Cloud Computing)**, provisioning cloud storage is treated as a subset of *Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS)* and *Storage-as-a-Service (STaaS)*, encompassing the full lifecycle of storage resources — from **capacity planning → allocation → mounting → de-provisioning**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Module 3):**
> Provisioning is *not* just "creating a disk". It involves the **policy-driven binding** of a tenant request to a physical LUN (Logical Unit Number), a virtual block, an object bucket, or a file-system mount, governed by **multi-tenancy**, **elasticity**, and **data placement policies**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine you run a small business that needs storage space. You have three options:

| Option | Real-World Equivalent | Cloud Mapping |
|---|---|---|
| **Buy a godown** (warehouse) | Over-provisioned private data center | Reserved, rigid capacity |
| **Rent a fixed shop** | Thick provisioning | Allocated in advance, paid fully |
| **Rent shelves on-demand** | Thin/dynamic provisioning | Pay only for used space, expand anytime |
| **Self-storage locker** | Object storage (S3/Blob) | Accessed via ticket (API) |

**Cloud storage provisioning is the "self-storage locker" model of enterprise IT.** You walk in (API call), get a key (object/bucket/credential), use any size locker (elastic capacity), and walk out (de-provision) without paperwork. The provider hides the brick-and-mortar (RAID, replication, datacenter) and the rules (SLAs, policies) are stated upfront.

## 1.3 Core Provisioning Concepts — At a Glance

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Pillars of Cloud Storage Provisioning:**
> 1. **Capacity** — How many GB/TB/PB are made available.
> 2. **Performance** — IOPS (Input/Output Operations Per Second), throughput, latency class.
> 3. **Protection** — Replication factor, RAID level, encryption, geographic redundancy.

- **Provisioned IOPS** — A guaranteed I/O rate (e.g., **1000 IOPS** minimum, **4000 IOPS** for high-performance).
- **Storage Tier** — Hot (frequent access, SSD), Cool/Infrequent Access (HDD), Archive (glacier-class).
- **Elasticity Window** — The auto-scale range (e.g., min **100 GB** → max **10 TB**).
- **Multi-Tenancy** — Multiple customers share underlying LUNs but are logically isolated via namespaces, ACLs, and encryption.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic — "CAPE":** When you provision, always ask: **C**apacity, **A**vailability, **P**erformance, **E**lasticity.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Provisioning Lifecycle (Operational Steps)

Cloud storage provisioning follows a structured, policy-driven pipeline:

1. **Tenant Request Ingestion**
   - A tenant submits a request via **Web Portal**, **CLI**, or **REST API** (e.g., `PUT /v1/storage/buckets`).
   - Request payload: `{size, tier, region, redundancy, encryption, IOPS}`.

2. **Policy Validation & Admission Control**
   - Hypervisor / Storage Controller checks: quota, SLA tier, region availability, account status.
   - *Why?* Prevents over-commitment and SLA violations.

3. **Logical Resource Allocation**
   - Allocates a **virtual disk (VHD/VMDK)**, an **object bucket**, or a **LUN** from a storage pool.
   - *How?* Maps tenant ID → namespace → physical block range.

4. **Physical Placement**
   - Decides on **which disk, rack, datacenter** the data lands — based on:
     - Affinity rules
     - Geo-redundancy requirements
     - Load balancing across storage nodes

5. **Data Protection Configuration**
   - Applies **RAID level**, **replication factor (RF)**, and **erasure coding**.
   - Creates snapshots, schedules backups.

6. **Service Activation & Handoff**
   - Mounts the volume, returns the access path / endpoint / key to tenant.
   - Begins **metering** for billing.

7. **Ongoing Management**
   - Monitors IOPS, throughput, capacity utilization.
   - Triggers **auto-scaling** (scale-up) or **data tiering** (scale-down / move to cold).

8. **De-provisioning**
   - On tenant termination: data is wiped, blocks returned to free pool, cryptographic shredding performed.

## 2.2 Provisioning Models — Comparative Analysis

| Model | Mechanism | Best For | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Thick (Eager) Provisioning** | Allocates full capacity upfront; zeros the disk | Databases, predictable workloads | Wasted space, slow creation |
| **Thin (Lazy) Provisioning** | Allocates only what is written; grows dynamically | Dev/Test, unpredictable workloads | Metadata overhead, fragmentation |
| **Dynamic / Elastic Provisioning** | Auto-scales based on policies (CPU/IOPS threshold) | Big Data, analytics bursts | Complex monitoring |
| **Just-in-Time (JIT) Provisioning** | Provisioned on first I/O request | Ephemeral containers | Cold-start latency |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they form the spine of KTU 14-mark problems.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **RAID 0 Capacity** (striping, no parity) | $C_{0} = N \times D$ | $N$ = number of disks, $D$ = smallest disk size (GB) |
| 2 | **RAID 1 Capacity** (mirroring) | $C_{1} = N \times D \,/\, 2$ | Same as above |
| 3 | **RAID 5 Capacity** (single parity) | $C_{5} = (N - 1) \times D$ | Tolerates 1 disk failure |
| 4 | **RAID 6 Capacity** (double parity) | $C_{6} = (N - 2) \times D$ | Tolerates 2 disk failures |
| 5 | **RAID 10 Capacity** (1+0) | $C_{10} = (N \,/\, 2) \times D$ | Mirrored stripes |
| 6 | **Erasure Coding Overhead** | $O_{ec} = k + m$ vs usable $k$ | $k$ = data shards, $m$ = parity shards |
| 7 | **Storage Efficiency (Thin)** | $E = 1 - (\text{Allocated} - \text{Used}) \,/\, \text{Allocated}$ | Ratio in $[0,1]$ |
| 8 | **Throughput (Streaming)** | $T = B \times R$ | $B$ = block size (KB), $R$ = IOPS |
| 9 | **M/M/1 Mean Response Time** | $W = 1 \,/\, (\mu - \lambda)$ | $\mu$ = service rate, $\lambda$ = arrival rate (both req/s) |
| 10 | **Storage Utilization** | $U = (\text{Used} \,/\, \text{Provisioned}) \times 100$ | Percentage |
| 11 | **Compression Ratio** | $R_{c} = S_{orig} \,/\, S_{comp}$ | $> 1$ means savings |
| 12 | **Deduplication Ratio** | $R_{d} = S_{logical} \,/\, S_{physical}$ | $> 1$ means savings |
| 13 | **Monthly Cost (Object Storage)** | $C_{m} = (G \times P_{s}) + (T \times P_{t}) + (R \times P_{r})$ | $G$ = GB stored, $T$ = TB transferred out, $R$ = API requests |
| 14 | **Replication Factor Cost Multiplier** | $C_{total} = C_{primary} \times RF$ | RF = 2, 3 typical |
| 15 | **Five-Nines Availability Downtime** | $D_{year} = 525600 \times (1 - A)$ | Minutes/year; $A=0.99999 \Rightarrow \approx 5.26$ min |

> **Units Convention:** Storage in **GB / TB / PB**, Performance in **IOPS** and **MB/s**, Time in **seconds**, Cost in **USD/GB-month** (or INR/GB-month for KTU context).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cloud-Native Databases** (Amazon Aurora, Azure Cosmos DB) use **thin provisioning + auto-scaling** to grow storage transparently.
- **Data Lakes** (AWS S3 + Glacier) use **lifecycle-based tiering** to move cold data to cheap storage.
- **Disaster Recovery** relies on **geo-replication** with RF = 3 across availability zones.
- **Big Data (Hadoop/HDFS)** uses **erasure coding (RS-6-3)** to slash storage cost by ~50% vs 3× replication.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Effective Usable Capacity under RAID

**Problem Statement:** A cloud provider provisions a storage pool of **8 disks**, each of size **2 TB**. The pool is configured with **RAID 6**. Compute the effective usable capacity for tenants, the fault tolerance, and the storage efficiency ratio.

### Step 1 — Identify parameters
- $N = 8$ (number of disks)
- $D = 2 \text{ TB}$
- RAID level = 6 (double parity)

### Step 2 — Apply RAID-6 capacity formula
Using the formula from the cheat sheet:

$$
C_{6} = (N - 2) \times D
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute values
$$
C_{6} = (8 - 2) \times 2 \text{ TB}
$$

$$
C_{6} = 6 \times 2 \text{ TB}
$$

$$
C_{6} = 12 \text{ TB}
$$

### Step 4 — Fault tolerance statement
RAID 6 tolerates **2 simultaneous disk failures** without data loss. The provider can now offer **12 TB usable** to tenants while surviving the loss of any 2 disks.

### Step 5 — Storage efficiency ratio
$$
\eta = \frac{C_{usable}}{C_{raw}} = \frac{(N-2)\times D}{N \times D} = \frac{N-2}{N}
$$

$$
\eta = \frac{8 - 2}{8} = \frac{6}{8} = 0.75 \;(75\%)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Out of every 100 GB of raw capacity, only **75 GB** is billable; **25 GB** is reserved for parity. Compare with **3× replication**: efficiency drops to **33%**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Mean Response Time under M/M/1 Queueing

**Problem Statement:** A cloud block-storage volume receives requests at a rate of $\lambda = 90$ requests/sec. Each request takes on average $1/\mu = 10$ ms to service. Compute the mean response time $W$ and the server utilization $\rho$.

### Step 1 — Identify parameters
- $\lambda = 90$ req/s
- Service time $S = 1/\mu = 10 \text{ ms} = 0.01 \text{ s}$
- Therefore, $\mu = 100$ req/s

### Step 2 — Compute utilization
$$
\rho = \frac{\lambda}{\mu} = \frac{90}{100} = 0.9
$$

> The storage controller is busy 90% of the time — saturation is near.

### Step 3 — Apply M/M/1 mean response time formula
$$
W = \frac{1}{\mu - \lambda}
$$

### Step 4 — Substitute
$$
W = \frac{1}{100 - 90} = \frac{1}{10} = 0.1 \text{ seconds} = 100 \text{ ms}
$$

### Step 5 — Interpretation
The average tenant request will wait **100 ms** in total (service + queue). Compare with $\lambda = 50$:
$$
W_{50} = \frac{1}{100 - 50} = \frac{1}{50} = 20 \text{ ms}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** Halving the arrival rate cuts the response time by **5×** — classic non-linear queueing behavior. This is why cloud SLAs quote performance under **specific load thresholds**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Monthly Cost of Object Storage

**Problem Statement:** A tenant stores **500 GB** in an S3-class object store for one month, performs **2,000,000 GET requests**, and transfers **3 TB** out to the internet. Given:
- Storage price: $P_s = \$0.023$ per GB-month
- Request price: $P_r = \$0.0004$ per 1,000 GETs
- Egress price: $P_t = \$0.09$ per GB

Compute the total monthly bill.

### Step 1 — Storage cost
$$
C_{storage} = 500 \text{ GB} \times \$0.023/\text{GB} = \$11.50
$$

### Step 2 — Request cost
$$
C_{req} = \frac{2{,}000{,}000}{1000} \times \$0.0004 = 2000 \times 0.0004 = \$0.80
$$

### Step 3 — Egress (transfer) cost
$$
C_{egress} = 3 \text{ TB} \times 1024 \text{ GB/TB} \times \$0.09/\text{GB}
$$

$$
C_{egress} = 3072 \times 0.09 = \$276.48
$$

### Step 4 — Total
$$
C_{total} = C_{storage} + C_{req} + C_{egress}
$$

$$
C_{total} = 11.50 + 0.80 + 276.48 = \$288.78
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Trap:** Students often forget to convert TB → GB for egress, or skip the request cost. The **egress (data transfer OUT)** is typically the largest cost component — not storage.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Thin vs Thick Provisioning Savings

**Problem Statement:** A tenant requests a **1 TB** volume. Actual usage after 30 days is **250 GB**. Compare:
- (a) Thick-provisioned cost at $0.10/GB-month.
- (b) Thin-provisioned cost (charged on **used** capacity).

### Step 1 — Thick cost
$$
C_{thick} = 1024 \text{ GB} \times \$0.10 = \$102.40
$$

### Step 2 — Thin cost
$$
C_{thin} = 250 \text{ GB} \times \$0.10 = \$25.00
$$

### Step 3 — Savings
$$
\text{Savings} = C_{thick} - C_{thin} = \$77.40
$$

$$
\text{Savings \%} = \frac{77.40}{102.40} \times 100 = 75.59\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Trade-off:** Thin provisioning saves money for tenants but **risks over-subscription** for the provider — the pool must be carefully monitored.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Provisioning Model to Choose?

| Workload Profile | Best Model | Justification | Risk |
|---|---|---|---|
| Relational DB (predictable I/O) | Thick + Provisioned IOPS | Stable latency, no contention | Over-provisioning cost |
| Log archive (bursty writes) | Thin + Cold tier | Pay only for written data | Slow first-byte |
| Analytics (Hadoop) | Erasure-coded HDFS | Cheap, fault-tolerant | Higher rebuild cost |
| Web app assets (CDN-backed) | Object + Lifecycle policy | Auto-tier to cold | Retrieval latency |
| VM boot volume | Thick (preallocated) | Predictable boot time | Wasted space |
| Container ephemeral volume | JIT / dynamic | Auto-cleanup | Cold start |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Cloud Storage Provisioning Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Tn[Tenant Portal or REST API] --> Req[Request Ingestion Layer]
    Req --> Auth[Authentication and ACL Check]
    Auth --> Adm[Admission Control and Quota Check]
    Adm --> Plcy[Policy Engine SLAs and Tiers]
    Plcy --> Alloc[Logical Resource Allocator]
    Alloc --> VDisk[Virtual Disk VHD VMDK]
    Alloc --> Obj[Object Bucket S3 compatible]
    Alloc --> LUN[Block LUN iSCSI FC]
    VDisk --> Phy[Physical Placement Engine]
    Obj --> Phy
    LUN --> Phy
    Phy --> Pool1[Storage Pool SSD Hot Tier]
    Phy --> Pool2[Storage Pool HDD Cool Tier]
    Phy --> Pool3[Object Store Cold Archive]
    Pool1 --> Prot[Data Protection RAID EC Replication]
    Pool2 --> Prot
    Pool3 --> Prot
    Prot --> Mtr[Metering and Billing Engine]
    Prot --> Mon[Monitoring IOPS Latency Capacity]
    Mon --> Auto[Auto Scaling and Tier Migrator]
    Auto --> Pool1
    Auto --> Pool2
    Auto --> Pool3
    Mtr --> Bill[Monthly Bill Generation]
    Mon --> Alert[Alerts and Thresholds]
```

## 4.2 Provisioning Workflow — State Transition View

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Requested
    Requested --> Validated: Policy check passed
    Validated --> Allocated: Resources reserved in pool
    Allocated --> Active: Mounted and exposed to tenant
    Active --> Scaling: Threshold breached
    Scaling --> Active: Resized
    Active --> TierChanged: Lifecycle rule fired
    TierChanged --> Active: Moved across tier
    Active --> Snapshotted: Snapshot policy triggered
    Snapshotted --> Active
    Active --> Deprovisioned: Tenant termination
    Deprovisioned --> SecurelyWiped: Crypto shred
    SecurelyWiped --> [*]
    Validated --> Rejected: Quota exceeded
    Rejected --> [*]
```

## 4.3 Storage Tiering — Cost vs Access Latency Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Tenant Writes Data] --> B{Access Frequency}
    B -- Hot --> C[Standard SSD Tier]
    B -- Warm --> D[Standard HDD Tier]
    B -- Cold --> E[Infrequent Access Tier]
    B -- Archive --> F[Glacier Cold Archive]
    C --> G[Lifecycle Policy 30 days idle]
    G --> D
    D --> H[Lifecycle Policy 90 days idle]
    H --> E
    E --> I[Lifecycle Policy 180 days idle]
    I --> F
```

## 4.4 Thin vs Thick Provisioning — Allocation View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Thick[Thick Provisioning]
        T1[Provisioned 1 TB] --> T2[Allocated 1 TB upfront]
        T2 --> T3[Used 250 GB]
        T3 --> T4[Wasted 750 GB]
    end
    subgraph Thin[Thin Provisioning]
        Th1[Provisioned 1 TB] --> Th2[Allocated 250 GB on demand]
        Th2 --> Th3[Used 250 GB]
        Th3 --> Th4[Free 750 GB for others]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
**Define cloud storage provisioning. Differentiate between thick and thin provisioning with an example.**

**Model Answer (≈ 12–14 lines):**

Cloud storage provisioning is the process of allocating, configuring, and exposing storage resources (capacity, tier, IOPS) to cloud tenants on demand, governed by SLAs and policies.

| Aspect | Thick Provisioning | Thin Provisioning |
|---|---|---|
| **When allocated** | Upfront, at creation | On first write (lazy) |
| **Capacity shown to tenant** | Full requested | Only what is written |
| **Storage efficiency for provider** | Low (idle blocks) | High (over-subscription) |
| **Example** | AWS EBS `gp2` pre-allocated | AWS EBS `st1` (lazy) or thin LUNs in vSphere |

**Use case:** Thick → production databases needing guaranteed IOPS; Thin → dev/test, log volumes.

> [!TIP]
> Examiner awards 1 mark for the definition, 1.5 marks for the comparison, 0.5 for the example.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**
**List any three storage performance metrics and explain the meaning of "Provisioned IOPS".**

**Model Answer:**

1. **IOPS** — I/O Operations Per Second; number of read/write operations completed per second.
2. **Throughput** — Data transferred per second, measured in **MB/s** or **GB/s**.
3. **Latency** — Time taken to complete a single I/O, in **milliseconds (ms)**.

**Provisioned IOPS** is a cloud storage pricing/performance model where the tenant **reserves a guaranteed minimum I/O rate** (e.g., 4,000 IOPS) at an additional cost. It ensures that the volume can sustain a deterministic number of I/O requests per second, regardless of contention from other tenants. AWS EBS `io1/io2`, Azure Ultra Disks, and GCP PD-Extreme support this model.

> [!TIP]
> Always quote the **unit (IOPS, MB/s, ms)** — losing the unit loses a mark.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **architecture of a cloud storage provisioning system**. List the responsibilities of each layer. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A cloud storage pool contains **10 disks of 3 TB each**, configured as **RAID 5**. A tenant provisions a volume of **8 TB** on this pool. Compute:
  - (i) Total usable capacity of the pool.
  - (ii) Storage efficiency.
  - (iii) Fault tolerance capability.
  - (iv) Whether the request can be fulfilled.
**(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### Part (a) — Architecture Explanation

The architecture of a cloud storage provisioning system consists of **five functional layers**:

1. **Request Ingestion Layer** — Receives tenant requests via Web Portal, CLI, or REST API. Validates authentication and authorization (RBAC).
2. **Policy & Admission Control Layer** — Enforces tenant quotas, SLA tiers, region constraints, and rejects overcommitment.
3. **Logical Allocation Layer** — Creates a **virtual disk / object bucket / LUN** and binds it to a tenant namespace.
4. **Physical Placement & Protection Layer** — Maps logical volumes to physical disks; applies **RAID, replication, or erasure coding**; decides datacenter/zone.
5. **Metering, Monitoring & Lifecycle Layer** — Tracks usage, IOPS, capacity; triggers **auto-scaling, tiering, snapshots, and de-provisioning**.

*(Draw the architecture diagram from Section 4.1 — 4 marks for diagram, 3 marks for explanation.)*

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Given:** $N = 10$, $D = 3 \text{ TB}$, RAID = 5, requested volume = **8 TB**.

**(i) Total usable capacity (RAID 5):**

$$
C_{5} = (N - 1) \times D = (10 - 1) \times 3 = 27 \text{ TB}
$$

**(ii) Storage efficiency:**

$$
\eta = \frac{N - 1}{N} = \frac{9}{10} = 0.90 \;(90\%)
$$

**(iii) Fault tolerance:** RAID 5 tolerates **1 disk failure** without data loss. A second concurrent failure causes data loss.

**(iv) Can the request be fulfilled?**
Pool has **27 TB usable**, tenant asks for **8 TB** → **Yes, request is valid**. (Assuming no other tenants occupy the pool.)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes (Examiner's Pitfall):**
> 1. Writing RAID 5 capacity as $N \times D$ — **wrong;** must subtract **1 disk** for parity.
> 2. Saying RAID 5 tolerates 2 disk failures — **wrong;** that's RAID 6.
> 3. Forgetting to compare requested vs available capacity in part (iv).
> 4. Missing units (TB) in the final answer — **−0.5 mark** per instance.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** — **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** Compare **thick, thin, and dynamic provisioning** models. State two scenarios each where they are best suited. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A cloud object storage service is used by a tenant as follows:
  - Data stored: **750 GB**
  - GET requests: **1,500,000**
  - Data transferred out: **2 TB**
  - Pricing: $P_s = \$0.021$/GB-month, $P_r = \$0.0005$/1000 GETs, $P_t = \$0.085$/GB egress.

Compute the **total monthly bill** and identify the **largest cost component**. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### Part (a) — Provisioning Models Comparison

| Feature | Thick | Thin | Dynamic |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Allocated upfront** | Full | None | Variable |
| **Visibility to tenant** | Full capacity | Used only | Auto-grown |
| **Suitable for** | Production DBs, boot volumes | Dev/test, logs | Analytics, auto-scaling clusters |
| **Provider risk** | Wasted capacity | Over-subscription | Rebalancing cost |
| **Latency on first I/O** | None | Slight | Moderate |

**Scenarios:**
- **Thick** → SQL Server production DB; Oracle RAC.
- **Thin** → Jenkins build workspace; container scratch storage.
- **Dynamic** → Spark/Hadoop scratch space; auto-scaling Kubernetes PVCs.

---

#### Part (b) — Cost Calculation

**Storage cost:**
$$
C_s = 750 \times 0.021 = \$15.75
$$

**Request cost:**
$$
C_r = \frac{1{,}500{,}000}{1000} \times 0.0005 = 1500 \times 0.0005 = \$0.75
$$

**Egress cost:**
$$
C_t = 2 \text{ TB} \times 1024 \text{ GB/TB} \times 0.085 = 2048 \times 0.085 = \$174.08
$$

**Total bill:**
$$
C_{total} = 15.75 + 0.75 + 174.08 = \$190.58
$$

**Largest cost component:** **Egress (data transfer out) at $174.08 (≈ 91% of total).**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Question B:**
> - Forgetting to convert **2 TB → 2048 GB** → loses **2 marks**.
> - Listing storage as the largest cost when egress dominates → loses **0.5 mark**.
> - Not writing the **unit ($)** consistently.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize these before entering the exam hall:**

- ✅ **Provisioning** = lifecycle management of storage: request → allocate → protect → meter → de-provision.
- ✅ **Thick vs Thin**: Thick = upfront full; Thin = lazy, on-write.
- ✅ **RAID capacity formulas**: RAID 0 → $N \times D$; RAID 1 → $(N \times D)/2$; RAID 5 → $(N-1) \times D$; RAID 6 → $(N-2) \times D$.
- ✅ **Erasure coding** uses $k$ data + $m$ parity shards; cheaper than 3× replication at scale.
- ✅ **M/M/1 mean response time** $W = 1/(\mu - \lambda)$ — drives SLA latency guarantees.
- ✅ **Object storage cost = Storage + Requests + Egress** — egress is the silent killer.
- ✅ **Five-Nines (99.999%)** = ~5.26 min downtime/year.
- ✅ **Storage tiers**: Hot (SSD, ms latency) → Warm (HDD) → Cold (IA) → Archive (Glacier, hours).
- ✅ **Lifecycle policies** automatically move data across tiers based on access age.
- ✅ **Multi-tenancy** is achieved via namespaces, ACLs, and per-tenant encryption keys.
- ✅ **Thin provisioning savings** can exceed **70%** for under-utilized workloads.
- ✅ **Provisioned IOPS** is a guaranteed throughput/IOPS contract, billed separately.
- ✅ **Auto-scaling triggers**: capacity threshold, IOPS saturation, scheduled policy.
- ✅ **De-provisioning must include secure wiping** (crypto-shred / NIST 800-88 purge).
- ✅ **Geo-replication** with RF = 3 across AZs is the default for high-availability buckets.

> **Final Tip:** In KTU exams, always **draw the architecture diagram** for full-mark answers in Part B. A well-labeled diagram fetches **3–4 marks** even if the explanation is partial.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Managed and Unmanaged Cloud Storage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT634 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Managed and Unmanaged Cloud Storage

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Managed and Unmanaged Cloud Storage

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Cloud Storage Models):**
> Cloud storage is a cloud computing model that stores data on the Internet through a cloud computing provider who manages and operates data storage as a service. It is delivered on demand with **capacity**, **throughput**, **latency**, and **durability** guarantees. Cloud storage is broadly classified into **Managed Cloud Storage** and **Unmanaged Cloud Storage** based on the division of operational responsibility between the cloud provider and the consumer.

### 1.1 Managed Cloud Storage

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Managed Cloud Storage** is a storage service where the cloud service provider (CSP) takes complete responsibility for the underlying infrastructure, operating system, virtualization, maintenance, patching, backups, replication, and scaling of the storage system. The consumer is responsible only for **storing data, configuring access policies, and managing application-level usage**.

**Formal Definition (KTU/Industry Standard):**
Managed cloud storage is a *Platform-as-a-Service (PaaS)* or *Software-as-a-Service (SaaS)* style storage offering where the provider abstracts the entire storage stack — physical disks, RAID arrays, hypervisors, object/file/block protocols, durability mechanisms, and software updates — and exposes only a clean API or console to the developer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Managed Storage Services:**
> - **AWS:** S3 (Object), EBS (Block), Glacier (Archive)
> - **Microsoft Azure:** Blob Storage, File Storage, Queue Storage
> - **Google Cloud:** Cloud Storage (GCS), Persistent Disk, Filestore
> - **IBM Cloud:** Cloud Object Storage

### 1.2 Unmanaged Cloud Storage

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unmanaged Cloud Storage** (also called *raw* or *IaaS-style* storage) refers to storage provisioned through Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS), where the cloud provider only supplies the raw storage hardware/virtual disks. The consumer must install, configure, patch, secure, back up, and scale the operating system, file system, and storage software themselves.

**Formal Definition (KTU/Industry Standard):**
Unmanaged cloud storage is a storage model where the provider offers raw block devices or virtual machines and leaves the operational burden — such as filesystem tuning, RAID configuration, OS hardening, replication, and snapshot scheduling — to the user. The user essentially "owns" the storage stack similar to an on-premises data center setup.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy (Restaurant):**
> - **Managed Cloud Storage** is like ordering a meal at a fine-dining restaurant. You just order the dish (e.g., "store 1 TB of video files"), and the kitchen (cloud provider) handles everything: ingredients (disks), cooking (replication), plating (API responses), and even cleaning up (patching). You only consume the meal.
> - **Unmanaged Cloud Storage** is like renting a commercial kitchen. You pay for the space, gas, and stove, but YOU must buy ingredients, cook, plate, and clean. You have total control, but total responsibility.
> - **On-Premises Storage** is like owning a private kitchen at home — you built it, you maintain it, and you bear all capital and operational expenses.

### 1.4 Key Standard Metrics (Highlighted in Bold)

- **Durability:** `99.999999999%` (eleven 9s) — e.g., AWS S3 Standard
- **Availability:** `99.9%` to `99.99%` — depending on storage class
- **Latency:** Milliseconds (first-byte), microseconds (caching)
- **Elasticity:** Auto-scaling in GB to **EB (Exabyte)** range
- **Replication Factor:** Typically **3x** across Availability Zones

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of Managed Cloud Storage

### 2.1.1 Operational Stack (Layered View)

The managed storage stack consists of the following layers, **all managed by the CSP** except the topmost data layer:

1. **Physical Layer:** SSD, HDD, NVMe disks in hyperscale data centers
2. **Infrastructure Layer:** RAID, erasure coding, replication engines
3. **Virtualization Layer:** Hypervisors (Xen, KVM), containers, multi-tenancy isolation
4. **Storage Engine Layer:** Object/File/Block protocol handlers (e.g., S3 API, NFS, iSCSI)
5. **Management Plane:** Auto-scaling, monitoring, patching, security
6. **Data Plane (User-controlled):** The actual user data and access policies

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 'Managed'?**
> Because the consumer interacts ONLY with the data plane and management APIs. Everything beneath is the provider's operational concern (the "undifferentiated heavy lifting").

### 2.1.2 Why Choose Managed Storage? (Operational Benefits)

- **Zero OS administration** — no patching, no kernel upgrades
- **Built-in high availability** — provider guarantees **99.99%** uptime SLAs
- **Automatic scaling** — pay-per-GB, no capacity planning
- **Integrated security** — encryption at rest (AES-256) and in transit (TLS 1.2+)
- **Cross-region replication** — disaster recovery out-of-the-box
- **Versioning & lifecycle policies** — automated tiering to cold storage

## 2.2 Architecture of Unmanaged Cloud Storage

### 2.2.1 Operational Stack (Layered View)

In unmanaged storage, the user controls layers 2 through 5:

1. **Physical Layer:** CSP-owned (you don't see it)
2. **Infrastructure Layer:** **User-configured** (RAID 0/1/5/6, LVM, ZFS, etc.)
3. **Operating System Layer:** **User-managed** (Linux/Windows patching)
4. **File System Layer:** **User-tuned** (ext4, XFS, NTFS)
5. **Application/Service Layer:** User's database, web server, etc.
6. **Data Layer:** User's files

### 2.2.2 Why Choose Unmanaged Storage? (Strategic Benefits)

- **Full root/admin access** — install any software, custom kernel modules
- **Custom file systems** — ZFS, Btrfs, GlusterFS, Ceph
- **Specialized workloads** — high-performance computing (HPC), legacy apps
- **Compliance control** — bring your own encryption keys (BYOK), air-gapped configurations
- **Predictable cost** — fixed monthly billing, no per-operation charges

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Comparison Table)

| **Parameter** | **Managed Cloud Storage** | **Unmanaged Cloud Storage** |
|---|---|---|
| **Service Model** | PaaS / SaaS style | IaaS style |
| **Provider Responsibility** | Full stack (hardware → application) | Hardware + Hypervisor only |
| **User Responsibility** | Data + Access policies | OS, FS, Patching, Backups |
| **Control Level** | Low (API-based) | High (root/admin access) |
| **Operational Overhead** | Very Low | High |
| **Cost Model** | Pay-per-GB + per-request | Pay-per-allocated instance/GB |
| **Scaling** | Automatic, elastic | Manual or scripted |
| **Availability SLA** | 99.9% – 99.99% | Depends on user configuration |
| **Durability** | `99.999999999%` (11 nines) | Depends on user's RAID/replication |
| **Security** | Built-in, provider-managed | User-implemented |
| **Setup Time** | Minutes (console click) | Hours to days |
| **Best For** | Modern apps, startups, serverless | Legacy apps, HPC, custom stacks |
| **Examples** | AWS S3, Azure Blob, GCS | AWS EC2 + EBS, Azure VM + Managed Disk |
| **Failure Recovery** | Provider handles | User handles (snapshots, replicas) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Quick-Check Formula:**
> $$\text{Responsibility}_{\text{user}} = f(\text{Service Model})$$
> $$\text{SaaS} \rightarrow \text{Data only} \quad ; \quad \text{PaaS} \rightarrow \text{Data + App} \quad ; \quad \text{IaaS} \rightarrow \text{Data + App + OS + FS}$$

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Startups** use managed storage to ship products in days, not months.
- **Banking/Healthcare** often use unmanaged storage with on-prem-like control for **regulatory compliance (HIPAA, GDPR, RBI)**.
- **AI/ML pipelines** prefer managed object storage (e.g., S3) for massive datasets due to **parallel throughput** via multipart uploads.
- **HPC clusters** use unmanaged parallel file systems (Lustre, BeeGFS) for low-latency I/O at scale.

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of Thumb (KTU Board Favorite):**
> *"If you can describe your workload as 'just store and retrieve files', pick managed. If you must say 'I need to install X software on the storage server', pick unmanaged."*

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown

## 3.1 Cost-Benefit Analytical Framework

Let us derive the **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** for both models over a horizon of $T$ years.

### 3.1.1 TCO for Managed Cloud Storage

Let:
- $D$ = Total data stored (in GB)
- $r_m$ = Per-GB monthly storage rate (in USD/GB/month)
- $R$ = Total read/write requests per month
- $c_r$ = Cost per 1,000 requests (in USD)
- $E_m$ = Per-GB monthly egress (data transfer out) cost

The **monthly cost** is:
$$C_{m,\text{monthly}} = (D \cdot r_m) + \left( \frac{R}{1000} \cdot c_r \right) + (E_m \cdot \text{egress rate})$$

The **TCO over $T$ years** is:
$$C_{m,\text{TCO}} = 12 \cdot T \cdot C_{m,\text{monthly}}$$

> **Explanation of step:** Storage cost is a linear function of data and request volume. We multiply by 12 to convert months to years, then by $T$ for the full time horizon. The 1000-divisor for $R$ accounts for industry billing granularity (most CSPs bill per 1,000 requests).

### 3.1.2 TCO for Unmanaged Cloud Storage

Let:
- $I$ = Hourly instance rate (USD/hour)
- $h$ = Hours per month (typically $h = 730$)
- $S_u$ = Allocated storage size (GB)
- $r_u$ = Per-GB-month provisioned-disk rate

The **monthly cost** is:
$$C_{u,\text{monthly}} = (I \cdot h) + (S_u \cdot r_u)$$

The **TCO over $T$ years** is:
$$C_{u,\text{TCO}} = 12 \cdot T \cdot C_{u,\text{monthly}} + C_{\text{ops}}$$

where $C_{\text{ops}}$ is the additional operational cost (DevOps engineer salaries, monitoring tools, etc.) for the unmanaged stack.

> **Explanation of step:** Unmanaged storage is dominated by *provisioned* instance hours — you pay even when idle. The $C_{\text{ops}}$ term reflects the human cost of patching, scaling, and backup management, which is absent in the managed model.

### 3.1.3 Break-Even Analysis

The break-even point is where both TCOs are equal:
$$12 \cdot T \cdot C_{m,\text{monthly}} = 12 \cdot T \cdot C_{u,\text{monthly}} + C_{\text{ops}}$$

Solving for $T$:
$$T = \frac{C_{\text{ops}}}{12 \cdot (C_{m,\text{monthly}} - C_{u,\text{monthly}})}$$

> **Interpretation:** If $C_{m,\text{monthly}} > C_{u,\text{monthly}}$, the denominator is negative, meaning **managed is always more expensive in raw compute terms**, and break-even is reached only when operational savings outweigh the higher per-GB rate. Conversely, if your data grows exponentially, managed's pay-as-you-go wins long-term.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam-Style)

> **Problem:** A startup stores **500 GB** of user-uploaded images on AWS S3. They make **2 million GET requests** and **500,000 PUT requests** per month. Egress is **200 GB/month**.
> - S3 Standard: $0.023 per GB-month, $0.0004 per 1,000 GET, $0.005 per 1,000 PUT
> - Self-managed EC2 (m5.large, $0.096/hr) + 500 GB EBS gp3 ($0.08/GB-month)
> - DevOps cost: $1,000/month (1 part-time engineer)
>
> Compare managed vs unmanaged over **1 year**.

**Step 1 — Managed Cost (S3):**
$$C_{m,\text{monthly}} = (500 \cdot 0.023) + \left( \frac{2{,}000{,}000}{1000} \cdot 0.0004 \right) + \left( \frac{500{,}000}{1000} \cdot 0.005 \right)$$
$$C_{m,\text{monthly}} = 11.50 + 0.80 + 2.50 = 14.80 \text{ USD/month}$$

(Egress excluded for simplicity; in exam, mention as $0.09/GB × 200 = \$18$, making total $\approx \$32.80$.)

**Step 2 — Unmanaged Cost (EC2 + EBS):**
$$C_{u,\text{monthly}} = (0.096 \cdot 730) + (500 \cdot 0.08) + 1000$$
$$C_{u,\text{monthly}} = 70.08 + 40.00 + 1000.00 = 1110.08 \text{ USD/month}$$

**Step 3 — TCO over 1 year (excluding egress):**
$$C_{m,\text{TCO}} = 12 \cdot 14.80 = 177.60 \text{ USD}$$
$$C_{u,\text{TCO}} = 12 \cdot 1110.08 = 13320.96 \text{ USD}$$

> **Conclusion:** Managed storage is **~75x cheaper** for this scenario because the workload is light and predictable, and operational overhead dominates unmanaged cost. **The break-even is negative** — unmanaged is never justified here.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Strategy:** Whenever a KTU question asks "which model is suitable?", compute the workload's **request-to-storage ratio** $R/D$. If $R/D > 1$ with low data, managed wins. If $R/D < 0.001$ with stable, large data, unmanaged may win.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Managed Cloud Storage

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["USER APPLICATION LAYER"] --> B["REST API GATEWAY"]
    B --> C["AUTHENTICATION AND IAM POLICY ENGINE"]
    C --> D["OBJECT STORAGE SERVICE LAYER"]
    D --> E["REPLICATION AND DURABILITY MODULE"]
    D --> F["LIFECYCLE AND TIERING POLICY"]
    D --> G["ENCRYPTION AT REST MODULE"]
    E --> H["AVAILABILITY ZONE 1 PHYSICAL DISK POOL"]
    E --> I["AVAILABILITY ZONE 2 PHYSICAL DISK POOL"]
    E --> J["AVAILABILITY ZONE 3 PHYSICAL DISK POOL"]
    F --> K["HOT TIER SSD"]
    F --> L["WARM TIER HDD"]
    F --> M["COLD TIER GLACIER ARCHIVE"]
    G --> N["KMS KEY MANAGEMENT"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** In managed storage, the user only touches the top (Application + API + IAM). The provider owns replication, tiering, encryption, and physical infrastructure across multiple availability zones — all transparent to the consumer.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Unmanaged Cloud Storage

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["USER APPLICATION LAYER"] --> B["USER CONFIGURED FILE SYSTEM EXT4 XFS ZFS"]
    B --> C["USER MANAGED OPERATING SYSTEM LINUX WINDOWS"]
    C --> D["HYPERVISOR PROVIDED BY CSP KVM XEN"]
    D --> E["VIRTUAL BLOCK DEVICE EBS MANAGED DISK"]
    E --> F["PHYSICAL DISK POOL CSP OWNED"]
    G["USER SIDE SCRIPTS"] --> H["BACKUP AND SNAPSHOT JOBS CRON"]
    G --> I["RAID AND LVM CONFIGURATION"]
    G --> J["OS PATCHING AND SECURITY"]
    H --> E
    I --> E
    J --> C
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** In unmanaged storage, the user is responsible for the **OS, file system, backup, RAID/LVM, and patching layers** (shown in red-highlighted path on the right). The provider only owns the hypervisor and physical disks. This gives freedom but multiplies operational burden.

## 4.3 Decision Flowchart — Choosing Between Managed and Unmanaged

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["START: Need Cloud Storage"] --> Q1{"Workload is unpredictable or spiky?"}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{"Need OS-level control or custom FS?"}
    Q1 -- No --> Q3{"Legacy software or specific compliance?"}
    Q2 -- No --> Managed["USE MANAGED STORAGE S3 Azure Blob GCS"]
    Q2 -- Yes --> Unmanaged["USE UNMANAGED STORAGE EC2 plus EBS Azure VM"]
    Q3 -- Yes --> Unmanaged
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{"Team size below 5 engineers?"}
    Q4 -- Yes --> Managed
    Q4 -- No --> Q5{"Cost sensitive long term?"}
    Q5 -- Yes --> Unmanaged
    Q5 -- No --> Managed
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Trick:** Many students lose marks by saying "managed is always better" or "unmanaged is always cheaper". The correct answer is **workload-dependent**. Always cite the decision criteria (elasticity, control, ops cost).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define managed cloud storage. List any two examples.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Managed cloud storage is a cloud storage model in which the service provider is responsible for managing the entire storage infrastructure, including hardware, software, patching, backups, replication, and scaling, while the user is responsible only for the data and access configuration. **(2 marks)**
> **Examples:** AWS S3, Microsoft Azure Blob Storage, Google Cloud Storage. **(1 mark for any two)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Compare managed and unmanaged cloud storage on the basis of (i) control and (ii) operational overhead.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **(i) Control:** Managed storage offers limited control, exposing only APIs and policy configurations. Unmanaged storage provides full root/admin control over the OS, file system, and storage software. **(1.5 marks)**
> **(ii) Operational Overhead:** Managed storage has very low operational overhead since the provider handles patching, scaling, and backups. Unmanaged storage carries high operational overhead as the user must perform all these tasks. **(1.5 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **Note:** Per KTU ESE 2024 regulations, Part B Module 3 question carries **14 marks** with internal choice (either Q. A or Q. B). Each question has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – July 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of managed cloud storage with a neat block diagram. Discuss its key features and benefits. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss unmanaged cloud storage. When is it preferred over managed storage? Provide two real-world scenarios. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Architecture of Managed Cloud Storage (7 marks):**
Managed cloud storage follows a layered architecture where the cloud provider manages all infrastructure layers:
1. **Physical Layer:** Hyperscale data centers with SSD/HDD/NVMe storage media.
2. **Storage Engine Layer:** Implements object, block, or file protocols (e.g., S3 API, NFS).
3. **Management Plane:** Handles auto-scaling, replication, encryption (AES-256), and lifecycle policies.
4. **Data Plane:** The user-facing layer where consumers upload, retrieve, and manage data.

**Key Features:** **(3 marks)**
- **Eleven nines durability** (99.999999999%) — data is replicated across **3+ Availability Zones**.
- **Elastic scaling** — storage grows from GB to PB without user intervention.
- **Built-in security** — IAM-based access, KMS-managed encryption keys.
- **Lifecycle policies** — automatic tiering from hot (S3 Standard) to cold (Glacier).

**Benefits:** **(2 marks)**
- Zero operational overhead, predictable pricing, faster time-to-market, integrated disaster recovery.

> (Block diagram to be drawn in exam — refer to Section 4.1 of these notes.)

**(b) Unmanaged Cloud Storage and Its Use Cases (7 marks):**
Unmanaged cloud storage is provided through IaaS (e.g., AWS EC2 with EBS volumes) where the consumer controls the OS, file system, and storage software.

**Why preferred over managed storage (4 marks):**
1. **Full OS-level customization** — required for installing proprietary database engines (e.g., Oracle RAC) or custom kernels.
2. **Compliance and data sovereignty** — financial institutions can enforce strict access controls and bring their own encryption keys.
3. **Predictable cost for stable workloads** — fixed monthly billing suits steady-state, high-throughput workloads.
4. **Specialized file systems** — HPC clusters require parallel file systems (Lustre, GPFS) not offered in managed tiers.

**Real-world scenarios (3 marks):**
- **Banking core systems:** Running legacy DB2 or Oracle on a managed IaaS VM to retain full OS audit trails for RBI compliance.
- **Genomics research labs:** Using Lustre on raw EC2 instances for petabyte-scale DNA sequencing where I/O throughput exceeds managed tiers.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Compare and contrast managed and unmanaged cloud storage. Which one is suitable for a startup building a photo-sharing mobile app? Justify your answer with cost and operational reasoning. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A company is evaluating cloud storage for 50 TB of archival logs that are written once and read rarely. Compare the cost of storing this in (i) AWS S3 Standard vs (ii) AWS S3 Glacier Deep Archive over 1 year, and recommend the appropriate storage class. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison and Startup Use Case (7 marks):**
Comparison table (3 marks) — covering control, cost, scaling, security, operational overhead, examples.

**Startup recommendation: Managed cloud storage (3 marks).**
- **Operational reasoning:** A small startup team (typically 2–5 engineers) cannot afford a dedicated DevOps engineer. Managed storage eliminates patching, backup, and scaling tasks, freeing engineers to focus on the mobile app.
- **Cost reasoning:** With a photo-sharing app, traffic is unpredictable (viral spikes). Managed storage's pay-per-GB and pay-per-request model aligns cost with revenue, unlike unmanaged's always-on instance billing.
- **Time-to-market:** Storage can be set up in minutes, allowing MVP launch in weeks.

**(b) Cost Calculation for Archival Storage (7 marks):**
Given: 50 TB = 50,000 GB, 1 year = 12 months.

**(i) AWS S3 Standard (1 mark):**
- Rate: $0.023 per GB-month
- Monthly cost: $50{,}000 \times 0.023 = \$1{,}150$
- Annual cost: $1{,}150 \times 12 = \$13{,}800$

**(ii) AWS S3 Glacier Deep Archive (1 mark):**
- Rate: $0.00099 per GB-month
- Monthly cost: $50{,}000 \times 0.00099 = \$49.50$
- Annual cost: $49.50 \times 12 = \$594$

**Recommendation (5 marks):**
- **Choose S3 Glacier Deep Archive** because the workload is "write once, read rarely" — exactly the use case for cold storage. The annual cost is **~23x cheaper** ($594 vs $13,800), saving **$13,206** per year.
- **Additional justification:** Glacier Deep Archive offers the same 11-nines durability as S3 Standard, so there is **no durability trade-off** — only retrieval latency trade-off (12 hours standard retrieval), which is acceptable for archival logs.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (KTU Board Patterns):**
> 1. **Confusing 'managed' with 'unmanaged' in definitions** — Always start with the responsibility split (what the provider does vs. what the user does).
> 2. **Forgetting units in cost calculations** — Always write `GB-month` or `per 1,000 requests`. Marks are deducted for ambiguous units.
> 3. **Drawing flowcharts instead of block diagrams** — KTU expects layered architectural diagrams for storage questions, not process flowcharts.
> 4. **Saying "managed is always better"** — This is a fatal answer. Always qualify with workload characteristics (size, frequency, control needs).
> 5. **Missing durability SLA** — When listing features, the phrase **"11 nines durability"** is a guaranteed 1-mark bonus in managed storage answers.
> 6. **Calculating TCO without the operations term** — Always include $C_{\text{ops}}$ (DevOps salary) for unmanaged storage. Skipping it is a 2-mark loss.
> 7. **Spelling "Glacier" as "Glashier"** — Typos in service names are flagged by KTU evaluators.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Managed vs Unmanaged Cloud Storage**

### **Core Concepts (Must Memorize):**
- ✅ Managed storage = Provider handles everything except data and access policies
- ✅ Unmanaged storage = IaaS model; user handles OS, FS, backups, scaling
- ✅ Durability SLA: 99.999999999% (11 nines) for AWS S3 Standard
- ✅ Typical replication factor: 3x across Availability Zones
- ✅ Common managed examples: AWS S3, Azure Blob, Google Cloud Storage
- ✅ Common unmanaged examples: EC2 + EBS, Azure VM + Managed Disk

### **Comparison Pillars (Use in Every Answer):**
- ✅ **Control:** Managed (Low) vs Unmanaged (High)
- ✅ **Operational Overhead:** Managed (Low) vs Unmanaged (High)
- ✅ **Cost Model:** Managed (Pay-per-use) vs Unmanaged (Pay-provisioned)
- ✅ **Scaling:** Managed (Auto) vs Unmanaged (Manual/Scripted)
- ✅ **Setup Time:** Managed (Minutes) vs Unmanaged (Hours-Days)

### **Formulas to Remember:**
- ✅ $C_{m,\text{TCO}} = 12 \cdot T \cdot [D \cdot r_m + (R/1000) \cdot c_r + E_m \cdot \text{rate}]$
- ✅ $C_{u,\text{TCO}} = 12 \cdot T \cdot [I \cdot 730 + S_u \cdot r_u] + C_{\text{ops}}$
- ✅ Break-even: $T = \dfrac{C_{\text{ops}}}{12 \cdot (C_m - C_u)}$

### **Decision Rule (High-Yield):**
- ✅ **Unpredictable workload + small team** → Managed
- ✅ **Legacy software + compliance needs** → Unmanaged
- ✅ **Cold/archival data** → Managed Glacier tier
- ✅ **HPC + custom file systems** → Unmanaged parallel FS

### **Exam Buzzwords (Use in Answers for Bonus Marks):**
- ✅ *Eleven nines durability*
- ✅ *Pay-as-you-go elasticity*
- ✅ *Undifferentiated heavy lifting* (AWS term — impresses examiners)
- ✅ *Lifecycle policy and tiering*
- ✅ *Bring Your Own Key (BYOK)*
- ✅ *Cross-region replication for disaster recovery*

---

**End of Notes — Module 3: Managed and Unmanaged Cloud Storage**
**Total Marks Coverage:** Part A (2 × 3 = 6 marks) + Part B (1 × 14 = 14 marks) = **20 marks practice bank prepared.**

> **Best of luck for your KTU ESE 2024 examinations!**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cloud Security
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT634 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cloud Security

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Cloud Security** refers to the comprehensive set of policies, controls, procedures, and technologies that work jointly to protect cloud-based systems, data, and infrastructure from internal and external threats. As per the KTU 2024 Scheme for *PECDT634 - Cloud Computing*, it is formally defined as:

> **Cloud Security** is a discipline of cyber-security dedicated to securing cloud computing environments, applications, data, virtualized infrastructure, and services against vulnerabilities, unauthorized access, data breaches, and compliance violations. It is delivered as a combination of **Identity and Access Management (IAM)**, **data encryption**, **network security controls**, **security monitoring**, and **compliance governance**, forming an integrated layer above and within the cloud service stack (IaaS, PaaS, SaaS).

The KTU 2024 curriculum categorizes cloud security into three fundamental security requirements built upon the **CIA Triad**:

- **Confidentiality** — Ensuring that data is accessible *only* to authorized users.
- **Integrity** — Guaranteeing that data is *not altered or tampered* with during storage, transit, or processing.
- **Availability** — Ensuring that cloud services and data remain *accessible* when required by legitimate users.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CIA Triad** is the foundational security model universally tested in KTU examinations. Every security mechanism discussed in Module 3 directly maps to one or more pillars of the CIA Triad.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain English Intuition

Think of **Cloud Security** like securing a **massive apartment complex** (the cloud) where thousands of tenants (users) live:

| Cloud Component | Apartment Analogy | Security Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| Cloud Data | Tenant's personal belongings | Encrypted lockers |
| User Access | Apartment keys & ID cards | Identity & Access Management (IAM) |
| Network | Building corridors & elevators | Firewalls & Virtual Private Networks (VPN) |
| Cloud Provider | Building management | Shared Responsibility Model |
| Audit Logs | CCTV camera recordings | Security Monitoring & SIEM |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** When you store your photos in Google Drive, *you* are responsible for choosing a *strong password* and enabling *2-factor authentication*. Google (the provider) is responsible for securing the *data center* where your photos physically reside. This division of responsibility is called the **Shared Responsibility Model** — a key concept that appears repeatedly in KTU Part B questions.

## 1.3 Standard Security Metrics in Cloud Environments

The following standard metrics are universally applied in cloud security assessments and are **bold-marked for examiner emphasis**:

- **AES-256** — Advanced Encryption Standard with a 256-bit key length, considered the gold standard for data-at-rest encryption.
- **TLS 1.3** — Transport Layer Security protocol version 1.3, securing data-in-transit with Perfect Forward Secrecy (PFS).
- **RTO (Recovery Time Objective)** — Maximum tolerable downtime, typically measured in **minutes** for mission-critical cloud workloads.
- **RPO (Recovery Point Objective)** — Maximum acceptable data loss measured in time units (seconds to hours).
- **SLA Uptime** — Service Level Agreement availability commitment, typically **99.9% ("three nines")** or **99.99% ("four nines")**.
- **MFA (Multi-Factor Authentication)** — Authentication using 2 or more independent credentials (knowledge, possession, inherence).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SLA Uptime Quick Math for KTU:**
> - **99.9% uptime** ≈ **8.77 hours** of allowable downtime per year
> - **99.99% uptime** ≈ **52.6 minutes** of allowable downtime per year
> - **99.999% uptime** ("five nines") ≈ **5.26 minutes** of allowable downtime per year

---

## 1.4 KTU 2024 Learning Outcomes Mapped to This Module

By the end of this section, students should be able to:

1. **CO3 (Understand):** Explain the fundamental security challenges in cloud computing and their relation to the CIA Triad.
2. **CO3 (Apply):** Identify appropriate security controls for IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS service models.
3. **CO4 (Analyze):** Compare deployment models (Public, Private, Hybrid, Community) in terms of security trade-offs.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Shared Responsibility Model (SRM)

The **Shared Responsibility Model** delineates security ownership between the **Cloud Service Provider (CSP)** and the **Cloud Customer (Client)**. This is a *direct* KTU favorite question.

| Security Layer | IaaS Customer | PaaS Customer | SaaS Customer | CSP Responsibility |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|---|
| **Data** | ✔ Client | ✔ Client | ✔ Client | — |
| **Application** | ✔ Client | ✔ Client | — (managed) | ✔ (SaaS) |
| **Runtime / OS** | ✔ Client | — (managed) | — (managed) | ✔ |
| **Middleware** | ✔ Client | — (managed) | — (managed) | ✔ |
| **Virtualization** | — | — | — | ✔ CSP |
| **Servers / Storage** | — | — | — | ✔ CSP |
| **Networking** | — | — | — | ✔ CSP |
| **Physical Data Center** | — | — | — | ✔ CSP |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The customer is *always* responsible for **DATA security** and **IAM**, regardless of service model. The provider secures *the infrastructure underneath*.

## 2.2 Core Security Threats in Cloud (OWASP-aligned)

The Open Web Application Security Project (OWASP) and CSA (Cloud Security Alliance) identify the following top cloud security risks:

1. **Data Breaches** — Unauthorized access to sensitive data.
2. **Misconfiguration** — Incorrectly set storage buckets (e.g., AWS S3 left public).
3. **Insufficient Identity & Access Management** — Weak authentication leading to privilege escalation.
4. **Insecure APIs** — Cloud APIs (REST/SOAP) becoming attack vectors.
5. **Account Hijacking** — Phishing/credential theft of cloud accounts.
6. **Insider Threat** — Malicious or negligent employees.
7. **Advanced Persistent Threats (APTs)** — Long-term stealthy intrusions.
8. **Denial of Service (DoS / DDoS)** — Overwhelming the service.

## 2.3 Cryptographic Pillars (Core Mathematical Foundation)

### 2.3.1 Symmetric Encryption
A single shared key is used for both encryption and decryption. The complexity of brute-forcing a key of length $n$ bits is $2^n$.

**Formula: Brute-Force Search Space**

$$\text{Keyspace} = 2^{n} \quad \text{where } n = \text{key length in bits}$$

**Example:** AES-128 has $2^{128} \approx 3.4 \times 10^{38}$ possible keys.

### 2.3.2 Asymmetric Encryption
Uses a **public key** $K_{pub}$ for encryption and a **private key** $K_{priv}$ for decryption. Built on mathematical one-way functions like RSA and Elliptic Curve Cryptography (ECC).

**RSA Key Generation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Choose primes } & p, q \\
\text{Compute } n &= p \cdot q \\
\text{Euler's totient } & \phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1) \\
\text{Choose } e & \text{ such that } \gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1 \\
\text{Compute } d & \text{ such that } e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)} \\
\text{Public key: } & (n, e) \\
\text{Private key: } & (n, d)
\end{aligned}$$

**Encryption of plaintext $M$:**

$$C = M^{e} \mod n$$

**Decryption of ciphertext $C$:**

$$M = C^{d} \mod n$$

### 2.3.3 Hashing (One-Way Function)
A hash function $H$ maps data of arbitrary size to a fixed-size output. Properties: **deterministic**, **collision-resistant**, **pre-image resistant**.

**SHA-256 Output Length:** **256 bits** = 64 hexadecimal characters.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This table is the **single most important reference** for solving numerical/analytical KTU Part B questions on cloud security.

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Unit / Boundary | Real-world Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Keyspace (Symmetric) | $K = 2^{n}$ | $n$ = key bits | AES-256 → $2^{256}$ keys |
| 2 | RSA Modulus | $n = p \times q$ | $p, q$ prime | Public-key cryptography |
| 3 | Euler's Totient | $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$ | For RSA | Key derivation |
| 4 | RSA Encrypt | $C = M^{e} \mod n$ | $0 \le M < n$ | Secure transmission |
| 5 | RSA Decrypt | $M = C^{d} \mod n$ | — | Recovery of plaintext |
| 6 | Hash Output Size | $H(x) = h$ bits | Fixed for algorithm | SHA-256 → 256 bits |
| 7 | Hash Collision Prob (birthday) | $P \approx 1 - e^{-n^2 / 2N}$ | $n$ trials, $N$ space | Hash strength |
| 8 | SLA Annual Downtime | $D = (1 - U) \times 8760$ hrs | $U$ = uptime fraction | 99.9% → 8.77 hrs |
| 9 | Storage Overhead (Erasure Coding) | $E = \frac{k}{n}$ ratio | $k$ data, $n$ total | RAID-like in cloud |
| 10 | Shannon Entropy (Info) | $H = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ | bits per symbol | Password strength |

### 2.5 Identity & Access Management (IAM) Architecture

IAM operates on three core pillars:

1. **Authentication** — *"Who are you?"* — Verifying identity (password, biometrics, MFA).
2. **Authorization** — *"What can you do?"* — Granting permissions (RBAC, ABAC).
3. **Accounting / Auditing** — *"What did you do?"* — Logging activities (SIEM, audit trails).

**Access Control Models:**

- **DAC (Discretionary Access Control)** — Owner decides permissions.
- **MAC (Mandatory Access Control)** — System enforces labels (e.g., military classifications).
- **RBAC (Role-Based Access Control)** — Permissions based on job role.
- **ABAC (Attribute-Based Access Control)** — Permissions based on user/resource/environment attributes.

> [!NOTE]
> **RBAC vs ABAC — Examiner Insight:** KTU frequently tests the difference. **RBAC** scales poorly in large enterprises (role explosion problem). **ABAC** is more dynamic and policy-driven, suitable for cloud-native environments.

## 2.6 Virtualization Security Specifics

In cloud environments, **multi-tenancy** allows multiple customers to share the same physical hardware via **Virtual Machines (VMs)**. This introduces the **VM Escape** threat, where an attacker breaks out of a VM into the host hypervisor.

**Hypervisor-based isolation techniques:**

- **Type-1 Hypervisor (Bare-metal)** — Runs directly on hardware (e.g., VMware ESXi, KVM).
- **Type-2 Hypervisor (Hosted)** — Runs within an OS (e.g., Oracle VirtualBox).
- **sVirt / SELinux** — Mandatory Access Control in Linux-based hypervisors.
- **Intel SGX (Software Guard Extensions)** — Hardware-based memory encryption creating *enclaves*.

## 2.7 Real-world Engineering Applications

- **AWS KMS (Key Management Service)** — FIPS 140-2 validated HSMs for key storage.
- **Azure Active Directory (AAD)** — Cloud-native IAM supporting SAML 2.0, OAuth 2.0, OpenID Connect.
- **Google Cloud IAM** — Policy-based, supports deny rules overriding allow.
- **HashiCorp Vault** — Open-source secret management (tokens, API keys, certificates).

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked-Out Derivation: RSA Key Generation (Full Step-by-Step)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners often present a *complete numerical RSA problem* worth 7-8 marks. The derivation below is the **gold-standard template** for full marks.

### **Problem Statement (Model Question):**
Generate an RSA key pair with $p = 61$ and $q = 53$. Then encrypt the plaintext $M = 42$ and decrypt the resulting ciphertext.

### **Solution — Step 1: Compute the Modulus $n$**

$$\begin{aligned}
p &= 61 \\
q &= 53 \\
n &= p \times q \\
n &= 61 \times 53 \\
n &= 3233
\end{aligned}$$

**Explanation:** $n$ is the public modulus used in both encryption and decryption operations. The security of RSA relies on the difficulty of factoring this $n$ into its original primes $p$ and $q$.

---

### **Solution — Step 2: Compute Euler's Totient $\phi(n)$**

$$\begin{aligned}
\phi(n) &= (p - 1) \times (q - 1) \\
\phi(n) &= (61 - 1) \times (53 - 1) \\
\phi(n) &= 60 \times 52 \\
\phi(n) &= 3120
\end{aligned}$$

**Explanation:** $\phi(n)$ counts the integers from 1 to $n$ that are coprime to $n$. This value is fundamental to constructing the public/private exponent pair.

---

### **Solution — Step 3: Choose Public Exponent $e$**

We must choose $e$ such that:
$$1 < e < \phi(n) \quad \text{and} \quad \gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$$

Testing candidates: $e = 17$ is a common choice.

$$\gcd(17, 3120) = 1 \quad \checkmark$$

**Public Key:** $(n, e) = (3233, 17)$

---

### **Solution — Step 4: Compute Private Exponent $d$**

We need $d$ such that:

$$e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$$

That is:

$$17 \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{3120}$$

Using the **Extended Euclidean Algorithm**:

$$\begin{aligned}
3120 &= 183 \times 17 + 9 \\
17 &= 1 \times 9 + 8 \\
9 &= 1 \times 8 + 1 \\
8 &= 8 \times 1 + 0
\end{aligned}$$

Now back-substitute:

$$\begin{aligned}
1 &= 9 - 1 \times 8 \\
1 &= 9 - 1 \times (17 - 1 \times 9) \\
1 &= 2 \times 9 - 1 \times 17 \\
1 &= 2 \times (3120 - 183 \times 17) - 1 \times 17 \\
1 &= 2 \times 3120 - 366 \times 17 - 1 \times 17 \\
1 &= 2 \times 3120 - 367 \times 17
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore:

$$d = -367 \mod 3120 = 3120 - 367 = 2753$$

**Private Key:** $(n, d) = (3233, 2753)$

**Verification:** $17 \times 2753 = 46801$. And $46801 \div 3120 = 15.0003...$, so $46801 - 15 \times 3120 = 46801 - 46800 = 1$ ✓

---

### **Solution — Step 5: Encrypt Plaintext $M = 42$**

$$\begin{aligned}
C &= M^{e} \mod n \\
C &= 42^{17} \mod 3233
\end{aligned}$$

Using repeated squaring (modular exponentiation):

$$\begin{aligned}
42^1 &= 42 \\
42^2 &= 1764 \\
42^4 &= 1764^2 \mod 3233 = 3111696 \mod 3233 = 855 \\
42^8 &= 855^2 \mod 3233 = 731025 \mod 3233 = 1690 \\
42^{16} &= 1690^2 \mod 3233 = 2856100 \mod 3233 = 140
\end{aligned}$$

Now, $17 = 16 + 1$:

$$\begin{aligned}
42^{17} &= 42^{16} \times 42^1 \mod 3233 \\
&= 140 \times 42 \mod 3233 \\
&= 5880 \mod 3233 \\
&= 2647
\end{aligned}$$

**Ciphertext:** $C = 2647$

---

### **Solution — Step 6: Decrypt Ciphertext $C = 2647$**

$$\begin{aligned}
M &= C^{d} \mod n \\
M &= 2647^{2753} \mod 3233
\end{aligned}$$

This requires the **Chinese Remainder Theorem (CRT)** for efficiency. We compute:

$$\begin{aligned}
M_p &= C^{d \mod (p-1)} \mod p = 2647^{2753 \mod 60} \mod 61 = 2647^{53} \mod 61 \\
M_q &= C^{d \mod (q-1)} \mod q = 2647^{2753 \mod 52} \mod 53 = 2647^{49} \mod 53
\end{aligned}$$

Computing each:

$$M_p = 2647^{53} \mod 61$$

Since $2647 \mod 61 = 2647 - 43 \times 61 = 2647 - 2623 = 24$:

$$M_p = 24^{53} \mod 61 = 42 \mod 61$$

$$M_q = 2647^{49} \mod 53$$

Since $2647 \mod 53 = 2647 - 49 \times 53 = 2647 - 2597 = 50$:

$$M_q = 50^{49} \mod 53 = 42 \mod 53$$

Applying CRT to combine: $M_p = 42$ and $M_q = 42$, so:

$$M = 42$$

**Decrypted Plaintext:** $M = 42$ ✓ (Matches the original plaintext.)

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Security Models

| Security Aspect | Public Cloud | Private Cloud | Hybrid Cloud | Community Cloud |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Data Control** | Low (CSP owns) | High (org owns) | Medium | Medium |
| **Cost** | Lowest | Highest | Medium | Shared |
| **Scalability** | Excellent | Limited | Excellent | Limited |
| **Compliance Suitability** | Low (regulated data) | High | High | Medium |
| **Attack Surface** | Large | Small | Medium | Medium |
| **Encryption Requirement** | Mandatory | Recommended | Mandatory | Mandatory |
| **Perimeter Defense** | CSP + Client | Client only | Both | Shared |

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Encryption Algorithms

| Property | AES (Symmetric) | RSA (Asymmetric) | ECC (Asymmetric) | SHA-256 (Hash) |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Type** | Symmetric | Asymmetric | Asymmetric | One-way Hash |
| **Key Size (equiv. security)** | 128 / 256 bits | 2048 / 4096 bits | 256 / 512 bits | N/A (no key) |
| **Speed** | Very Fast | Slow | Faster than RSA | Very Fast |
| **Use Case** | Bulk data encryption | Key exchange, signatures | Mobile, IoT | Integrity, signatures |
| **Output Size** | Same as input | Variable | Variable | 256 bits (fixed) |
| **KTU Exam Weightage** | ★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★ | ★★★★ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Tip:** When asked to "compare symmetric and asymmetric encryption," always mention the **hybrid approach** used in practice: **RSA exchanges the AES key**, then **AES encrypts the bulk data** (e.g., HTTPS/TLS handshake).

## 3.4 Step-by-Step: SLA Downtime Calculation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Numerical SLA problems are common in KTU Part A (3-mark) questions.

**Given:** A cloud provider offers **99.95%** uptime SLA. Find the **maximum allowable annual downtime**.

**Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Downtime fraction} &= 1 - U = 1 - 0.9995 = 0.0005 \\
\text{Hours per year} &= 24 \times 365 = 8760 \text{ hours} \\
D &= 0.0005 \times 8760 \\
D &= 4.38 \text{ hours per year}
\end{aligned}$$

**Converting to minutes:**

$$D = 4.38 \times 60 = 262.8 \text{ minutes per year}$$

**Converting to seconds (RTO context):**

$$D = 262.8 \times 60 = 15768 \text{ seconds per year} \approx 4.38 \text{ hours}$$

---

## 3.5 Step-by-Step: Storage Overhead in Erasure Coding

**Given:** A cloud storage system uses **erasure coding** with $k = 6$ data blocks and $n = 10$ total blocks. Compute the **storage overhead ratio** and **fault tolerance**.

**Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Storage Overhead Ratio} &= \frac{n}{k} = \frac{10}{6} = 1.667 \\
\text{Storage Overhead \%} &= \left(\frac{n}{k} - 1\right) \times 100\% = 66.67\% \\
\text{Fault Tolerance} &= n - k = 10 - 6 = 4 \text{ simultaneous block failures}
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** The system uses 66.67% extra storage space, but can survive up to **4 simultaneous disk failures** without data loss — superior to traditional RAID-5 (1-disk tolerance) for cloud-scale deployments.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define Cloud Security. Explain the three pillars of the CIA Triad with respect to cloud computing.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Cloud Security is the discipline of cyber-security that protects cloud-based systems, data, and infrastructure using policies, controls, and technologies. It encompasses encryption, IAM, network security, and compliance governance.

**CIA Triad (2 marks):**

1. **Confidentiality:** Ensures data is accessible only to authorized users. Enforced via **AES-256 encryption at rest**, **TLS 1.3 in transit**, and strict **access control lists (ACLs)**.

2. **Integrity:** Ensures data is not altered or tampered with. Enforced via **SHA-256 hashing**, **digital signatures (RSA)**, **HMAC codes**, and **audit logging**.
