| TLS Version | $\geq 1.2 \text{ (TLS 1.3 preferred)}$ | Secure transmission |
| Defense Layers | $n \geq 3 \text{ independent layers}$ | Architecture validation |
| Zero Trust | $\text{Trust}(X) = 0, \forall X \in \text{Entities}$ | Default deny policy |
| Least Privilege | $\text{Perms}(U) = \min(\text{Required for task})$ | Access provisioning |

**Note:** In formulas above, $\vert$ symbols were replaced with $\mid$ or descriptive text to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.3 Reference Architecture Layers

A complete cloud security architecture follows this **layered model** (top-down):

### Layer 1: Governance, Risk, and Compliance (GRC)
- Policies, standards, procedures
- Risk assessment frameworks (NIST, ISO 27001, CSA STAR)
- Audit trails and compliance reporting

### Layer 2: Identity & Access Management
- Centralized identity store (e.g., AWS IAM, Azure AD)
- Federation (SAML 2.0, OAuth 2.0, OpenID Connect)
- Privileged Access Management (PAM)
- Just-in-Time (JIT) access provisioning

### Layer 3: Application Security
- Secure SDLC (SSDLC)
- Static/Dynamic Application Security Testing (SAST/DAST)
- Web Application Firewalls (WAF)
- API security gateways
- Secrets management (HashiCorp Vault, AWS KMS)

### Layer 4: Data Security
- Encryption at rest, in transit, in use
- Data Loss Prevention (DLP)
- Tokenization and masking
- Key Management Services (KMS)
- Data classification and labeling

### Layer 5: Network Security
- VPCs, subnets, route tables
- Security groups and NACLs
- Web Application Firewalls
- IDS/IPS systems
- DDoS protection
- Network segmentation (micro-segmentation)

### Layer 6: Compute & Host Security
- OS hardening and patching
- Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR)
- Container security (image scanning, runtime protection)
- Serverless security
- Immutable infrastructure patterns

### Layer 7: Physical Security (CSP Responsibility)
- Data center access controls
- Surveillance and environmental controls
- Hardware destruction procedures
- Geographic redundancy

## 2.4 Zero Trust Architecture (ZTA) ⭐

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Zero Trust** is a flagship concept in modern cloud security — high probability of KTU questions.

**Core Tenet:** *"Never trust, always verify."*

Traditional perimeter security assumed anything inside the network was trusted. Zero Trust **eliminates this implicit trust**.

**Zero Trust Principles:**

1. **Verify explicitly:** Authenticate and authorize every request using all available data (identity, location, device, service, data sensitivity).
2. **Least privilege access:** Limit user access with Just-In-Time and Just-Enough-Access (JIT/JEA).
3. **Assume breach:** Design as if a breach has already occurred; minimize blast radius with micro-segmentation.

**Zero Trust Control Plane (NIST SP 800-207):**

$$\text{ZTA} = f(\text{Subject}, \text{Resource}, \text{Policy Engine}, \text{Policy Administrator})$$

The **Policy Engine (PE)** makes access decisions; the **Policy Administrator (PA)** enforces them.

## 2.5 Compliance & Regulatory Frameworks

Cloud security architecture must align with industry standards:

| Framework | Focus | Applicability |
|---|---|---|
| **ISO 27001** | Information Security Management System | Global standard |
| **NIST CSF** | Cybersecurity Framework (Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover) | US federal/enterprise |
| **SOC 2** | Service Organization Controls (Type I/II) | SaaS providers |
| **PCI DSS** | Payment Card Industry Data Security | E-commerce, payments |
| **HIPAA** | Health Insurance Portability Act | Healthcare data |
| **GDPR** | General Data Protection Regulation | EU personal data |
| **CSA STAR** | Cloud Security Alliance certification | Cloud-specific |
| **IT Act 2000 (India)** | Information Technology Act | Indian context (KTU-relevant) |

## 2.6 Security Monitoring & Incident Response

### SIEM (Security Information and Event Management)

Aggregates logs from across the environment for **real-time correlation** and **threat detection**.

**Detection Formula:**

$$\text{Alert} = \text{Event} \cap \text{Threshold}(\text{Frequency}, \text{Anomaly Score})$$

### SOAR (Security Orchestration, Automation, and Response)

Automates repetitive security tasks and orchestrates incident response workflows.

### Incident Response Lifecycle (NIST SP 800-61):

$$\text{IR} = \text{Preparation} \to \text{Detection} \to \text{Containment} \to \text{Eradication} \to \text{Recovery} \to \text{Lessons Learned}$$

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Security Architecture Design is applied across:

- **Financial Services:** Protecting transaction data, meeting PCI DSS
- **Healthcare:** HIPAA-compliant patient record systems
- **Government:** FedRAMP-authorized cloud deployments
- **E-commerce:** PCI DSS, fraud prevention, DDoS mitigation
- **IoT/Edge:** Securing distributed devices and data streams
- **AI/ML Workloads:** Protecting training data and model integrity

The cost of *not* having proper security architecture is staggering — average breach cost in 2023 was **USD 4.45 million** (IBM Cost of Data Breach Report).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🔬 Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Derivation: Shared Responsibility Allocation

**Problem Context:** A company deploys a workload on AWS EC2 (IaaS model). Determine the responsibility split for: (1) Data encryption at rest, (2) Hypervisor patching, (3) Network firewall configuration.

### Step 1: Identify the Service Model
The service model is **IaaS (Infrastructure as a Service)** using EC2.

### Step 2: Map Responsibilities Using the Stack

```
Layer                  Responsibility Holder
+--------------------------------+
| Data & Access        | CUSTOMER |
+--------------------------------+
| Application          | CUSTOMER |
+--------------------------------+
| OS & Runtime         | CUSTOMER |
+--------------------------------+
| Hypervisor           | CSP      |
+--------------------------------+
| Physical Infra       | CSP      |
+--------------------------------+
```

### Step 3: Apply Responsibility to Each Item

For **Data encryption at rest**:
- Customer controls the data and EC2 instance.
- Customer is responsible for encryption.
- $$\text{Responsibility}_{\text{encryption}} = \text{Customer} = 100\%$$

For **Hypervisor patching**:
- Hypervisor is a layer below the EC2 instance, managed by AWS.
- $$\text{Responsibility}_{\text{hypervisor}} = \text{CSP (AWS)} = 100\%$$

For **Network firewall configuration**:
- Security groups and NACLs are customer-configured.
- Underlying physical network is AWS-managed.
- $$\text{Responsibility}_{\text{firewall config}} = \text{Customer} = 100\%$$

### Step 4: Verification

$$\text{Customer}_{\text{total}} + \text{CSP}_{\text{total}} = 100\%$$

For this scenario, the customer manages encryption + firewall (2 items), CSP manages hypervisor (1 item). This is consistent with IaaS shared responsibility.

## 3.2 Derivation: RSA Key Strength and Encryption Capacity

**Problem:** An organization needs to encrypt data using RSA. Calculate the maximum plaintext block size for RSA with a 2048-bit key, and determine the security strength in equivalent symmetric bits.

### Step 1: Establish RSA Mathematical Foundation

RSA encryption is based on the relationship:

$$C = M^{e} \mod n$$

where:
- $M$ is the plaintext message (as integer)
- $e$ is the public exponent
- $n = p \cdot q$ is the modulus (product of two large primes)
- $C$ is the ciphertext

### Step 2: Determine Maximum Block Size

The plaintext $M$ must satisfy:

$$0 \leq M < n$$

Therefore, the maximum block size in bytes for a 2048-bit key is:

$$\text{Max Block} = \frac{2048 \text{ bits}}{8 \text{ bits/byte}} = 256 \text{ bytes}$$

### Step 3: Map RSA Key Size to Symmetric Equivalent

The security strength of RSA relative to symmetric encryption:

$$\text{Equivalent Symmetric Bits} = f(\text{RSA Key Bits})$$

**Standard Mapping (NIST SP 800-57):**

```
RSA Key Size    |  Equivalent Symmetric Security
+----------------+-------------------------------
   1024 bits    |     ~80 bits  (DEPRECATED)
   2048 bits    |    ~112 bits  (CURRENT MINIMUM)
   3072 bits    |    ~128 bits
   7680 bits    |    ~192 bits
  15360 bits    |    ~256 bits
```

For 2048-bit RSA:
$$\text{Symmetric Equivalent} = 112 \text{ bits} \approx \text{3DES/AES-112}$$

### Step 4: Operational Implication

AES-256 (256-bit symmetric) is computationally equivalent to **RSA-15360 bits** in security strength. RSA's computational overhead makes it impractical at high key sizes, hence **hybrid encryption** (RSA + AES) is used in practice.

## 3.3 Derivation: Hash Collision Probability (Birthday Problem)

**Problem:** A system uses SHA-256 (256-bit hash). After how many messages does the probability of a collision reach 50%?

### Step 1: Birthday Problem Formulation

The probability of **at least one collision** after hashing $n$ messages:

$$P(\text{collision}) \approx 1 - e^{-\frac{n^2}{2 \cdot 2^{b}}}$$

where $b$ is the hash output bits.

### Step 2: Solve for 50% Probability

Setting $P(\text{collision}) = 0.5$:

$$0.5 = 1 - e^{-\frac{n^2}{2 \cdot 2^{256}}}$$

$$0.5 = e^{-\frac{n^2}{2^{257}}}$$

$$\ln(0.5) = -\frac{n^2}{2^{257}}$$

$$n^2 = -\ln(0.5) \cdot 2^{257} = \ln(2) \cdot 2^{257}$$

$$n = \sqrt{\ln(2)} \cdot 2^{128.5}$$

### Step 3: Calculate Numerical Value

$$\ln(2) \approx 0.693$$
$$\sqrt{0.693} \approx 0.8326$$
$$n \approx 0.8326 \cdot 2^{128.5}$$

This is approximately $2^{128}$ — a number with 39 digits.

### Step 4: Practical Implication

$$\text{Collision-resistant messages} \approx 2^{128} \text{ for SHA-256}$$

This is computationally infeasible, confirming SHA-256's cryptographic strength for integrity verification.

## 3.4 Derivation: Availability Calculations from SLA

**Problem:** A cloud provider advertises **99.99% availability SLA**. Calculate the maximum allowed downtime per year, per month, and per week.

### Step 1: Yearly Downtime

$$\text{Downtime}_{\text{year}} = (1 - 0.9999) \cdot 365 \cdot 24 \cdot 60 \text{ minutes}$$

$$= 0.0001 \cdot 525{,}600 \text{ min}$$

$$= 52.56 \text{ minutes/year}$$

### Step 2: Monthly Downtime

$$\text{Downtime}_{\text{month}} = \frac{52.56}{12} = 4.38 \text{ minutes/month}$$

### Step 3: Weekly Downtime

$$\text{Downtime}_{\text{week}} = \frac{52.56}{52} \approx 1.01 \text{ minutes/week}$$

### Step 4: Comparison Table

| SLA | Annual Downtime | Monthly Downtime | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **99%** (two nines) | 3.65 days | 7.20 hours | Development environments |
| **99.9%** (three nines) | 8.76 hours | 43.80 minutes | Production web apps |
| **99.95%** | 4.38 hours | 21.90 minutes | Business-critical apps |
| **99.99%** (four nines) | 52.56 minutes | 4.38 minutes | Enterprise mission-critical |
| **99.999%** (five nines) | 5.26 minutes | 0.44 minutes | Telecom, life-safety systems |

## 3.5 Derivation: Risk Score Calculation (Quantitative Risk Assessment)

**Problem:** A cloud-hosted database has the following risk profile:
- Asset Value: $500,000
- Threat Likelihood: 0.3 (30% per year)
- Impact Factor: 0.8 (80% loss if exploited)

Calculate the **Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE)**.

### Step 1: Define the Risk Equation

$$\text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}$$

where:
- $\text{SLE} = \text{Single Loss Expectancy} = \text{Asset Value} \times \text{Impact Factor}$
- $\text{ARO} = \text{Annual Rate of Occurrence}$

### Step 2: Calculate SLE

$$\text{SLE} = 500{,}000 \times 0.8 = 400{,}000$$

### Step 3: Determine ARO

$$\text{ARO} = 0.3 \text{ occurrences/year}$$

### Step 4: Calculate ALE

$$\text{ALE} = 400{,}000 \times 0.3 = 120{,}000 \text{ USD/year}$$

### Step 5: Decision Criterion

A security control is cost-effective if:

$$\text{Control Cost} < \text{ALE Reduction}$$

If a proposed firewall costs $50,000/year and reduces risk by 70%, the savings would be:
$$\text{Savings} = 120{,}000 \times 0.7 = 84{,}000 \text{ USD/year}$$
$$\text{Net Benefit} = 84{,}000 - 50{,}000 = 34{,}000 \text{ USD/year (positive)}$$

Therefore, the control is **justified**.

## 3.6 Worked Example: Zero Trust Access Decision

**Scenario:** A user attempts to access a sensitive S3 bucket from a new device in an unusual geographic location at 3 AM.

**Traditional Security Decision:**
$$\text{Access} = \text{True (if authenticated inside network)}$$

**Zero Trust Decision:**

$$\text{Access} = f(\text{Identity}, \text{Device Trust}, \text{Location}, \text{Time}, \text{Data Sensitivity}, \text{Behavior})$$

Evaluating each factor:

```
Factor              | Score | Weight | Weighted Score
+--------------------+-------+--------+---------------
Identity verified   |  1.0  |  0.30  |     0.30
Device trusted      |  0.2  |  0.25  |     0.05
Location normal     |  0.1  |  0.20  |     0.02
Time normal         |  0.1  |  0.10  |     0.01
Data sensitivity    |  0.0  |  0.15  |     0.00
                     +-------+--------+---------------
TOTAL RISK SCORE          |        |     0.38
```

**Decision threshold:** Risk score $\geq 0.7$ = Deny; $0.4$ to $0.7$ = MFA challenge; $< 0.4$ = Allow with logging.

In this case, score $= 0.38 < 0.4$, so access is allowed but with **enhanced logging and step-up authentication** for the next access attempt. This illustrates the **continuous verification** aspect of Zero Trust.

# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Rapid Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the Shared Responsibility Model in cloud security. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**

The Shared Responsibility Model is a cloud security framework that **divides security responsibilities between the Cloud Service Provider (CSP) and the customer** based on the service model deployed.

**Key points:**

- In **IaaS**, the customer is responsible for data, applications, OS, and runtime, while the CSP manages virtualization, servers, storage, and networking.
- In **PaaS**, the CSP also manages the OS, runtime, and middleware; the customer manages data and applications.
- In **SaaS**, the CSP manages almost everything; the customer is responsible only for data and user access.

**Core principle:** The customer's responsibility for **data and access never disappears**, regardless of service model. The CSP's responsibility **increases** as you move from IaaS → PaaS → SaaS.

> [!NOTE]
> Examiners award full marks only when all three service models are clearly contrasted.

### Question 2: Explain the Zero Trust security model with its core principles. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]

**Model Answer:**

**Zero Trust** is a security architecture model that **eliminates implicit trust** and requires **continuous verification** of every user, device, and request, regardless of whether it originates inside or outside the network perimeter.

**Three Core Principles:**

1. **Verify Explicitly:** Authenticate and authorize every request using identity, device posture, location, and data sensitivity.
2. **Least Privilege Access:** Provide Just-In-Time (JIT) and Just-Enough-Access (JEA) to minimize standing privileges.
3. **Assume Breach:** Operate as if the network is already compromised; use micro-segmentation to limit blast radius.

**Key Components:** Policy Engine (PE), Policy Administrator (PA), and Policy Enforcement Point (PEP).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — With Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1): Comprehensive Security Architecture Design

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper Pattern]**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:** Design a layered security architecture for a cloud-based e-commerce application handling payment card data. Identify and explain at least **five security layers** with specific controls.

**Model Solution:**

**Layer 1: Governance, Risk, and Compliance (GRC)**
- Implement ISO 27001-aligned Information Security Management System (ISMS).
- Conduct annual risk assessments.
- Achieve **PCI DSS** certification for payment data handling.
- Establish policies for data classification, access control, and incident response.

**Layer 2: Identity and Access Management (IAM)**
- Deploy **Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)** for all administrative and customer accounts.
- Implement **Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)** with least-privilege principle.
- Use **federated identity** (OAuth 2.0, OpenID Connect) for customer logins.
- Implement **Privileged Access Management (PAM)** for administrative access with session recording.

**Layer 3: Application Security**
- Follow **Secure SDLC (SSDLC)** with security reviews at each phase.
- Deploy **Web Application Firewall (WAF)** to protect against OWASP Top 10 (SQL injection, XSS, CSRF).
- Use **SAST/DAST tools** in CI/CD pipeline.
- Implement **API security gateways** with rate limiting and authentication.

**Layer 4: Data Protection**
- **Encryption at rest:** AES-256 for databases and storage using customer-managed KMS keys.
- **Encryption in transit:** TLS 1.3 for all communications.
- **Tokenization** for payment card data — replace PAN with tokens.
- **Data Loss Prevention (DLP)** to detect and block sensitive data exfiltration.
- **Backup encryption** with geo-redundant storage.

**Layer 5: Network Security**
- Deploy in a **Virtual Private Cloud (VPC)** with private subnets for backend systems.
- Implement **security groups** (stateful) and **NACLs** (stateless) for defense-in-depth.
- Use **DDoS protection** (e.g., AWS Shield, Azure DDoS Protection).
- Deploy **IDS/IPS** for anomaly detection.
- Implement **micro-segmentation** to isolate workloads.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:** Explain the Shared Responsibility Model with a comparative table for IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS. How does this influence the security architecture design decisions?

**Model Solution:**

The **Shared Responsibility Model** divides security tasks between the CSP and the customer. The CSP secures the **cloud itself** (infrastructure, hardware, foundational services), while the customer secures **what they put in the cloud** (data, access, configuration).

**Comparative Responsibility Table:**

| Component | IaaS | PaaS | SaaS |
|---|---|---|---|
| Data & Access | Customer | Customer | Customer |
| Application Code | Customer | Customer | CSP |
| OS & Runtime | Customer | CSP | CSP |
| Middleware | Customer | CSP | CSP |
| Virtualization | CSP | CSP | CSP |
| Servers/Storage | CSP | CSP | CSP |
| Networking | CSP | CSP | CSP |

**Influence on Architecture Design Decisions:**

1. **IaaS:** Maximum control, maximum responsibility. Customer must implement OS hardening, patching, network security groups, and host-based firewalls. Higher operational overhead.

2. **PaaS:** Balanced model. Customer focuses on application security, data classification, and IAM. OS/runtime patching handled by CSP. Faster development cycles.

3. **SaaS:** Minimum customer control, minimum responsibility. Customer focuses on data governance, user provisioning, and access policies. Suitable for non-core business functions.

**Key Insight for Architecture:**

- The choice of service model directly impacts **which security controls** must be designed and implemented by the customer.
- Even in SaaS, the customer must enforce **strong IAM and data classification** because data security responsibility never transfers to the CSP.
- Architecture decisions must include **identity federation** to integrate cloud services with on-premises directories.
- Compliance scope (PCI DSS, HIPAA) determines which layers require customer-implemented controls.

---

### Question B (Choice 2): Cryptography and Identity Management Focus

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Question Paper Pattern]**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:** Compare symmetric and asymmetric encryption. Explain the digital signature process with its mathematical foundation and identify its role in cloud security.

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison: Symmetric vs Asymmetric Encryption**

| Parameter | Symmetric | Asymmetric |
|---|---|---|
| Keys Used | Single shared key | Public + Private key pair |
| Speed | Fast (bulk data) | Slow (computationally heavy) |
| Key Distribution | Challenging | Easier (public key can be shared) |
| Scalability | Poor (n² keys for n users) | Better (2n keys for n users) |
| Algorithms | AES, DES, 3DES | RSA, ECC, DSA |
| Key Length | 128/256 bits | 2048/4096 bits |
| Use Case | Data encryption | Key exchange, digital signatures |

**Digital Signature Process:**

**Mathematical Foundation:**

$$\text{Signing: } \sigma = E_{K_{\text{private}}}(\text{Hash}(M))$$

$$\text{Verification: } \text{Valid if } \text{Hash}(M) = D_{K_{\text{public}}}(\sigma)$$

**Step-by-Step Process:**

1. **Compute Hash:** Sender computes a cryptographic hash of the message: $h = H(M)$ using SHA-256.
2. **Sign Hash:** Sender encrypts the hash with their **private key** to produce the signature: $\sigma = E_{K_{\text{priv}}}(h)$.
3. **Transmit:** Sender sends the original message $M$ along with the signature $\sigma$.
4. **Verify Hash:** Receiver computes the hash of the received message: $h' = H(M)$.
5. **Decrypt Signature:** Receiver decrypts the signature using the sender's **public key**: $h = D_{K_{\text{pub}}}(\sigma)$.
6. **Compare:** If $h = h'$, the signature is **valid**; otherwise, it is **rejected**.

**Role in Cloud Security:**

- **Authentication:** Confirms the sender's identity.
- **Integrity:** Ensures the message has not been altered.
- **Non-Repudiation:** The sender cannot deny signing the message.
- **Code Signing:** Verifies software artifacts and container images in cloud deployments.
- **API Authentication:** Used in JWT tokens for secure API communication.
- **Document Signing:** Legally binding electronic signatures in cloud workflows.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:** Describe the components and working of Identity and Access Management (IAM) in cloud environments. Explain RBAC, ABAC, and the principle of least privilege with examples.

**Model Solution:**

**IAM Components:**

1. **Subjects:** Users, services, devices requesting access.
2. **Identities:** Unique digital representations (user IDs, service accounts).
3. **Authentication Mechanisms:** Passwords, MFA, biometrics, certificates.
4. **Authorization Engine:** Policy Decision Point (PDP) and Policy Enforcement Point (PEP).
5. **Identity Store:** Centralized database (LDAP, Active Directory, cloud-native).
6. **Federation Services:** SAML 2.0, OAuth 2.0, OpenID Connect.
7. **Audit Logs:** Track access events for compliance and forensics.

**IAM Working Process:**

$$\text{Access Decision} = f(\text{Subject}, \text{Resource}, \text{Action}, \text{Context})$$

1. User requests access to a resource.
2. Authentication verifies identity.
3. Authorization engine evaluates policies.
4. Access is granted or denied based on policy.
5. Activity is logged for auditing.

**RBAC (Role-Based Access Control):**

- Permissions are assigned to **roles**, not individual users.
- Users inherit permissions by being assigned to roles.
- **Example:** In AWS, a "Database Administrator" role may have permissions to read/write RDS instances, but not to delete S3 buckets.

$$\text{Permissions}(U) = \sum_{r \in \text{Roles}(U)} \text{Permissions}(r)$$

**ABAC (Attribute-Based Access Control):**

- Access decisions based on **attributes** of subject, resource, action, and environment.
- Uses policies like: *"Allow read access to financial reports IF user.department = 'Finance' AND time < 18:00 AND device.trust_level > 0.7"*
- More granular and dynamic than RBAC.

$$\text{Allow} = \text{Policy}(\text{Subj}_{\text{attrs}}, \text{Res}_{\text{attrs}}, \text{Env}_{\text{attrs}})$$

**Principle of Least Privilege (PoLP):**

- Grant users **only the minimum permissions** necessary to perform their tasks.
- Reduces attack surface and limits damage from compromised accounts.
- **Example:** A junior developer should not have production database deletion rights; they should have read access to logs only.

$$\text{Granted}_{\text{permissions}} = \min(\text{Permissions} \mid \text{Task can be completed})$$

**Implementation in Cloud:**

- AWS IAM Policies (JSON-based)
- Azure RBAC with built-in and custom roles
- Google Cloud IAM with predefined and custom roles
- Just-In-Time (JIT) access provisioning
- Time-bound and resource-scoped permissions

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing the layers** in the Shared Responsibility Model — students often mix up IaaS vs PaaS vs SaaS allocations. Memorize that **data and access are ALWAYS customer's responsibility**.
> 2. **Forgetting non-repudiation** in digital signature answers — it is a frequently missed point worth 1-2 marks.
> 3. **Not providing examples** for RBAC/ABAC — abstract definitions lose marks; always include a concrete cloud scenario.
> 4. **Skipping the "why"** — examiners want to know *why* a control is used, not just *what* it is.
> 5. **Ignoring compliance frameworks** — mentioning ISO 27001, PCI DSS, or NIST adds depth and earns bonus marks.
> 6. **Writing symmetric and asymmetric as "same"** — clearly differentiate key count, speed, and use cases.
> 7. **Not mentioning TLS version** — always specify TLS 1.2 or 1.3 (TLS 1.0/1.1 are deprecated).
