- **Zero Trust:** Never trust, always verify — implement via mTLS, short-lived tokens, device posture, and policy engines.
- **Compliance:** PCI-DSS, HIPAA, GDPR, ISO 27001, SOC 2 — all mandate application-layer controls.
- **Always end 14-mark answers with a diagram/matrix + cloud-specific examples.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Security
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT634 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Data Security

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Data Security** in the context of Cloud Computing refers to the set of policies, controls, procedures, and technological measures collectively employed to protect cloud-resident data from unauthorized access, alteration, destruction, or disclosure throughout its entire lifecycle. It encompasses three primary data states: **Data at Rest** (stored in cloud databases/object stores), **Data in Transit** (moving across networks), and **Data in Use** (actively being processed in memory/CPU).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CIA Triad (Core Principle of Data Security)**
> Every data security mechanism in the cloud must guarantee:
> - **Confidentiality** – Only authorized parties can read the data.
> - **Integrity** – Data is not tampered with or altered in transit or at rest.
> - **Availability** – Data is reliably accessible when required by authorized users.
> - (Modern extensions: **Authenticity, Non-Repudiation, Accountability**)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cloud Bank Vault" 

Imagine you are depositing your most valuable jewelry in a bank's safe deposit locker (the cloud).

| Real-World Action | Cloud Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Locking the locker with a physical key | **Symmetric Encryption** (same key locks & unlocks) |
| Giving a copy of the key to a friend via a registered courier | **Asymmetric Encryption** (public key to encrypt, private key to decrypt) |
| The bank's signature on the deposit receipt | **Digital Signature** |
| Fingerprint scan to enter the vault room | **Authentication / MFA** |
| A sealed, tamper-proof bag | **Hashing + Integrity Check** |
| The bank's promise that it can produce your items any time | **Availability (Replication, Backups)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is Cloud Data Security harder than traditional security?**
> In a cloud environment, the data owner **does not physically possess** the storage media. Therefore, trust must be placed in the **Cloud Service Provider (CSP)**, the underlying infrastructure, and the cryptographic protocols that wrap the data before it leaves the owner's premises.

### 1.3 The Three States of Data

- **Data at Rest**: Static data stored in databases, file systems, or object storage (e.g., AWS S3). Protected via encryption-at-rest, access control lists (ACLs).
- **Data in Transit**: Data moving between client ↔ cloud, or between cloud services. Protected via TLS/SSL, IPsec, VPN tunnels.
- **Data in Use**: Data being processed in volatile memory (RAM, CPU caches). Protected via confidential computing, secure enclaves (e.g., Intel SGX, AMD SEV).

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners frequently ask: *"Differentiate between Data at Rest, Data in Transit, and Data in Use."* Memorize the protecting mechanism for each state.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Pillars of Cloud Data Security

1. **Encryption** – Mathematical transformation of plaintext into ciphertext.
2. **Hashing** – One-way function producing a fixed-size digest of arbitrary-length input.
3. **Digital Signatures** – Combines hashing + asymmetric encryption for authenticity & non-repudiation.
4. **Public Key Infrastructure (PKI)** – Framework of CAs, certificates, and keys for trust distribution.
5. **Access Control Models** – RBAC, ABAC, MAC, DAC.
6. **Data Loss Prevention (DLP)** – Tools that detect/prevent sensitive data exfiltration.
7. **Key Management** – Generation, rotation, storage, and destruction of cryptographic keys (KMS, HSM).

### 2.2 Symmetric vs. Asymmetric Encryption — The Core Comparison

**Symmetric Key Encryption (e.g., AES, DES, 3DES, Blowfish)**
- Single shared key for both encryption and decryption.
- **Pros**: Very fast, ideal for bulk data encryption.
- **Cons**: Key distribution problem — how to securely share the key?
- **Block ciphers** (AES) operate on fixed blocks; **Stream ciphers** (RC4) operate bit-by-bit.

**Asymmetric Key Encryption (e.g., RSA, ECC, ElGamal)**
- A mathematically-related key **pair**: public key (K_pub) encrypts, private key (K_prv) decrypts.
- **Pros**: Solves the key distribution problem; enables digital signatures.
- **Cons**: ~1000× slower than symmetric; hence, used to encrypt small payloads (e.g., the symmetric session key).
- **Hybrid Encryption**: Real systems (like TLS) use asymmetric crypto to safely exchange a randomly generated symmetric session key, then use symmetric crypto for the bulk data transfer.

### 2.3 Cryptographic Hash Functions (Data Integrity)

A hash function `H(M)` takes arbitrary input `M` and produces a fixed-length output `h` (the digest).
- **Properties**: Deterministic, one-way (pre-image resistance), collision-resistant, avalanche effect.
- **Common Algorithms**: **MD5** (128-bit, broken), **SHA-1** (160-bit, deprecated), **SHA-256 / SHA-3** (current standard).

### 2.4 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Notation | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Symmetric Encryption | $C = E_K(P), \quad P = D_K(C)$ | E=Encrypt, D=Decrypt, K=shared key, P=plaintext, C=ciphertext |
| 2 | Asymmetric Encryption | $C = E_{K_{pub}}(P), \quad P = D_{K_{prv}}(C)$ | K_pub is public, K_prv is private |
| 3 | RSA Modulus | $n = p \cdot q$ | p, q are large distinct primes (each $\geq 1024$ bits) |
| 4 | RSA Euler Totient | $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$ | Number of integers coprime to n |
| 5 | RSA Public Exponent | $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$ | e is chosen coprime to $\phi(n)$ |
| 6 | RSA Private Exponent | $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$ | d is the modular multiplicative inverse of e |
| 7 | RSA Encryption | $C \equiv M^{e} \pmod{n}$ | M is the integer plaintext, $0 \leq M < n$ |
| 8 | RSA Decryption | $M \equiv C^{d} \pmod{n}$ | Recovers the original plaintext |
| 9 | Digital Signature | $\sigma = D_{K_{prv}}(H(M))$ | Sender signs the hash of the message |
| 10 | Verification | $H'(M) = E_{K_{pub}}(\sigma)$ | Receiver compares hashes; match ⇒ authentic & intact |
| 11 | AES Block Size | $128$ bits | Fixed block size in AES |
| 12 | AES Key Sizes | $128 \text{ bits} \vert 192 \text{ bits} \vert 256 \text{ bits}$ | Determines rounds: 10 / 12 / 14 |
| 13 | Hash Length (SHA-256) | $256$ bits = $32$ bytes | Hex output: 64 characters |
| 14 | TLS Cipher Suite | $K_{session} \gets \text{Random}(128 \text{ to } 256 \text{ bits})$ | Symmetric key exchanged via asymmetric handshake |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The notation $\vert$ in the AES key sizes row above represents a logical "OR / choice" — the key may be any one of the three lengths, not an absolute value. This avoids breaking the markdown table syntax.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **E-commerce**: TLS handshake uses RSA/ECC to exchange a one-time AES key, then encrypts the credit-card payload via AES-256-GCM.
- **Healthcare (HIPAA on cloud)**: Patient records encrypted at rest with AES; integrity verified by SHA-256; doctors authenticate via X.509 digital certificates.
- **Cloud Object Storage (S3, Azure Blob)**: Server-Side Encryption with KMS-managed keys (SSE-KMS), envelope encryption for large files.
- **Blockchain & Cloud Auditing**: Merkle trees built using SHA-256 hashes allow efficient integrity verification of millions of records.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive RSA Key Generation, Encryption, and Decryption (Worked Numerical Example)

We use deliberately small prime numbers for pedagogical clarity. In production, `p` and `q` are each ≥ 1024 bits.

**Step 1 — Choose two distinct primes `p` and `q`.**
Let $p = 5$ and $q = 7$. Both are prime and distinct.

**Step 2 — Compute the RSA modulus `n`.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= p \times q \\
  &= 5 \times 7 \\
  &= 35
\end{aligned}
$$

The public modulus $n = 35$ will be made public.

**Step 3 — Compute Euler's Totient $\phi(n)$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi(n) &= (p - 1) \cdot (q - 1) \\
        &= (5 - 1) \cdot (7 - 1) \\
        &= 4 \cdot 6 \\
        &= 24
\end{aligned}
$$

The value $\phi(n) = 24$ is kept **secret** (in real life, knowing $\phi(n)$ would allow an attacker to factor $n$ via $p + q = n - \phi(n) + 1$).

**Step 4 — Choose the public exponent `e`.**
We need $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = \gcd(e, 24) = 1$. Let $e = 5$.
Verification: $\gcd(5, 24) = 1$ ✓ (5 is coprime to 24).

**Step 5 — Compute the private exponent `d` such that $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$.**
We need $5d \equiv 1 \pmod{24}$, i.e., $5d = 1 + 24k$ for some integer $k$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
5d &= 1 + 24k \\
\text{Try } k = 1: \quad 5d &= 1 + 24 = 25 \;\Rightarrow\; d = 5
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore $d = 5$. **Note**: Here $e = d = 5$ is a special case; in real RSA, $d \neq e$.

**Step 6 — Publish the public key and keep the private key secret.**
- Public key: $(e, n) = (5, 35)$
- Private key: $(d, n) = (5, 35)$

**Step 7 — Encrypt the plaintext message $M = 2$.**
RSA requires $0 \le M < n$. Here $M = 2$ satisfies this.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &\equiv M^{e} \pmod{n} \\
  &\equiv 2^{5} \pmod{35} \\
  &\equiv 32 \pmod{35} \\
  &\equiv 32
\end{aligned}
$$

Ciphertext $C = 32$ is transmitted over the insecure channel.

**Step 8 — Decrypt the ciphertext $C = 32$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M' &\equiv C^{d} \pmod{n} \\
   &\equiv 32^{5} \pmod{35}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8a — Simplify the base using modular reduction.**
Note that $32 \equiv -3 \pmod{35}$ (since $32 - 35 = -3$). This makes the exponentiation far easier.

$$
\begin{aligned}
M' &\equiv (-3)^{5} \pmod{35} \\
   &\equiv -243 \pmod{35}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8b — Reduce $-243$ modulo $35$.**
We seek the largest non-negative remainder.

$$
\begin{aligned}
-243 &= -7 \times 35 + 2 \\
     &= -245 + 2 \\
\Rightarrow\ M' &\equiv 2 \pmod{35}
\end{aligned}
$$

Decrypted message $M' = 2 = M$ ✓. The original plaintext is recovered intact.

**Step 9 — Verification (Why does this work mathematically?).**
By **Euler's Theorem**, for $\gcd(M, n) = 1$:

$$
M^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}
$$

Hence:

$$
M^{e \cdot d} = M^{1 + k\phi(n)} = M \cdot (M^{\phi(n)})^{k} \equiv M \cdot 1^{k} \equiv M \pmod{n}
$$

This is the foundational theorem that makes RSA decryption work.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Cryptographic Tool

| Goal in Cloud | Recommended Mechanism | Why? |
|---|---|---|
| Encrypt a 50 GB video file in cloud storage | **AES-256 (symmetric)** | 1000× faster than RSA; suitable for bulk data |
| Safely send the AES key to a remote user | **RSA-2048 or ECC P-256** | Solves the key-exchange problem with public-key crypto |
| Verify a software update is unmodified | **SHA-256 hash + RSA signature** | Hash detects tampering; signature authenticates publisher |
| Authenticate a user to a cloud API | **OAuth 2.0 + JWT (signed)** | Stateless, scalable, integrates with PKI |
| Protect data inside a VM while it is processing | **Confidential Computing (Intel SGX)** | Encrypts data in use in CPU enclaves |
| Audit who accessed a S3 bucket and when | **CloudTrail / IAM logs (Accountability)** | Provides non-repudiation for access events |

### 3.3 AES Round Structure (Conceptual Layered View)

AES operates on a 4×4 byte matrix called the **State**. For AES-128, the process is:

1. **Initial Round**: `AddRoundKey` — XOR the state with the round key derived from the main key.
2. **Main Rounds (9 rounds)**: `SubBytes` → `ShiftRows` → `MixColumns` → `AddRoundKey`.
3. **Final Round (10th)**: `SubBytes` → `ShiftRows` → `AddRoundKey` (no MixColumns).

> Each step is reversible, allowing decryption by applying inverse operations in reverse order with the corresponding round keys (expanded from the original key via the **Key Schedule**).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The CIA Triad — Foundation of Data Security

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Data Security in Cloud]:::root --> B[Confidentiality]:::pillar
    A --> C[Integrity]:::pillar
    A --> D[Availability]:::pillar
    B --> B1[AES Encryption]
    B --> B2[Access Control RBAC]
    C --> C1[SHA-256 Hashing]
    C --> C2[Digital Signatures]
    D --> D1[Replication]
    D --> D2[Backup and DR]
    A --> E[Authenticity]
    A --> F[Non Repudiation]
    E --> E1[PKI Certificates]
    F --> F1[Digital Signatures Audit Logs]
    classDef root fill:#1f2937,color:#ffffff,stroke:#facc15,stroke-width:3px
    classDef pillar fill:#0ea5e9,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0369a1,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 Symmetric vs. Asymmetric Encryption — Operational Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph SYM[Symmetric Encryption]
        SA[Plaintext P] --> SB[Encrypt with Shared Key K]
        SB --> SC[Ciphertext C]
        SC --> SD[Decrypt with Same K]
        SD --> SE[Recovered Plaintext P]
    end
    classDef symNode fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af,color:#000000
    class SA,SB,SC,SD,SE symNode
```

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph ASYM[Asymmetric Encryption]
        AA[Plaintext P] --> AB[Encrypt with Receiver Public Key]
        AB --> AC[Ciphertext C]
        AC --> AD[Decrypt with Receiver Private Key]
        AD --> AE[Recovered Plaintext P]
    end
    classDef asymNode fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,color:#000000
    class AA,AB,AC,AD,AE asymNode
```

### 4.3 Public Key Infrastructure (PKI) Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    RA[Root CA Self Signed] --> IA[Intermediate CA]
    IA --> SS1[Server Certificate example.com]
    IA --> SS2[Code Signing Certificate]
    IA --> SS3[User Email Certificate]
    RA -.-> CR1[Certificate Revocation List CRL]
    RA -.-> OC1[OCSP Online Certificate Status Protocol]
    classDef caNode fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#000000
    classDef certNode fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,color:#000000
    classDef metaNode fill:#f3e8ff,stroke:#6b21a8,color:#000000
    class RA,IA caNode
    class SS1,SS2,SS3 certNode
    class CR1,OC1 metaNode
```

### 4.4 SSL/TLS Handshake — Hybrid Encryption in Action

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Client as Client Browser
    participant Server as Cloud Web Server
    Client->>Server: 1. ClientHello Supported Ciphers Random
    Server->>Client: 2. ServerHello Chosen Cipher Server Certificate
    Client->>Client: 3. Verify Server Cert via Trusted CA
    Client->>Server: 4. Pre Master Secret Encrypted with Server Public Key
    Server->>Server: 5. Decrypt using Server Private Key
    Client->>Server: 6. Both Derive Symmetric Session Key
    Client->>Server: 7. Finished Symmetrically Encrypted
    Server->>Client: 8. Finished Symmetrically Encrypted
    Client->>Server: 9. Application Data AES 256 GCM
    Server->>Client: 10. Application Data AES 256 GCM
```

### 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Data Security Pipeline in Cloud Storage

```mermaid
graph TD
    UI[User Uploads File] --> V1[Identity Authentication IAM]
    V1 --> V2[Authorization RBAC Policy Check]
    V2 --> V3[Generate Data Encryption Key DEK]
    V3 --> V4[Encrypt File with DEK AES 256]
    V4 --> V5[Wrap DEK using KMS Master Key]
    V5 --> V6[Store Encrypted File plus Wrapped DEK in Object Store]
    V6 --> ST[(Cloud Object Storage S3 / Blob)]
    LOG[CloudTrail Audit Logging] -.-> V1
    LOG -.-> V2
    LOG -.-> V6
    classDef processNode fill:#e0f2fe,stroke:#075985,color:#000000
    classDef storeNode fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e,color:#000000
    classDef auditNode fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#9d174d,color:#000000
    class UI,V1,V2,V3,V4,V5,V6 processNode
    class ST storeNode
    class LOG auditNode
```

> [!NOTE]
> The architecture above is called **Envelope Encryption** — a standard pattern used by AWS KMS, Azure Key Vault, and Google Cloud KMS. The data is encrypted with a per-object DEK, and the DEK itself is encrypted with a rarely-rotated master key stored in a Hardware Security Module (HSM).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define Data Security. Explain the three states of data with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Data Security in cloud computing refers to the protective measures — policies, technologies, and controls — used to safeguard digital data from unauthorized access, corruption, or theft throughout its lifecycle.
The three states are:
1. **Data at Rest** — Static data stored in databases or object storage. *Example*: Files saved in AWS S3. Protected via AES-256 encryption-at-rest and bucket policies.
2. **Data in Transit** — Data moving over networks. *Example*: HTTPS request from a browser to a cloud API. Protected via TLS 1.3.
3. **Data in Use** — Data being actively processed in RAM or CPU. *Example*: Records being aggregated in a Spark job. Protected via confidential computing enclaves (Intel SGX, AMD SEV).

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**Differentiate between symmetric and asymmetric key encryption. Give one example algorithm for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Symmetric Encryption | Asymmetric Encryption |
|---|---|---|
| Keys used | Single shared key for E and D | Public key (E) + Private key (D) |
| Speed | Very fast | Slow (100–1000× slower) |
| Key distribution | Difficult / requires secure channel | Public key freely shareable |
| Use case | Bulk data encryption | Key exchange, digital signatures |
| Example | **AES-256** | **RSA-2048** |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the RSA algorithm in detail. Choose two small primes, generate the public and private keys, and demonstrate encryption and decryption of a sample message `M = 3`. (7 marks)

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

We will demonstrate the complete RSA cycle using primes $p = 5$ and $q = 11$.

**Step 1: Modulus** $n = p \times q = 5 \times 11 = 55$

**Step 2: Euler Totient** $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1) = 4 \times 10 = 40$

**Step 3: Public Exponent** Choose $e = 7$ (verify $\gcd(7, 40) = 1$ ✓)

**Step 4: Private Exponent** Find $d$ such that $7d \equiv 1 \pmod{40}$.
$7 \times 23 = 161 = 4 \times 40 + 1 \Rightarrow d = 23$

**Public key** = $(7, 55)$, **Private key** = $(23, 55)$.

**Step 5: Encrypt** $M = 3$.

$$
C \equiv 3^{7} \pmod{55} \equiv 2187 \pmod{55} \equiv 42
$$

So $C = 42$.

**Step 6: Decrypt** $C = 42$.

$$
M' \equiv 42^{23} \pmod{55}
$$

We use the simplification $42 \equiv -13 \pmod{55}$:

$$
(-13)^{23} \pmod{55} \Rightarrow \text{using CRT} \Rightarrow M' = 3
$$

Original plaintext $M = 3$ is recovered ✓.

**(b)** Describe the role of Public Key Infrastructure (PKI) and X.509 certificates in ensuring cloud data security. (7 marks)

**Model Solution (7 marks):**
PKI is a hierarchical framework that binds public keys to identities (users, servers, services) via digital **certificates**, enabling **trust** in an otherwise untrusted network.

**Components of PKI:**
1. **Certificate Authority (CA)** — Trusted third party that issues certificates. Root CAs are self-signed; intermediate CAs are signed by roots.
2. **Registration Authority (RA)** — Verifies the identity of the certificate applicant before CA issues the cert.
3. **X.509 Certificate** — A standard format containing: Subject (CN), Issuer, Public Key, Validity Period, Serial Number, and the CA's **Digital Signature**.
4. **Certificate Revocation List (CRL) / OCSP** — Mechanisms to revoke compromised or expired certificates.
5. **Certificate Store / Key Store** — Repositories where applications store trusted root certificates.

**Role in Cloud Data Security:**
- **Authentication**: TLS handshake verifies the cloud server's identity via its X.509 cert, preventing man-in-the-middle (MITM) attacks.
- **Confidentiality**: The server's public key in the cert is used to encrypt the symmetric session key.
- **Integrity & Non-Repudiation**: Code-signing certificates ensure that cloud-deployed software is unmodified and traceable to a publisher.
- **Federation**: SAML / OAuth tokens signed with X.509 certs enable Single Sign-On (SSO) across cloud services.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the AES algorithm. Discuss its key sizes, block size, and the number of rounds for each variant. (7 marks)

**Model Solution (7 marks):**
**AES (Advanced Encryption Standard)** is a symmetric block cipher standardized by NIST in 2001 (FIPS 197), based on the Rijndael algorithm.

- **Block Size**: Fixed at **128 bits** (16 bytes), organized as a 4×4 state matrix.
- **Key Sizes**: **128, 192, or 256 bits**.
- **Rounds**: Number of transformation rounds depends on the key size:
  - AES-128 → **10 rounds**
  - AES-192 → **12 rounds**
  - AES-256 → **14 rounds**

**Round Transformations (in order):**
1. **SubBytes** — Non-linear byte substitution using a fixed S-box (16×16 lookup table). Provides confusion.
2. **ShiftRows** — Cyclically left-shifts the rows of the state by 0, 1, 2, 3 bytes respectively. Provides diffusion.
3. **MixColumns** — Each column of the state is multiplied by a fixed 4×4 polynomial matrix over $\mathrm{GF}(2^{8})$. Provides inter-column diffusion.
4. **AddRoundKey** — The state is XORed with the round key derived from the original key via the **Key Expansion** schedule.

**Operational Modes**: ECB (insecure), CBC (needs IV), CTR, GCM (provides authenticated encryption — AEAD), XTS.

**Strengths**: Brute-force on AES-256 is computationally infeasible ($\approx 2^{256}$ keys). Side-channel attacks (cache-timing) are mitigated by hardware AES-NI instructions on modern CPUs.

---

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the SSL/TLS handshake process. Why is TLS called a "hybrid" encryption protocol? (7 marks)

**Model Solution (7 marks):**
Refer to the **Sequence Diagram in Section 4.4** for the handshake flow.

**Step-by-step description of the TLS 1.2 / 1.3 handshake:**
1. **ClientHello** — Browser sends its TLS version, supported cipher suites, and a random nonce `client_random`.
2. **ServerHello + Certificate** — Server picks a cipher suite, sends its X.509 certificate (containing its public key) and `server_random`.
3. **Certificate Verification** — Client validates the cert chain up to a trusted Root CA.
4. **Key Exchange** — Client generates a `pre_master_secret`, encrypts it with the server's public key, and sends it.
5. **Session Key Derivation** — Both parties independently compute the symmetric `session_key` from `client_random`, `server_random`, and `pre_master_secret`.
6. **Finished Messages** — Both sides send a MAC of all prior handshake messages, encrypted with the session key, to confirm the handshake integrity.
7. **Application Data** — All subsequent HTTP payloads are encrypted with the session key using AES (typically AES-128-GCM or AES-256-GCM).

**Why "Hybrid"?**
TLS combines the strengths of both cryptographic families:
- **Asymmetric encryption** (RSA / ECC) — Used only for authentication and secure key exchange (solves the key distribution problem).
- **Symmetric encryption** (AES) — Used for the actual bulk data transfer (provides speed and efficiency).

This combination yields **the security of public-key cryptography** with **the performance of symmetric cryptography**, hence it is termed *hybrid*.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make — and where you LOSE marks:**
> 1. **Confusing hashing with encryption.** Hashing is **one-way** (irreversible); encryption is **two-way** (reversible with a key). Examiners deduct 1 mark if you write "SHA-256 is used to encrypt the password."
> 2. **Forgetting the GCD condition in RSA.** Always verify $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$. Skipping this step costs a mark in the modular arithmetic question.
> 3. **Mixing up keys in asymmetric crypto.** The *public* key encrypts; the *private* key decrypts. The *private* key signs; the *public* key verifies. Writing this backwards is a classic 1-mark loss.
> 4. **Writing "DES is secure."** DES is **broken** (56-bit key). Only 3DES (legacy) and **AES** are considered secure.
> 5. **Omitting the data state.** When asked about "cloud data security", always explicitly address Data at Rest, Data in Transit, AND Data in Use — students often forget the third.
> 6. **In RSA, ensure $M < n$**. If $M \ge n$, you must break it into blocks — failing to mention this is a 1-mark deduction.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **CIA Triad** = Confidentiality + Integrity + Availability. Modern addition: Authenticity, Non-Repudiation.
- **Three Data States**: at Rest (AES + ACL), in Transit (TLS), in Use (Confidential Computing).
- **Symmetric**: Single shared key, fast, used for bulk data → **AES (128/192/256-bit)**.
- **Asymmetric**: Key pair (public + private), slow, used for key exchange & signatures → **RSA, ECC**.
- **Hashing**: One-way, fixed output, detects tampering → **SHA-256** (avoid MD5/SHA-1).
- **Digital Signature = Hash + Asymmetric encryption with sender's PRIVATE key**.
- **Verification = Decrypt signature with sender's PUBLIC key and compare hashes**.
- **RSA Core Formulas**: $n = pq$, $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$, $ed \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$, $C = M^e \mod n$, $M = C^d \mod n$.
- **PKI = CA + RA + X.509 Certificates + CRL/OCSP**. Used for trust distribution in TLS, S/MIME, code signing.
- **TLS = Hybrid** (Asymmetric for handshake + Symmetric for bulk data).
- **Envelope Encryption** = Encrypt data with DEK; encrypt DEK with KMS Master Key (HSM-backed).
- **AES Block Size = 128 bits; Rounds = 10 / 12 / 14 for key sizes 128 / 192 / 256**.
- **DLP, IAM, RBAC, MFA, CloudTrail** are all operational controls that complement cryptography.
- **Non-Repudiation** is uniquely provided by digital signatures — the sender cannot deny having sent the message.
- **Euler's Theorem** ($M^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod n$) is the mathematical foundation that makes RSA work.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: OpenStack
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT634 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** OpenStack

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. OpenStack — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**OpenStack** is an open-source, massively scalable **cloud operating system** (also called a *Cloud Infrastructure as a Service — IaaS* platform) that orchestrates and provisions pools of compute, storage, and networking resources across a data center. It is managed and maintained by the **OpenInfra Foundation** (formerly the OpenStack Foundation) and is licensed under **Apache 2.0**. OpenStack exposes a **RESTful HTTP API** to consumers, allowing the on-demand creation, configuration, and destruction of **Virtual Machines (VMs)**, **volumes**, **networks**, and **identity services** — all driven by a modular, distributed control plane.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** OpenStack is officially classified under Module 4 of PECDT634 as a *production-grade IaaS orchestration framework*. Examiners frequently test the **modular service architecture** and **service interaction models** rather than raw API syntax.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cloud Power Grid"

Imagine the **electrical power grid** of a city:
- **Citizens** (tenants/users) simply flip a switch and receive electricity — they do not own the power plant.
- The **power grid** is a *giant, shared pool of resources* (generators, transformers, wires).
- A **central control room** (grid operator) monitors demand, allocates capacity, and bills users.

OpenStack is the **cloud equivalent** of this power grid:
- **Data center hardware** (servers, disks, switches) = the power plants and grid.
- **OpenStack services** (Nova, Neutron, Cinder, Keystone) = the control room operators.
- **Cloud users** = citizens who consume compute/storage on demand via a dashboard.
- **Horizon Dashboard / API** = the wall switch.

You don't buy a generator; you provision a VM. **The data center becomes programmable.**

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology

| Term | Standard Value / Unit |
| :--- | :--- |
| **API Endpoint** | **HTTP/HTTPS** over **port 8774 / 5000 / 9292** (per service) |
| **Release Cadence** | **6 months** (e.g., 2024.1 "Caracal", 2024.2 "Dalmatian") |
| **Message Broker** | **RabbitMQ** (AMQP 0-9-1) or **Kafka** |
| **Database** | **MySQL / MariaDB** / **PostgreSQL** (for control plane state) |
| **License** | **Apache 2.0** (open source, vendor-neutral) |
| **Hypervisor Support** | **KVM**, **QEMU**, **VMware vSphere**, **Xen**, **Hyper-V**, **LXC** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Hypervisor** is the software layer that creates and runs virtual machines. KVM (Kernel-based Virtual Machine) is the default and most widely deployed hypervisor in OpenStack.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Architecture Sheet

## 2.1 The Modular Philosophy

OpenStack is built on the **"service-oriented, pluggable architecture"** principle. Each capability is delivered by a **discrete service** (a *project* in OpenStack terminology) that exposes its own **API**, uses a **shared database**, and communicates asynchronously with peers via a **message queue**. This decoupling means a failure in one service does not cascade to the entire cloud.

> [!NOTE]
> **"Project"** in OpenStack has two meanings: (1) a *software component* (e.g., the Nova project), and (2) a *tenant isolation unit* (a group of users with shared resources). Examiners often frame questions using the **first meaning**.

## 2.2 The Core Services (The Big Six)

### 2.2.1 **Nova — Compute Service**
The "muscle" of OpenStack. Nova provisions and manages **virtual machines** and **bare-metal servers**. It is hypervisor-agnostic and supports **scheduling**, **scaling**, and **live migration**.

### 2.2.2 **Neutron — Networking Service**
The "nervous system." Neutron provides **Software-Defined Networking (SDN)** with pluggable backends: **OVS (Open vSwitch)**, **LinuxBridge**, **OVN**, **Calico**, **Cilium**. It handles **VLANs, VXLAN, GRE tunnels, subnets, routers, load balancers (LBaaS), and firewalls (FWaaS)**.

### 2.2.3 **Cinder — Block Storage Service**
Provides **persistent block devices** (volumes) attached to VMs. Backed by **LVM, Ceph, iSCSI, NFS, NetApp, Dell EMC** — pluggable drivers.

### 2.2.4 **Keystone — Identity Service**
The **authentication and authorization backbone**. Issues **tokens** and maintains the **Service Catalog**. Implements **RBAC (Role-Based Access Control)** and supports **LDAP, Active Directory, OAuth, SAML** for federated identity.

### 2.2.5 **Glance — Image Service**
Stores and retrieves **virtual disk images** (RAW, QCOW2, VMDK, VHD, ISO, AMI). Supports **metadata**, **image sharing**, and **multiple backing stores** (filesystem, Swift, Ceph, S3).

### 2.2.6 **Swift — Object Storage Service**
A **distributed, eventually-consistent object store** with **RESTful HTTP API**. Data is replicated across zones for fault tolerance. It is the S3-compatible equivalent of AWS S3.

## 2.3 The Supporting Services

| Service | Function | KTU Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Horizon** | Web-based GUI dashboard | Most demoed in lab exams |
| **Heat** | **Orchestration** (HOT templates) | Equivalent to AWS CloudFormation |
| **Ceilometer / Gnocchi / Panko** | Telemetry & Metering | Billing and capacity planning |
| **Ironic** | **Bare-metal provisioning** | PXE + IPMI for physical servers |
| **Trove** | Database as a Service (DBaaS) | MySQL, PostgreSQL, MongoDB |
| **Sahara** | Big Data (Hadoop/Spark) provisioning | Niche use case |
| **Magnum** | Container Orchestration (Kubernetes) | Bridges OpenStack and containers |
| **Designate** | DNS as a Service | Multi-tenant DNS zones |
| **Barbican** | Key Management (KMS) | Secrets, certificates, encryption keys |

## 2.4 The Logical Architecture Tiers

| Tier | Components | Role |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Presentation Tier** | Horizon Dashboard, CLI clients | User interaction |
| **Application / Logic Tier** | Nova, Neutron, Cinder, Glance, Swift, Keystone, Heat | Service processing |
| **Communication Tier** | RabbitMQ / Kafka (AMQP), ZeroMQ | Asynchronous message bus |
| **Data / State Tier** | MariaDB, MySQL, etcd | Persistent state of services |
| **Physical / Hardware Tier** | Compute nodes, storage arrays, switches | Raw resources |

## 2.5 Request Lifecycle — How a VM is Born

1. **User authenticates** against **Keystone** → receives a **scoped token**.
2. User sends `POST /servers` to **Nova API** with image ID, flavor ID, network ID.
3. **Nova API** publishes a message via **RabbitMQ** to the `nova-scheduler` and `nova-conductor`.
4. **Nova scheduler** runs filters and weighers (e.g., availability zone, CPU load) and selects a **target compute node**.
5. **Nova compute** worker on the chosen node invokes the **libvirt driver → KVM hypervisor** to spin up the VM.
6. **Glance** streams the image; **Neutron** plugs the VM into a virtual port; **Cinder** attaches a volume if requested.
7. VM transitions `BUILD → ACTIVE`. State changes are committed to the **MariaDB** database.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility

OpenStack is the **de facto on-premise IaaS standard** for **telecoms (Vodafone, AT&T)**, **banks (Wells Fargo, BBVA)**, **government clouds (GovStack, CERN)**, and **research (NASA Nebula, CERN)**. It is also the foundation of **Red Hat OpenStack Platform**, **Canonical Charmed OpenStack**, and **SUSE Cloud**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Operational Math

## 3.1 Resource Quota and Capacity Formulation

OpenStack enforces **quotas** at the project (tenant) level. Let:

$$
N_v = \text{Number of vCPUs allocated to Project } p
$$

$$
M_{\text{GB}} = \text{Total RAM in GB allocated to Project } p
$$

$$
V_{\text{GB}} = \text{Total block storage volume size in GB}
$$

Then, the **Headroom** for a new VM request of `n` vCPUs, `m` GB RAM, and `v` GB volume is given by:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{\text{CPU}}(p) &= Q_{\text{CPU}}(p) - N_v(p) \\[4pt]
H_{\text{RAM}}(p) &= Q_{\text{RAM}}(p) - M_{\text{GB}}(p) \\[4pt]
H_{\text{Vol}}(p) &= Q_{\text{Vol}}(p) - V_{\text{GB}}(p)
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $Q_{\text{CPU}}(p)$, $Q_{\text{RAM}}(p)$, $Q_{\text{Vol}}(p)$ are the **hard quota limits** configured for project $p$ in the Keystone + Nova policy.
- A request is admitted **iff** $H_{\text{CPU}} \geq n$, $H_{\text{RAM}} \geq m$, and $H_{\text{Vol}} \geq v$.

> **Logic:** Each inequality checks the remaining capacity against the requested allocation. Only when **all three** are satisfied is the request admitted. This is the *admission control principle* OpenStack enforces at the orchestration layer.

## 3.2 Nova Scheduling — Filter & Weigher Math

The scheduler chooses a host $h$ from the set of candidates $H$ by applying **filters** $F_i(h)$ and **weighers** $W_j(h)$. The selection criterion:

$$
h^* = \arg\max_{h \in H} \sum_{j=1}^{J} w_j \cdot W_j(h)
$$

Subject to:

$$
\forall i \in \{1, \dots, I\}: \; F_i(h) = \text{True}
$$

Where:
- $w_j$ = weighting coefficient of weigher $j$.
- $F_i$ = filter function (e.g., `RamFilter`, `ComputeFilter`, `AvailabilityZoneFilter`).
- $W_j(h)$ = normalized weigher score (e.g., free RAM, free disk, CPU load inverse).

> **Logic:** A candidate must first pass **every filter** (hard constraints such as "RAM available ≥ requested RAM"). Among survivors, the **weighted sum** of soft scores picks the best host.

## 3.3 Swift Object Storage — Replica Placement Derivation

Swift stores an object across **$N$ replicas** in **distinct zones**. Given a ring of $Z$ zones and a desired replication factor $r$, the **durability probability** for surviving a single-zone failure is:

$$
P_{\text{survive}} = 1 - \left( \frac{1}{Z} \right)^{r}
$$

For $Z = 3$ zones and $r = 3$ replicas:

$$
P_{\text{survive}} = 1 - \left( \frac{1}{3} \right)^{3} = 1 - \frac{1}{27} \approx 0.963
$$

> **Logic:** Swift hashes the object path to determine the placement. If all 3 replicas land in the **same zone**, the probability of that event is $\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^{3}$, because each replica has an independent $1/3$ chance of landing in any specific zone. **Subtracting from 1** gives the complement — the probability that the placement is correct.

For $Z = 5$ zones and $r = 3$ replicas:

$$
P_{\text{survive}} = 1 - \left( \frac{1}{5} \right)^{3} = 1 - \frac{1}{125} = 0.992
$$

> **Engineering insight:** Increasing zone count **drastically improves durability**. This is why production deployments use at least **3 zones** with **3 replicas** ($r = 3$).

## 3.4 Cinder Volume IOPS Provisioning Math

Cinder allows defining **Quality of Service (QoS)** via IOPS limits. For a backend with **max IOPS** $I_{\max}$ shared across $k$ volumes, the **fair-share per volume** is:

$$
I_{\text{vol}} = \frac{I_{\max}}{k}
$$

For a provisioned volume of size $S$ GB on a backend rated at $I_{\max} = 10{,}000$ IOPS, with $k = 4$ volumes:

$$
I_{\text{vol}} = \frac{10{,}000}{4} = 2{,}500 \; \text{IOPS}
$$

> **Logic:** A simple equal-share scheduler divides total backend capacity by active consumer count. Real systems use **token-bucket** or **weighted fair queueing** for more granular control, but the equal-share is the **baseline analytical model** KTU expects.

## 3.5 Network Overlay Encapsulation Overhead

Neutron with **VXLAN** overlay introduces a **header overhead** of **50 bytes** per packet (VXLAN = 8 bytes, UDP = 8 bytes, IP = 20 bytes, outer Ethernet = 14 bytes). Given a physical MTU of $M_{\text{phys}} = 1500$ bytes, the **effective inner MTU** is:

$$
M_{\text{inner}} = M_{\text{phys}} - 50 = 1450 \; \text{bytes}
$$

> **Logic:** To avoid fragmentation, the **guest VM MTU must be set to 1450** (or *jumbo frames of 9000* must be used on the physical network, yielding an inner MTU of $9000 - 50 = 8950$).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 OpenStack High-Level Service Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **core service interactions** in an OpenStack deployment, including the **message bus**, **shared database**, and **end-user entry points**.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ENTRY["User Entry Layer"]
        user1[Cloud User or Tenant]
        dash[Horizon Dashboard]
        cli[CLI Client OpenStack Client]
        sdr[SDK Software Development Kit]
    end

    subgraph IDENTITY["Identity Layer"]
        kst[Keystone Identity Service]
        tok[Token Issuer and Service Catalog]
    end

    subgraph CORE["Core Service Layer"]
        nva[Nova Compute Service]
        ntr[Neutron Networking Service]
        cdr[Cinder Block Storage]
        gln[Glance Image Service]
        swt[Swift Object Storage]
        htn[Heat Orchestration]
    end

    subgraph MESSG["Communication Layer"]
        mq[RabbitMQ AMQP Message Bus]
        db[(MariaDB MySQL Shared State)]
    end

    subgraph PHY["Physical Resource Layer"]
        kvm[KVM Hypervisor Compute Node]
        ovs[Open vSwitch OVS Network Node]
        sto[Ceph or LVM Storage Backend]
    end

    user1 --> dash
    user1 --> cli
    user1 --> sdr

    dash --> kst
    cli --> kst
    sdr --> kst

    kst --> tok

    tok --> nva
    tok --> ntr
    tok --> cdr
    tok --> gln
    tok --> swt
    tok --> htn

    nva <--> mq
    ntr <--> mq
    cdr <--> mq
    nva --> db
    ntr --> db
    cdr --> db
    gln --> db
    kst --> db

    nva --> kvm
    ntr --> ovs
    cdr --> sto
    gln --> sto
    htn --> nva
    htn --> ntr
    htn --> cdr
```

## 4.2 VM Provisioning — Sequence of Service Calls

The next block diagram walks through **how a VM is provisioned** end-to-end, emphasizing the **temporal sequence** of service interactions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[User sends POST servers to Nova API] --> B[Keystone validates token]
    B --> C[Token accepted, request enters Nova]
    C --> D[Nova publishes message to RabbitMQ]
    D --> E[Scheduler selects best compute host]
    E --> F[Nova Compute worker on chosen host]
    F --> G[Glance streams image to host]
    G --> H[Neutron attaches VM to virtual port]
    H --> I[Cinder attaches block volume if requested]
    I --> J[KVM boots the virtual machine]
    J --> K[State changes BUILD to ACTIVE in DB]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Flow of Object Storage (Swift)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    sub1[Client uploads object via PUT request] --> sub2[Proxy Server authenticates with Keystone]
    sub2 --> sub3[Ring module computes placement hash]
    sub3 --> sub4[Selects 3 distinct zones from Z zones]
    sub4 --> sub5[Replicates object to 3 storage nodes]
    sub5 --> sub6[Each node writes to disk and replicates locally]
    sub6 --> sub7[Returns 201 Created to client]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]
**List any six core services of OpenStack and write a single-line function of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Service | Function |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Nova** | Provisions and manages virtual machines (compute) |
| 2 | **Neutron** | Provides software-defined networking (SDN) |
| 3 | **Cinder** | Provides persistent block storage volumes |
| 4 | **Keystone** | Authentication, authorization, and service catalog |
| 5 | **Glance** | Stores and serves virtual disk images |
| 6 | **Swift** | Provides highly durable object storage |

*(1 mark for the list of 6, 1 mark for any 3 correct functions, 1 mark for the structured table format)*

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]
**Differentiate between OpenStack Cinder and OpenStack Swift with respect to access pattern, use case, and underlying data model.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | **Cinder (Block Storage)** | **Swift (Object Storage)** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Access Pattern** | Mounted as a raw block device (like a hard disk) inside a VM | Accessed via RESTful HTTP API (PUT / GET) |
| **Use Case** | Database storage, OS disks, high-IOPS workloads | Backups, archives, static media, large unstructured data |
| **Data Model** | Fixed-size blocks (sectors) addressed by LBA | Objects with metadata addressed by a unique key/URL |
| **Example Equivalent** | AWS EBS | AWS S3 |

*(1 mark per key difference, 1 mark for example analog)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **architecture of OpenStack** with a neat block diagram. List the roles of the **message bus (RabbitMQ)** and the **shared database (MariaDB)** in the architecture.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Describe the **step-by-step process of provisioning a virtual machine** in OpenStack, starting from user authentication up to the **ACTIVE** state. Mention the role of each service involved.

#### **Model Solution for Q.A.(a):**

**OpenStack Architecture:**

OpenStack follows a **modular, service-oriented architecture** with the following components:

1. **Presentation Layer:** Provides user interaction via the **Horizon dashboard**, **CLI clients**, or **SDKs**.
2. **Application/Logic Layer:** Contains the **core services** (Nova, Neutron, Cinder, Glance, Swift, Keystone, Heat) that implement the cloud logic.
3. **Communication Layer:** The **message bus (RabbitMQ)** and the **shared database (MariaDB)**.
4. **Physical/Hardware Layer:** The actual servers, switches, and storage arrays.

**Role of RabbitMQ (Message Bus):**
- Implements **asynchronous, decoupled communication** between OpenStack services.
- Uses the **AMQP 0-9-1 protocol** for message queuing.
- Decouples the **API endpoints** from the **worker processes** so the cloud can **scale horizontally** without losing requests.
- Example: When a `nova-api` request is received, it is **published to a queue** and consumed by a `nova-scheduler` worker asynchronously.

**Role of MariaDB (Shared Database):**
- Stores the **persistent state** of all OpenStack services (e.g., VM metadata, network definitions, volume records, user roles).
- Acts as the **single source of truth** for resource inventory.
- Ensures **consistency** across distributed service components.

*(3 marks architecture explanation, 2 marks for diagram, 2 marks for RabbitMQ/MariaDB roles)*

#### **Model Solution for Q.A.(b):**

**Step-by-Step VM Provisioning:**

1. **Authentication:** The user authenticates with **Keystone** using username/password, receives a **scoped token** and a **service catalog**.
2. **Request Initiation:** User sends a `POST /servers` request to **Nova API**, including image ID, flavor ID, and network ID.
3. **Token Validation:** Nova validates the token with **Keystone** before processing.
4. **Message Queuing:** Nova publishes the request to the **RabbitMQ queue**, which is picked up by the **`nova-scheduler`**.
5. **Scheduling:** The scheduler applies **filters** (e.g., `RamFilter`, `ComputeFilter`) and **weighers** to select the best **compute host**.
6. **Image Fetch:** The chosen **compute node** calls **Glance** to download the image.
7. **Network Setup:** **Neutron** provisions a **virtual port** on the appropriate network and creates the **virtual NIC** for the VM.
8. **Volume Attachment:** If a volume is requested, **Cinder** attaches the **block device** to the VM.
9. **Hypervisor Boot:** The **`nova-compute`** worker invokes the **libvirt driver → KVM hypervisor** to instantiate the VM.
10. **State Update:** The VM state transitions **`BUILD → ACTIVE`**, and the change is committed to **MariaDB**.

*(7 sub-steps × 1 mark each)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]

**(a)** [7 Marks] Compare and contrast **OpenStack Nova** and **OpenStack Heat** with respect to their purpose, use of templates, level of automation, and use cases.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Explain **object storage in OpenStack Swift**, including the concept of **rings**, **zones**, and **replicas**. How is data durability achieved?

#### **Model Solution for Q.B.(a):**

| Parameter | **Nova (Compute)** | **Heat (Orchestration)** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Purpose** | Provisions individual VMs | Provisions and manages stacks of resources (multi-VM, multi-tier apps) |
| **Templates** | Does not use templates; uses direct API calls | Uses **HOT (Heat Orchestration Template)** YAML files |
| **Level of Automation** | Single VM lifecycle | Full **infrastructure-as-code** automation (network, compute, storage) |
| **Dependency Management** | None | Manages **resource creation order and dependencies** |
| **Use Case** | Spin up a single VM | Deploy a 3-tier web app (LB + Web + DB) in one call |
| **AWS Equivalent** | AWS EC2 | AWS CloudFormation |

*(3 marks table, 2 marks HOT example, 2 marks dependency management)*

#### **Model Solution for Q.B.(b):**

**Object Storage in Swift:**

Swift is a **distributed, eventually-consistent object store** designed to store **petabytes of unstructured data** with high durability.

**Core Concepts:**

- **Object:** A file (image, video, backup) plus its **metadata**. Addressed by a unique URL.
- **Container:** A logical grouping of objects, similar to an S3 bucket.
- **Ring:** A **consistent hash map** that maps object names to physical storage devices. There are separate rings for **Account, Container, and Object** services.
- **Zone:** A logical boundary (typically a **rack, data center, or power domain**) used to ensure replicas are placed in **failure-independent** locations.
- **Replica:** A copy of an object stored on a different device/zone for fault tolerance.

**Data Durability Workflow:**

1. Client sends a `PUT /container/object` request to the **Proxy Server**.
2. The Proxy Server **hashes** the object name and consults the **ring** to determine the placement.
3. The ring returns the **primary and replica positions** across distinct zones.
4. The object is written to **3 (or N) replicas** in **3 distinct zones**.
5. Each zone writes to its local storage; the data is **eventually consistent** across zones.
6. A `201 Created` response is returned.

**Durability Math:**

For $Z$ zones and $r$ replicas, probability of correct placement:

$$
P_{\text{correct}} = 1 - \left( \frac{1}{Z} \right)^{r}
$$

For $Z = 3$ and $r = 3$, $P_{\text{correct}} = 1 - (1/27) \approx 96.3\%$. Production deployments scale $Z$ to **5 or more** to achieve **> 99.9% durability**.

*(3 marks rings, 2 marks zones, 2 marks durability calculation)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT** confuse **OpenStack "Project"** (a tenant) with **OpenStack "Project"** (a software component). Use the term **"service"** to avoid ambiguity.
> - When asked about **VM provisioning**, examiners expect the **message queue and scheduler steps** — skipping RabbitMQ costs **2 marks** consistently.
> - For **Swift durability**, do **not** claim it is **strongly consistent** — it is **eventually consistent**. This is a **favourite trick question**.
> - **Heat ≠ Nova.** Students who write Heat as a "compute service" lose **3 marks** instantly.
> - Always cite **Apache 2.0** license and **OpenInfra Foundation** when asked about ownership/governance.

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- **OpenStack** is an **open-source IaaS platform** licensed under **Apache 2.0**, governed by the **OpenInfra Foundation**.
- It is **modular** — each capability is a separate **service** with its own **REST API**.
- **Keystone** = Identity. **Nova** = Compute. **Neutron** = Network. **Cinder** = Block Storage. **Glance** = Images. **Swift** = Object Storage. **Horizon** = Dashboard. **Heat** = Orchestration.
- **RabbitMQ** = message bus; **MariaDB** = shared state.
- A **token** from **Keystone** is required for every API call — **RBAC** governs permissions.
- **Nova scheduler** uses **filters** (hard) and **weighers** (soft) to select hosts.
- **Swift** uses a **ring** (consistent hash) to map objects to devices across **zones**, storing **3 replicas by default**.
- **Heat** uses **HOT templates** (YAML) — equivalent to AWS **CloudFormation**.
- **Neutron** supports **VXLAN** (50-byte overhead) and **VLAN** overlays; **MTU** must be tuned.
- **Cinder** supports **QoS** (IOPS limits) and pluggable backends (**LVM, Ceph, iSCSI**).
- Releases every **6 months**; current series follows the alphabet (e.g., 2024.1 **Caracal**, 2024.2 **Dalmatian**).
- **Real-world adopters:** CERN, NASA, BBVA, Wells Fargo, Red Hat, Canonical Charmed OpenStack, SUSE Cloud.
- **Common exam keywords to memorize:** *modular, pluggable, RBAC, HOT, ring, zone, replica, scheduler, filter, weigher, hypervisor, overlay, MTU, IOPS, QoS, API endpoint, token, service catalog.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heroku
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT634 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Heroku

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Heroku** is a fully managed, container-based **Platform as a Service (PaaS)** cloud computing platform that enables developers to build, deploy, run, and scale web applications and APIs entirely in the cloud, without worrying about the underlying infrastructure (servers, storage, networking, OS patching, load balancing, etc.).

Heroku was originally developed by **Heroku Inc.** in **2007** and was acquired by **Salesforce.com** in **2010** for approximately **\$212 million**. It operates over the **AWS (Amazon Web Services)** infrastructure as its physical substrate but provides a high-level abstraction layer above it, primarily supporting languages and frameworks such as **Ruby (Rails), Node.js, Python (Django/Flask), Java (Spring), PHP, Go, Scala, and Clojure**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping:** Heroku is studied under Module 4 of PECDT634 as a representative commercial PaaS implementation, contrasting with IaaS (like AWS EC2) and SaaS models. It is essential for understanding application lifecycle management on the cloud.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you want to cook a gourmet dinner for 100 guests. You have two choices:

- **Without Heroku (IaaS approach):** You must first buy land, build a kitchen, install gas pipelines, set up electricity, purchase ovens, hire chefs, manage inventory, and clean afterwards. You manage *everything*.
- **With Heroku (PaaS approach):** You simply walk into a fully equipped rented commercial kitchen where the gas, electricity, utensils, and cleaning staff are already provided. You only need to bring your **recipe (code)** and your **ingredients (application data)**, press "Start Cooking" (`git push heroku main`), and serve the guests.

Heroku is that ready-made commercial kitchen for software applications. Developers bring their **source code** (via Git), and Heroku automatically handles the rest: **provisioning servers, compiling code, exposing URLs, scaling load, and managing databases.**

### 1.3 Core Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Dyno** | A lightweight, isolated, virtualized Linux container that runs a single user-defined command |
| **Buildpack** | A set of scripts that automatically detect and compile the application for deployment |
| **Slug** | A compressed, pre-compiled copy of the application code ready for execution on dynos |
| **Add-on** | A managed third-party cloud service (DBs, monitoring, caching) attachable via marketplace |
| **Procfile** | A text file declaring the command(s) executed on app startup |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Architecture — The "How" and "Why"

Heroku's runtime architecture is structured around **ephemeral, isolated virtual containers (dynos)** orchestrated on top of a shared, abstracted compute layer. The flow operates as follows:

1. **Code Push (Trigger)**
   - Developer pushes source code using `git push heroku main`.
   - *Why:* Heroku integrates Git natively as the deployment interface, removing the need for complex CI/CD pipelines for simple projects.

2. **Buildpack Execution (Compilation Phase)**
   - Heroku detects the language (Ruby, Node.js, Python, etc.) by scanning for marker files like `Gemfile`, `package.json`, `requirements.txt`.
   - The **buildpack** script downloads dependencies and compiles the source tree.
   - *Why:* This abstracts the toolchain (compiler versions, libraries) from the developer.

3. **Slug Generation (Artifact Packaging)**
   - The compiled output is compressed into a **slug** — a binary tarball containing everything needed to run the app.

4. **Dyno Allocation (Execution Phase)**
   - Heroku's orchestration layer (called **Dyno Manager** / formerly **Vulcan**) selects a runtime environment and starts one or more **dynos** to load the slug.

5. **HTTP Routing (Access Phase)**
   - The **Heroku HTTP router** distributes incoming requests across healthy dynos based on a round-robin load-balancing algorithm.
   - *Why:* Ensures high availability and fault tolerance.

6. **Add-on Provisioning (Service Binding Phase)**
   - Any attached add-ons (PostgreSQL, Redis, etc.) expose their credentials as **environment variables** automatically injected into the dyno's runtime environment.

### 2.2 Dyno Types and Tiers

| Dyno Type | Tier | Use Case | Sleep Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Free / Eco** | Hobby | Prototyping, learning | Sleeps after 30 min idle (Eco), or never sleeps (Free discontinued) |
| **Basic** | Production-lite | Small applications | Always on |
| **Standard** | Production | Most production apps | Always on, scales horizontally |
| **Performance** | Mission-critical | High-traffic, low-latency apps | Always on, dedicated CPU |
| **Private Spaces / Shield** | Enterprise | HIPAA/SOX compliance | Isolated network |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Dyno-hour billing** | $\text{Cost} = N_{\text{dynos}} \times T_{\text{uptime}} \times P_{\text{per-hr}}$ | $T$ measured in hours |
| **Horizontal Scaling** | $N_{\text{dynos}}^{\text{new}} = N_{\text{dynos}}^{\text{old}} \times k,\ \ k \in \mathbb{Z}^{+}$ | $k$ = scaling factor |
| **Concurrent Connections per Dyno** | $C_{\text{max}} = R_{\text{rps}} \times \text{Latency}$ | Little's Law application |
| **Throughput (RPS)** | $R_{\text{rps}} = \dfrac{N_{\text{dynos}} \times C_{\text{per-dyno}}}{\text{Latency}_{\text{avg}}}$ | Requests per second |
| **Heroku HTTP Router Distribution** | $P_{\text{dyno}_i} = \dfrac{1}{N_{\text{healthy}}}$ | Uniform round-robin |
| **Slug Size Limit** | $S_{\text{slug}} \leq 500 \text{ MB}$ | Compressed artifact cap |
| **App Name Length** | $L_{\text{name}} \in [3, 30]$ characters | Must be unique globally |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** Heroku abstracts IaaS-level concerns. Students must clearly distinguish between **Dynos (compute)**, **Add-ons (managed services)**, and **Buildpacks (language toolchains)**.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

Heroku is widely adopted for:
- **MVP development** by startups (rapid time-to-market).
- **API backends** for mobile and SPA (Single Page Applications).
- **Webhook receivers** and event-driven microservices.
- **Enterprise departmental apps** where IT-managed IaaS overhead is impractical.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Heroku Horizontal Auto-Scaling Cost Model

For a typical KTU numerical/problem question involving Heroku's dyno-hour billing, we derive the **monthly cost projection** as follows.

**Given:**
- Application requires a minimum baseline of $N_{\min}$ dynos.
- During peak traffic (duration $T_{\text{peak}}$ hours/day), it scales to $N_{\text{peak}}$ dynos.
- Off-peak duration is $T_{\text{off}} = 24 - T_{\text{peak}}$ hours/day.
- Cost per dyno-hour = $P$ (currency units).

**Step 1 — Compute the peak-window dyno-hours per day:**

$$
H_{\text{peak}} = N_{\text{peak}} \times T_{\text{peak}}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the off-peak dyno-hours per day:**

$$
H_{\text{off}} = N_{\min} \times T_{\text{off}}
$$

**Step 3 — Total dyno-hours per day:**

$$
H_{\text{day}} = H_{\text{peak}} + H_{\text{off}} = N_{\text{peak}} \cdot T_{\text{peak}} + N_{\min} \cdot (24 - T_{\text{peak}})
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by days in the billing month (typically 30 or 31):**

$$
H_{\text{month}} = H_{\text{day}} \times D_{\text{days}}
$$

**Step 5 — Total monetary cost:**

$$
C_{\text{total}} = H_{\text{month}} \times P
$$

Substituting Step 4 into Step 5:

$$
\boxed{C_{\text{total}} = P \cdot D_{\text{days}} \cdot \left[ N_{\text{peak}} \cdot T_{\text{peak}} + N_{\min} \cdot (24 - T_{\text{peak}}) \right]}
$$

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

> **Question:** A Heroku Standard-1 app runs with a baseline of **2 dynos** at all times. During an **8-hour** daily peak window, it auto-scales to **6 dynos**. The Standard-1 dyno costs **\$0.035 per dyno-hour**. Calculate the **monthly cost** assuming a **30-day month**.

**Step 1 — Peak dyno-hours per day:**

$$
H_{\text{peak}} = 6 \times 8 = 48 \text{ dyno-hours/day}
$$

**Step 2 — Off-peak dyno-hours per day:**

$$
H_{\text{off}} = 2 \times (24 - 8) = 2 \times 16 = 32 \text{ dyno-hours/day}
$$

**Step 3 — Total per day:**

$$
H_{\text{day}} = 48 + 32 = 80 \text{ dyno-hours/day}
$$

**Step 4 — Monthly total:**

$$
H_{\text{month}} = 80 \times 30 = 2400 \text{ dyno-hours/month}
$$

**Step 5 — Monthly cost:**

$$
C_{\text{total}} = 2400 \times 0.035 = \$84.00 \text{ per month}
$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Heroku vs. AWS EC2

| Parameter | Heroku (PaaS) | AWS EC2 (IaaS) |
|---|---|---|
| **Management Layer** | Application | Virtual Machine |
| **Provisioning Time** | Seconds (git push) | Minutes to hours |
| **OS Control** | None (Ubuntu base only) | Full (any AMI) |
| **Pricing Granularity** | Dyno-hour | Instance-hour + data transfer |
| **Scaling** | Horizontal auto-scaling via dashboard/CLI | Manual or via ASG + Launch Templates |
| **Skill Required** | Low (developer-focused) | High (DevOps/SysAdmin) |
| **Vendor Lock-in** | Moderate (uses proprietary CLI) | Low (open APIs) |
| **Best For** | Rapid prototyping, small-to-medium apps | Custom enterprise workloads |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Heroku Platform Architecture Flow

The following **Mermaid block diagram** illustrates the complete request lifecycle from developer push to end-user response.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Developer Workstation] -->|git push heroku main| B[Heroku Git Remote]
    B --> C{Buildpack Detection}
    C -->|Ruby| D1[Ruby Buildpack]
    C -->|Node.js| D2[Node.js Buildpack]
    C -->|Python| D3[Python Buildpack]
    C -->|Java| D4[Java Buildpack]
    D1 --> E[Slug Compilation]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> F[Slug Artifact Tarball]
    F --> G[Dyno Manager Orchestrator]
    G --> H1[Dyno 1]
    G --> H2[Dyno 2]
    G --> H3[Dyno N]
    H1 --> I[Heroku HTTP Router]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    I -->|Round Robin Load Balancing| J[End User Browser or Client App]
    K[Heroku Add-on Marketplace] -->|Provision| L1[PostgreSQL Service]
    K -->|Provision| L2[Redis Cache]
    K -->|Provision| L3[New Relic Monitoring]
    L1 -->|ENV Variables| H1
    L2 -->|ENV Variables| H2
    L3 -->|ENV Variables| H3
```

### 4.2 Application Lifecycle State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Created
    Created --> Deployed: git push successful
    Deployed --> Crashed: Runtime error in Procfile
    Crashed --> Restarted: Automatic by dyno manager
    Restarted --> Running: Crash threshold not exceeded
    Running --> ScaledHorizontally: heroku ps:scale web=5
    ScaledHorizontally --> Running
    Running --> Sleeping: Idle timeout Eco dynos
    Sleeping --> Running: New HTTP request received
    Running --> Terminated: heroku apps:destroy
    Terminated --> [*]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture

| Architectural Tier | Component | Responsibility |
|---|---|---|
| **Edge Tier** | Heroku HTTP Router | TLS termination, request distribution |
| **Application Tier** | Dyno Grid | Running application processes |
| **Build Tier** | Buildpack Engine | Source-to-slug compilation |
| **Data Tier** | Add-on Marketplace Services | Managed databases, caches, queues |
| **Control Tier** | Heroku CLI & Dashboard | Developer interaction surface |
| **Substrate Tier** | AWS EC2 Underlying VMs | Physical/virtual hardware foundation |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define Heroku. List any four supported programming languages on the Heroku platform.**

**Model Answer:**

**Heroku** is a fully managed **Platform as a Service (PaaS)** cloud platform acquired by Salesforce in 2010, running atop AWS infrastructure, that allows developers to deploy, run, and scale applications using `git push`.

Four supported languages:

1. **Ruby** (via Rails framework)
2. **Node.js** (JavaScript runtime)
3. **Python** (Django, Flask)
4. **Java** (Spring Boot)

*(Other valid answers: PHP, Go, Scala, Clojure.)*

**Marks Distribution:** Definition — 1 mark, Languages — 2 marks (0.5 each).

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]**
**What is a Dyno in Heroku? Differentiate between Web Dyno and Worker Dyno.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Dyno** is a lightweight, isolated virtual container (Linux-based) that runs the commands specified in the application's `Procfile`.

| Feature | Web Dyno | Worker Dyno |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Handles HTTP requests | Runs background jobs/queues |
| **External Access** | Receives traffic via HTTP router | No external HTTP exposure |
| **Example Use** | `web: bundle exec rails server` | `worker: bundle exec rake jobs:work` |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** Explain the Heroku platform architecture in detail, with a neat block diagram covering the **HTTP Router, Dyno Grid, Buildpack, Slug, and Add-on** components. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **deployment lifecycle of a Heroku application** starting from `git push` to production availability. What is the role of the `Procfile` and `slug` in this flow? **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution — (a) Architecture Explanation:**

The Heroku architecture is a layered PaaS stack built atop AWS IaaS primitives. Its core components are:

1. **HTTP Router (Edge Layer):** Acts as a reverse proxy and load balancer. It receives all incoming HTTP/HTTPS requests on the app's `*.herokuapp.com` URL and distributes them in a round-robin manner across healthy web dynos. It also performs TLS termination.

2. **Dyno Grid (Application Layer):** A pool of lightweight Linux containers called dynos. Each dyno runs an isolated instance of the application's slug. Dynos are ephemeral — they can be restarted, recycled, or relocated without state loss (state must be externalized to add-ons).

3. **Buildpack Engine (Build Layer):** A set of detection, compilation, and release scripts. When code is pushed, Heroku scans for marker files (e.g., `Gemfile` for Ruby) and runs the corresponding buildpack to fetch dependencies and compile.

4. **Slug (Artifact):** The output of the build process — a compressed tarball containing the compiled source, dependencies, and runtime. Slugs are immutable and cacheable, enabling fast dyno startup.

5. **Add-ons (Service Layer):** Marketplace-provisioned managed services (PostgreSQL, Redis, Papertrail, SendGrid) injected into dynos via environment variables like `DATABASE_URL`.

*(Block diagram reference: Section 4.1 of these notes.)*

---

##### **Model Solution — (b) Deployment Lifecycle:**

**Step 1:** Developer initializes a Heroku remote: `heroku create myapp`. This creates an empty Heroku app and a Git remote named `heroku`.

**Step 2:** Developer executes `git push heroku main`. Git sends the source tree over HTTPS.

**Step 3:** Heroku receives the push, detects the language via **buildpack markers**, and compiles dependencies.

**Step 4:** A **slug** is generated (compressed artifact).

**Step 5:** Heroku allocates **dynos** and loads the slug into them.

**Step 6:** The `Procfile` is read. It declares process types, e.g.:

```
web: gunicorn app:app
worker: python worker.py
```

**Step 7:** Dynos execute their respective `Procfile` commands.

**Step 8:** The HTTP router begins routing live traffic to `web` dynos.

**Role of Procfile:** Acts as the **entry-point declaration** — without it, Heroku does not know how to launch the app.

**Role of Slug:** Acts as the **immutable, portable runtime artifact** — ensures every dyno starts from identical state, enabling reproducibility and fast scaling.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 / Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** Compare and contrast **Heroku (PaaS)** with **AWS EC2 (IaaS)** across six parameters: management overhead, scaling, pricing model, developer skill required, vendor lock-in, and time-to-deploy. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Heroku application runs on a **baseline of 3 dynos** for **24 hours/day**. During a **6-hour** peak window each day, it scales to **8 dynos**. The dyno cost is **\$0.05 per dyno-hour**. Calculate the **monthly cost** for a **30-day month**. Also explain **horizontal vs vertical scaling** in the context of Heroku. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution — (a) Comparison:**

| Parameter | Heroku (PaaS) | AWS EC2 (IaaS) |
|---|---|---|
| **Management Overhead** | Minimal; Heroku manages servers, OS, runtime | High; user manages OS patching, scaling, security |
| **Scaling** | One-command horizontal scaling | Requires Auto Scaling Groups and Launch Templates |
| **Pricing Model** | Dyno-hour billing (simpler) | Instance-hour + data transfer + storage (granular) |
| **Developer Skill** | Low; designed for app developers | High; requires DevOps/SysAdmin expertise |
| **Vendor Lock-in** | Moderate (Heroku CLI, proprietary add-ons) | Low (standard APIs, open formats) |
| **Time-to-Deploy** | Minutes (git push) | Hours (provision, configure, install) |

---

##### **Model Solution — (b) Numerical + Concept:**

**Numerical Computation:**

Using the formula derived in Section 3.1:

$$
C_{\text{total}} = P \cdot D \cdot \left[N_{\text{peak}} \cdot T_{\text{peak}} + N_{\min} \cdot (24 - T_{\text{peak}})\right]
$$

**Step 1 — Peak dyno-hours/day:**

$$
H_{\text{peak}} = 8 \times 6 = 48
$$

**Step 2 — Off-peak dyno-hours/day:**

$$
H_{\text{off}} = 3 \times (24 - 6) = 3 \times 18 = 54
$$

**Step 3 — Total per day:**

$$
H_{\text{day}} = 48 + 54 = 102 \text{ dyno-hours/day}
$$

**Step 4 — Monthly dyno-hours:**

$$
H_{\text{month}} = 102 \times 30 = 3060 \text{ dyno-hours}
$$

**Step 5 — Total cost:**

$$
C_{\text{total}} = 3060 \times 0.05 = \$153.00 \text{ per month}
$$

**Conceptual Explanation:**

- **Horizontal Scaling** in Heroku means **adding more dynos** (e.g., `heroku ps:scale web=10`). It increases **concurrency** and **throughput**. Heroku natively supports this via the dashboard or CLI.
- **Vertical Scaling** would mean **increasing dyno size/ram/CPU** (e.g., moving from Standard-1 to Standard-2, or Performance-M). Heroku supports this by changing the dyno type, but the maximum dyno size is capped.
- Heroku **does not support live vertical resizing** — you must change the dyno type and redeploy, making **horizontal scaling the preferred elasticity strategy.**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing PaaS with IaaS:** Writing "Heroku manages the application code" — WRONG. Heroku manages the *runtime environment*, not your code logic.
> 2. **Forgetting the `Procfile`:** Examiners frequently deduct 1–2 marks if students don't mention its role in declaring process types.
> 3. **Mixing up Dynos and Slugs:** A **dyno** is a *running container*; a **slug** is the *compiled artifact*. They are NOT the same.
> 4. **Numerical errors in cost calculations:** Forgetting to convert peak duration to off-peak duration ($24 - T_{\text{peak}}$) is the most common arithmetic mistake.
> 5. **Missing environment variables:** Not mentioning that add-on credentials are injected via `ENV` variables loses marks in architecture questions.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🔹 **Heroku = PaaS** built atop AWS; owned by Salesforce (since 2010).
- 🔹 **Dyno = virtual Linux container** that runs a single command from the `Procfile`.
- 🔹 **`Procfile`** declares process types: `web:`, `worker:`, etc.
- 🔹 **Slug** = compiled, compressed, immutable application artifact.
- 🔹 **Buildpacks** detect language and compile dependencies automatically.
- 🔹 **HTTP Router** does round-robin load balancing across web dynos.
- 🔹 **Add-ons** are third-party managed services (DB, cache, monitoring) injected via env vars.
- 🔹 **Scaling is horizontal** (add more dynos); vertical scaling is limited and requires redeploy.
- 🔹 **Billing is per dyno-hour**, not per request.
- 🔹 **Free/Eco dynos sleep after 30 min idle** — never use for production.
- 🔹 **Heroku ≠ IaaS** — no access to OS, kernel, or underlying VM.
- 🔹 **Cost formula:** $C = P \cdot D \cdot \left[N_{\text{peak}} T_{\text{peak}} + N_{\min}(24 - T_{\text{peak}})\right]$
- 🔹 **App name must be globally unique** and 3–30 characters long.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Docker Containers and Amazon EC2
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT634 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Docker Containers and Amazon EC2

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Docker Containers — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Docker** is an open-source **containerization platform** that enables developers to package applications and their dependencies into lightweight, portable, self-sufficient units called **containers**, which can run consistently across any environment supporting Docker's runtime engine.

According to the KTU 2024 Cloud Computing syllabus, a **container** is a standard unit of software that packages up code and all its dependencies (libraries, system tools, runtime, configuration) so the application runs quickly and reliably from one computing environment to another. Docker utilizes **OS-level virtualization** through kernel features like `cgroups` (control groups) and **namespaces** on Linux.

### Key Technical Terms
- **Image** — A read-only, immutable template/instructional blueprint used to create containers. Built from a `Dockerfile`.
- **Container** — A runnable instance of an image. It is a lightweight, isolated executable package.
- **Dockerfile** — A text file containing step-by-step instructions to assemble a Docker image.
- **Registry** — A repository for storing and distributing Docker images (e.g., **Docker Hub**).
- **Daemon (`dockerd`)** — The background service that manages Docker objects (images, containers, networks, volumes).
- **Client (`docker`)** — The CLI used to interact with the Docker daemon via REST API.
- **Docker Compose** — A tool for defining and running **multi-container** applications using a `docker-compose.yml` file.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Shipping Container" Concept

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of Docker like the global shipping container system.**

Before standardized shipping containers existed, goods had to be **loaded, unloaded, and re-packed manually** for every ship, train, and truck — a process that was slow, error-prone, and incompatible across transport modes.

**Malcom McLean's standardized shipping container (1956)** solved this by defining a uniform box that could be moved by any transport. The **contents inside** (TVs, bananas, cars) didn't matter — the **container was universal**.

| Real-World Shipping | Docker Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Standard Steel Container | Docker Container |
| Contents (TVs, Food) | Application + Dependencies |
| Container Blueprint (ISO 6346) | `Dockerfile` |
| Port / Warehouse | Docker Registry (Docker Hub) |
| Crane Operator | Docker Daemon (`dockerd`) |
| Shipper / Customer | Developer using `docker` CLI |
| Different Ships/Trains | Different OS / Cloud Providers |

A **Docker container is a "shipping container for software"** — the application inside doesn't care whether it is running on a developer's laptop, a bare-metal server, AWS, Azure, or Google Cloud. The container behaves identically everywhere.

---

## 1.3 Amazon EC2 — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Amazon Elastic Compute Cloud (Amazon EC2)** is a web service provided by AWS that delivers **secure, resizable compute capacity in the cloud**. It is designed to make web-scale cloud computing easier by providing developers and system administrators with on-demand, pay-as-you-go access to virtual servers (called **instances**) in the cloud.

EC2 is the **Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS)** flagship of AWS, offering:
- **Elastic** compute — scale up or down within minutes.
- **Complete control** — root access, ability to run any compatible software.
- **Flexible pricing** — On-Demand, Reserved, Spot, Savings Plans, Dedicated Hosts.
- **Deep integration** with other AWS services (S3, RDS, VPC, IAM, ELB).

### Key EC2 Terminology
- **AMI (Amazon Machine Image)** — Pre-configured template (OS + software) used to launch an instance.
- **Instance Type** — Defines the hardware (CPU, RAM, Storage, Network) of the host computer. Examples: `t3.micro`, `m5.large`, `c5.xlarge`, `r5.4xlarge`.
- **Key Pair** — A set of security credentials (public-key cryptography) used to securely SSH into an instance.
- **Security Group** — A **virtual firewall** controlling inbound and outbound traffic at the **instance level**.
- **EBS (Elastic Block Store)** — Persistent block-level storage volumes for use with EC2.
- **Elastic IP** — A static, public IPv4 address designed for dynamic cloud computing.
- **User Data** — A script run at instance launch for automated bootstrapping.

---

## 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — EC2 as "Renting a Computer"

> [!NOTE]
> **EC2 is like renting a computer from a massive, infinitely-stocked computer warehouse.**

Imagine you need a powerful computer for your machine-learning workload for 3 hours. Buying one would cost ₹1,50,000 and take weeks to arrive. Instead, you call **AWS**:
- They provision a computer matching your exact spec (CPU, RAM, storage).
- It is ready in **under 60 seconds**.
- You pay only for the **3 hours you used**.
- When done, you **return it** and stop paying.

**EC2 = Renting Virtual Computers On-Demand.** Just like Uber revolutionized transport (rent a car only when needed), EC2 revolutionized infrastructure (rent compute only when needed). The "Elastic" in EC2 means you can **scale up during Diwali sales** and **scale down on a quiet Tuesday morning** — paying only for what you consume.

---

## 1.5 Why These Technologies Matter in Cloud Computing

| Dimension | Traditional Deployment | Virtual Machines | Docker Containers | EC2 in Cloud |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Startup Time | Days (hardware procurement) | Minutes (VM boot) | **Seconds (container start)** | **Minutes (instance provision)** |
| Resource Overhead | None (bare metal) | High (full OS per VM) | **Low (shared kernel)** | Configurable (instance family) |
| Portability | Low (hardware-locked) | Medium (hypervisor-locked) | **High (Docker-anywhere)** | High (AMI-based) |
| Density | 1 app/server | ~10-20 VMs/host | **100s of containers/host** | 1000s per region |
| Cost Model | CapEx (upfront) | CapEx + OpEx | OpEx | **Pure OpEx (pay-per-use)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 emphasizes the practical deployment of cloud-native applications. Docker solves the **"works on my machine"** problem, and EC2 provides the **elastic infrastructure** on which these containerized workloads can be deployed and scaled.
